question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her remarks in the Assembly on 17 November 2025 regarding AQO 2709/22-27, for her assessment of the abolition of air passenger duty.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQO 2709/22-27 in which I described air passenger duty as an unfair tax which continues to have a significant detrimental impact on domestic aviation here. Short haul APD remains a reserved matter which needs to be addressed by the British Government.  		Furthermore, my Department will consider the impact of reducing air passenger duty as part of our aviation policy development.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they agreed the Anti-Poverty Strategy on 15 May 2025.
answer_text: At its meeting on 15 May 2025, the Executive Committee agreed that the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy could be published and proceed to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is considering the resurfacing of Ardmore Park South, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.Whilst there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Belfast City Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department has spent in the 2022-27 mandate, on the improvement of the appearance of Finaghy Bridge, Belfast.
answer_text: I am aware that the appearance of Finaghy Railway Bridge is of significant interest to the local community. During the mandate period to date, my Department has spent approximately £5,500 to address areas of offensive graffiti on the bridge and in August 2025 Belfast City Council funded the painting of the floral mural on the bridge parapets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount her Department has spent, in each of the last five years, in compensation for wrongful arrest.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to create parity with the level of support given to people with lifelong needs complex needs in Northern Ireland with the support provided in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is distinctive from the rest of the UK in terms of its integrated Health and Social Care model which plans and commissions services on the basis of assessed health and social care need. My department is committed to ensuring that those with lifelong complex needs in Northern Ireland, can access the most consistent support possible. To strengthen Northern Irelands service provision for individuals with lifelong complex needs, my department has just finished a consultation on a new draft Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM), which sets out a regionally consistent approach across transitions, health and wellbeing, day opportunities, support for families and carers, and independent living. A three-year Service Delivery Plan (SDP), developed in collaboration with key partners across the sector, commits to expanding community supports, improving access to meaningful activity and strengthening governance and practice across all Trusts.The draft LDSM sets out clear ambitions for people with complex needs, to receive lifelong, co-ordinated support, tailored to their needs and enabling choice and control. It identifies challenges that currently limit consistency across Northern Ireland, including shortages in the workforce, pressures on day services and short breaks and variation in access to community-based support. The reform actions within the LDSM and SDP are intended to address these issues and improve outcomes for people with lifelong and complex needs. As part of developing the Model, my department undertook a comparative analysis of policy and frameworks across England, Scotland and Wales. This work concluded that, if fully implemented, the draft LDSM would meet or exceed the frameworks published in other jurisdictions. The LDSM presents an overarching system of services and interventions with the service user/family in the centre. It has the potential to go further than other models by addressing the interfaces with eg housing, education, employment, which other governments are seeking to address via strategic plans/policy interventions.As part of implementing the SDP, my department will conduct a review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 and all appropriate legislation, to determine whether there is a need for legislative change, which better reflects today's needs and modern models of support for people with lifelong complex needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to help alleviate parking issues in the Lower Malone area, Belfast.
answer_text: The Lower Malone area is currently scheduled as part of a routine enforcement patrol, Monday to Friday between 8:00 and 18:00.  As a result of requests from residents, this area is also randomly patrolled in the evenings. Our enforcement approach is flexible and should you wish to highlight a particular problem area please contact the parking enforcement team at Parking.request@infrastructure-ni.gov.ukI am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes and it is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to install a pedestrian crossing.
answer_text: The cost of installing a crossing can vary significantly and will be dependent on a range of factors, including:The type of pedestrian crossing;Site-specific conditions, such as road width, road layout and traffic management requirements; andThe extent of associated works, including kerb realignment / resurfacing; drainage adjustments; and utility diversions.Given these variables, my Department does not collate information on the average cost of a pedestrian crossing, as the provision of any average would not accurately reflect the cost of installations across different locations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will publish the details of her proposed developers compulsory wastewater levy.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure. The consultation ran for 14 weeks and sought views on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions – either on a voluntary basis or in the form of a compulsory levy – which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock the capacity of our sewerage network. 293 responses to the consultation were received.I am currently considering my decision on the way forward on developer contributions with a view to announcing this in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department plans on publishing the multi-year budget before Christmas 2025 recess.
answer_text: I will bring proposals on a draft Budget to the Executive in December, following the Chancellors Autumn Budget on 26 November.Subject to Executive agreement on the draft Budget, I will shortly thereafter launch a 12-week public consultation.  Publication of the draft multi-year Budget document will form part of this process.  Feedback from the consultation will help shape the Executive’s decisions on the final Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his invitation to visit Israel was initially received (i) through his Ministerial office; (ii) posted to his Ministerial office; or (iii) sent to the Minister’s email.
answer_text: The invitation was not received (i) through my Ministerial office; (ii) posted to my Ministerial office; or (iii) sent to my Ministerial email address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he will consider facilitating costing for official opposition budget policies, as in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I have already provided a detailed assessment on this matter in my response to your letter. As outlined in that response, the position taken by the Executive is consistent with that adopted by the devolved administrations in Scotland and Wales, where the civil service does not provide for the costing of opposition policy proposals. I would encourage you to explore existing mechanisms that are available to support the development of costed opposition policy proposals including AQW, AQO, briefings both oral and written available to Assembly Committees and published Department of Finance reports and other widely available figures and statistics. As well as other resources available including the Assembly Finance Assistance for Political Parties Scheme which in addition to the standard payment made to all parties, the Scheme offers enhanced financial support to opposition parties, comprising a base payment of £25,000, supplemented by an additional £7,500 for each Assembly Member affiliated with the party.I would also suggest that the Opposition further explore the existing mechanisms that are available to them such as the Assembly Research and Information Service. My department currently operates with a small team focused on delivering the Executive’s Budget, and given the ongoing financial constraints, it is unlikely that additional resources will be available to expand the team’s capacity to provide such a facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer (i) AQW 33437/22-27; and (ii) AQW 33438/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 33437/22-27 was answered on 9 January 2026	AQW 33438/22-27 was answered on 11 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to monitor the pilot scheme launched by the homeless charity Crisis in London, under which the charity will register as a landlord to better provide accommodation for homeless people.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to monitor the Crisis pilot scheme. I have already introduced the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA) scheme to enable charities in Northern Ireland to fund the purchase of properties as a route into housing for those who cannot access permanent social housing or private rented accommodation.The scheme provides funding at a low interest rate for the provision of well-managed, long term, private rented sector accommodation at an affordable rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of people, currently being held in custody in prisons within Northern Ireland, who have not yet been found guilty in a court.
answer_text: On 11th November 2025, 41.2% of the prison population in Northern Ireland were on remand awaiting trial and had not been convicted of the offences for which they were detained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department plans to spend on consultancy companies, related to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: As a result of ongoing appeal on the A5, all scheme costs in relation to the project are currently under review.  The requirement for this financial year is currently being assessed as part of the in-year monitoring round.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any resurfacing works her Department has planned for Sicily Park, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years.Whilst there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Belfast City Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department instructed the Housing Executive to remove priority need status, for single refugees seeking temporary accommodation.
answer_text: Priority Need is defined in Article 5 of the Housing (NI) Order, 1988.  This has not changed.The Department has not issued any new guidance or direction in relation to the definition of priority need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether he informed the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office in advance about (i) his fact finding mission to Israel; and (ii) his plans to visit Ofek School in East Jerusalem.
answer_text: I did not inform the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office in advance of (i) the fact-finding mission to Israel or (ii) my plans to visit Ofek school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions taken by her Department to alleviate the cost of car insurance, including any discussions she has had with the Financial Ombudsman Service.
answer_text: The regulation of Financial Services including insurance services is a reserved matter.  Complainants who contact my Department’s Trading Standards Service Consumerline about car insurance are advised to contact their insurer directly to try to resolve the issue. If they are dissatisfied with the response, then they should escalate the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). The FOS is a free, independent service that resolves disputes between consumers and financial services firms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when he plans to provide a statement regarding the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle, produced by the University of Stirling.
answer_text: I made a statement to the Assembly on 18 November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update for his Department's work towards supporting staff retention of physician associates from the Ulster University's MSc programme.
answer_text: My Department has been working proactively with employing Trusts, through the Regional PA Forum they have established, to try to ensure that posts are available across the HSC for PA graduates despite the very challenging financial situation faced by the HSC. It is recognised that the identification of recurrent funding is an essential requirement for the creation of jobs for the PAs graduating from the Ulster University programme each year. My officials will continue to work with stakeholders on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his meeting at the Ofek school complied with the requirements of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2021.
answer_text: I am satisfied that I complied with the requirements of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021. The Act places two key obligations in relation to meetings. First, it requires that the Permanent Secretary ensures appropriate arrangements are in place for any relevant meeting, including a written record. Second, it requires that Ministers take reasonable steps to ensure that a civil servant attends any such meeting.  The Act defines a “relevant meeting” as a pre-arranged meeting set up to conduct official business. In the context of my recent visit to Israel, no educational meetings were confirmed prior to departure. The itinerary issued on the day of travel indicated that an education engagement was to be confirmed and I was not aware of the specifics until the day of the visit.As a result, an official did not attend the school tour, nor would it have been reasonably possible to ensure attendance in those circumstances. The school visit was a tour rather than a formal meeting. There was no agenda, no formal actions and no decisions taken. The purpose was to observe educational practice and pedagogy, not to conduct official business. Therefore, the requirements of the Act did not apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has taken place of his and his Department's adherence to the provisions set out in the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: I thank the Member for the question. The Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 places two key obligations in relation to meetings. First, it requires that the Permanent Secretary ensures appropriate arrangements are in place for any relevant meeting, including a written record. Second, it requires that Ministers take reasonable steps to ensure that a civil servant attends any such meeting.The Act defines a “relevant meeting” as a pre-arranged meeting set up to conduct official business. In the context of my recent visit to Israel, no educational meetings were confirmed prior to departure. The itinerary issued on the day of travel indicated that an education engagement was to be confirmed and I was not aware of the specifics until the day of the visit.As a result, an official did not attend the school tour, nor would it have been reasonably possible to ensure attendance in those circumstances. Following the visit, on 3 November, officials met with me to review the engagements against the provisions of the Act and wider guidance. That review concluded that none of the engagements during the trip met the definition of a relevant meeting under the Act.The school visit was a tour rather than a formal meeting. There was no agenda, no formal actions and no decisions taken. The purpose was to observe educational practice and pedagogy, not to conduct official business. Therefore, the requirements of the Act did not apply.Finally, I want to make clear that the Act does not govern the issuing of press releases. Press releases cover a wide range of activities, including school visits, ceremonies and announcements, many of which do not constitute relevant meetings under the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider implementing a school crossing patrol or traffic calming measures on Windsor Avenue, Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need. Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the location did not score highly enough for inclusion of a controlled crossing in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become availableThe most recent traffic calming assessment was conducted in 2021, and I have asked my officials to undertake a review of this location given the passage of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the spending on traffic calming measures by District Electoral Area in the Belfast area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.Details of all completed traffic calming schemes are included in the Department’s annual reports to Belfast City Council which are published on my Department’s website.However, the table below provides the expenditure on specific traffic calming engineering measures in the Belfast City Council area over the past five years, including traffic surveys,  and enhancements to original schemes such as signage and road markings. 2020-212021-222022-232023-242024-252025-26Total spend £12,2029,4568,0935,2434,4777,028My officials also assist private developers and other statutory bodies in the legislative requirement for traffic calming measures associated with their projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the spending on road maintenance by District Electoral Area in the Belfast area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not record road maintenance spend by District Electoral Area.  However, the table below sets out the road maintenance spend within Belfast City Council for the period 2019-20 to 2023-24, comprising Resource and Capital Structural maintenance expenditure: District CouncilStructural MaintenanceYear 19-20Year 20-21Year 21-22Year 22-23Year 23-24£mBelfast City CouncilResource1.21.81.71.82.4Capital7.15.36.18.48.6Total8.37.17.810.211.1Note: Figures may contain roundings and District Council analysis for 2024-25 is not yet available.Resource Structural Maintenance covers patching of carriageway and footways.  Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any progress made on the Together: Building a United Community review, particularly any work on the implementation of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition report recommendations.
answer_text: The T:BUC review is currently under consideration. We are actively working with Officials regarding the development of a refreshed approach to Good Relations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is satisfied that he complied with sections seven to nine of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, during his fact-finding mission to Israel.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to reduce his Department’s dependency on agency staff following the 237 per cent increase in yearly agency staff costs.
answer_text: DAERA’s vacancies are, like those in other departments, filled in accordance with NICS policies and procedures and the Department of Finance’s NICSHR 	teams work with all departments on an ongoing basis to prioritise recruitment across all NICS departments within their allocated financial and staff resources. 		Temporary roles support essential services and delivery of statutory and departmental priorities and Windsor Framework obligations. A large proportion of the agency costs relates to Windsor Framework responsibilities that rest with the Defra Secretary of State in 2024-25. The department continues to monitor expenditure to ensure transparency and value for money.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has or will have any engagement with the Pharmaceutical Society NI regarding their enforcement of fitness to practise guidelines.
answer_text: I most recently met with the Chair and Chief Executive of the Pharmaceutical Society on 4 August 2025. This meeting covered a range of issues in relation to current and future performance of the Society. I also met with representatives of the Professional Standards Authority (PSA) on 21 May 2025, a meeting at which the performance of the Society in relation to PSA standards was discussed.My officials are in regular contact with the Society to monitor progress against the standards as required by the PSA. The enforcement of fitness to practise guidelines remains a matter for the Pharmaceutical Society as the regulatory body for pharmacists in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had regarding the creation of a pathway for the interim provisions of medication to treat ultra rare diseases that have been approved by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency, but are in the process of being approved by the National Institute for Healthcare and Excellence.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that patients in Northern Ireland have timely and equitable access to clinically and cost-effective medicines. While medicines may be authorised by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) for use in the UK, decisions regarding routine access are informed by Health Technology Assessments (HTAs), such as those undertaken by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). My Department has a formal link with NICE under which Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland. My Department remains committed to ensuring that NI patients are able to access innovative medicines which are both clinically and cost-effective, including those recommended by NICE for ultra rare diseases under their Technology Appraisal (TA) and Highly Specialised Technologies (HST) programmes. Following a commitment in the NHS 10 year plan, in  October 2025, the  MHRA and NICE launched a pilot for an accelerated aligned pathway to speed up patient access to new medicines by streamlining and aligning decisions on licensing and value, and NI patients will stand to benefit from this development in terms of faster access by virtue of our formal link with NICE. Additionally, my Department formally accepted an invitation to join the Innovative Licensing and Access Pathway (ILAP) in December 2024 which is focused on getting the most innovative new medicines to patients more quickly. This scheme is designed to reduce the time it takes for promising new treatments to move through development, licensing and, if approved, to NHS patients and my officials are engaged with MHRA colleagues to explore how the HSC can participate in a single UK-wide enhanced system readiness pathway. My Department remains committed to supporting initiatives that improve outcomes for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on a waiting list for spinal surgery.
answer_text: The number of people on the current active elective inpatient waiting list for elective Spinal surgery is 1,478 patients, with  783 patients awaiting surgery in Musgrave Park Hospital 684 awaiting surgery in Royal Victoria Hospital 11 awaiting surgery in RBHSC (patients awaiting surgery for spinal fractures are excluded from the above figures).This information has been supplied by Belfast Trust and relates to the position at 5 November 2025.  It is not possible for these figures to be validated by the Department at this time.The Belfast Trust spinal surgery service is fully commissioned to support a team of 8 surgeons and associated clinical infrastructure, including neurophysiology to deliver elective spinal surgery and trauma care for adults and children.  In recent years there have been challenges in maintaining the full complement of specialist medical staff, impacting on service availability. This has been compounded by difficulties in the recruitment of neurophysiology staff required to undertake spinal cord monitoring, due to national shortages in this service.  The Belfast Trust has also advised of increasing demands for spinal fractures and emergency care which has further impacted on the available capacity.As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, I detailed how the elective care ringfenced allocation for Health in this year’s budget will be invested to tackle waiting lists that include targeting long waiting patients with £10m set aside to support the expansion of mega clinics to enable a range of specialties, including spinal patients to be assessed earlier.Specifically, in respect of spinal surgery, additional in year funding has been allocated to help reduce the number of scoliosis patients waiting over 13 weeks.  To date, additional capacity to deliver these procedures has been secured with two NHS Trusts.  An Independent Sector contract has also been agreed to provide Interoperative (spinal) Monitoring in reach to Belfast Trust which will augment the delivery of spinal work locally.  The Department has also supported referrals for very complex scoliosis cases to the wider NHS to ensure these were undertaken within the required clinically specified window.  Good progress has and is continuing to be made to reduce waiting times for scoliosis and to commence the rebuild of resilience in the local spinal service.  Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two, and investments at this level or higher will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.I hope that the information provided give some reassurance that there are plans in place to address overall waiting lists with specific arrangements in place to help reduce the current numbers of patients on the waiting list for scoliosis spinal surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the proposed reforms to the house buying and selling process, in England and Wales.
answer_text: I understand that the Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government (MHCLG) is currently consulting on a range of proposals relating to aspects of the private house transaction process, including the conveyancing process and the role of estate agents. None of the aspects being consulted on fall to the policy remit of this Department.However, my officials are engaged with MHCLG officials to track the reforms, and should any proposals come forward that will have relevance to NI and fall to the remit of this Department, I will consider them further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of neurological care for conditions such as multiple sclerosis; and (ii) to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve care.
answer_text: I am aware of the substantial pressures within neurology services and the impact those pressures are having on people with neurological conditions, including those with Multiple Sclerosis. It is clear that more must be done to address those pressures and improve services.  My Department has undertaken a Regional Review of Neurology Services. As part of this Review, the voice of people with neurological conditions has been central. The Northern Ireland Neurological Charities Alliance was a member of the Neurology Review Team from the outset. There has also been targeted engagement with people with neurological conditions during the course of the Review which has been further supplemented through the use of resources including Care Opinion.The final report from this review proposes a range of recommendations which, if implemented, will drive improvements across services. My Department recently concluded a public consultation on the final report and officials are currently reviewing responses to that consultation with a view to identifying next steps including the development of an implementation plan.I must be clear however that full implementation of the report’s recommendations will require significant additional funding. While I will consider this in the context of competing priorities and future budget settlements, I must caution that funding will be a significant challenge. In this context the scale and pace of implementation may have to be adjusted in line with the funding available. In the interim, my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group continue to actively consider ways to drive improvements in services using current resources through collaborative approaches and innovative ways of working.

answer_text: The number of people on the current active elective inpatient waiting list for elective Spinal surgery is 1,478 patients, with  783 patients awaiting surgery in Musgrave Park Hospital 684 awaiting surgery in Royal Victoria Hospital 11 awaiting surgery in RBHSC (patients awaiting surgery for spinal fractures are excluded from the above figures).This information has been supplied by Belfast Trust and relates to the position at 5 November 2025.  It is not possible for these figures to be validated by the Department at this time.The Belfast Trust spinal surgery service is fully commissioned to support a team of 8 surgeons and associated clinical infrastructure, including neurophysiology to deliver elective spinal surgery and trauma care for adults and children. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work undertaken to implement proposals and recommendations, in the report of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition, in relation to parades and protests, since 2024.
answer_text: The recommendations included in the FICT Report were considered as part of the T:BUC Review. We are actively working with officials regarding the development of a refreshed strategic approach to Good Relations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress for (i) bus priority measures from Clifton Street to Innisfayle Park, Belfast; and (ii) the park and ride facility site at O’Neill Road, Belfast.
answer_text: On the 25th February 2025 I was pleased to announce the next steps for the Belfast Rapid Transit 2 project which will see implementation of this innovative service across the North and South of the city, bringing the benefits already established by the Glider service to communities living along this route.My announcement represented an initial £48 million investment in our public transport infrastructure and is one of the projects being taken forward by my department as part of the Belfast Region City Deal. Funding of £35m has been allocated from our Deal partners, with an additional £13 million to deliver the initial phase. My officials are finalising the Outline Business Case (OBC) in line with these delivery options. Once the OBC is approved, officials will progress the next steps associated with this project which will include consideration of the provision of the park and ride on the O'Neill Road and bus priority measures from Clifton Street to Innisfayle Park, Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans for traffic management measures for (i) Cardigan Drive; (ii) Knutsford Drive; (iii) Kelvin Parade; (iv) Kingsmere Avenue; and (v) Ashgrove Park, Belfast, including the expected date for the commencement and completion of the works.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.  Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the proposals for Cardigan Drive, Knutsford Drive, Kelvin Parade, Kingsmere Avenue and Ashgrove Park did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. These schemes will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.Details of schemes being taken forward are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the York Street Interchange scheme.
answer_text: In October 2022 my predecessor published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review report and asked officials to take forward further work to refine the options within it.Officials have completed this work and have been engaging with key stakeholders on the York Street Interchange project, to gather their assessment of the report.Officials have presented the findings of this work to me and I am taking the time to consider the next steps for this key infrastructure project.As the scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship project, I have asked my officials to consider possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate the scheme progression. This work is at an early stage and will take several months to complete.
