question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to address the waiting time for urgent spinal surgery.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust spinal surgery service is fully commissioned to support a team of 8 surgeons and associated clinical infrastructure, including neurophysiology to deliver elective spinal surgery and trauma care for adults and children.  In recent years there have been challenges in maintaining the full complement of specialist medical staff, impacting on service availability. This has been compounded by difficulties in the recruitment of neurophysiology staff required to undertake spinal cord monitoring, due to national shortages in this service.  My officials have engaged with the Trust on these challenges and developing a stabilisation plan including support from the wider NHS.  This is to facilitate greater service resilience, increase capacity and reduce waiting times.  The stabilisation plan will allow the Trust to secure additional spinal surgery capacity in 2025/26 and for two further years, facilitating a reduction in waiting times for spinal surgery whilst also facilitating the required training and recruitment of spinal cord monitoring staff. As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, I detailed how the elective care ringfenced allocation for Health in this year’s budget will be invested waiting list activities. Measures which will be taken forward to tackle waiting lists that include targeting long waiting patients, an expansion of elective services in Primary Care, by investment in Mega Clinics and the Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme. Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two, and investments at this level or higher will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.Specifically, in respect of spinal surgery, additional in year funding has been allocated to help reduce the number of scoliosis patients waiting over 13 weeks.  To date, additional capacity to deliver these procedures has been secured with two NHS Trusts.  An Independent Sector contract has also been agreed to provide Interoperative (spinal) Monitoring in reach to Belfast Trust which will augment the delivery of spinal work locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the Disabled Facilities Grant.
answer_text: My Department, in conjunction with the Department of Health and the Housing Executive, is undertaking a review of the Disabled Facilities Grant as part of a wider examination of Private Sector housing grants.This in-depth review covers both mandatory and discretionary grants and is being guided by an agreed Terms of Reference. A cross-departmental Project Board is overseeing the work, which is advancing across several key workstreams.I have prioritised the completion of the review within this mandate, and I intend to bring forward policy proposals for public consultation in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of Departmental staff who work to produce the Little Changes Podcast, including a full-time employee figure; (ii) the number of Departmental staff who participate in the Little Changes Podcast, including the time they spend taking part in the Podcast; (iii) all external production costs associated with the production of the Podcast; (iv) all payments made to external guests who have appeared on the Podcast; (v) any other costs associated with the production of the Podcast; and (vi) the number of people who have listened to the Pod
answer_text: The podcast is presented, recorded, produced and edited by one member of departmental staff in addition to their other duties.One member of departmental staff participates in each podcast – recording takes around 30 minutes of their time.There are no external production costs.No payments are made to external guests.Audio production equipment was purchased in summer 2025 at a total one-off cost of £1,297The podcast, to date, has been downloaded 161 times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he raised the reserve claim for the costs associated with the PSNI data breach, at his meeting with the Chancellor, on 12 August 2025.
answer_text: I have raised this matter repeatedly with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury as have my officials with Treasury officials – this is the appropriate channel for requesting access to the Treasury Reserve. On 11 August 2025, I wrote formally to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, on behalf of the Executive, to request access to the Reserve for the costs relating to the PSNI Data breach. Since then, there has been ongoing engagement with the Treasury on this issue, including between the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and me, and at official level. At the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee on 17 October, I again pressed the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on this matter.  I also wrote to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 23 October, in advance of the Autumn Budget, setting out the Executive’s priorities and challenges.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether traffic calming measures will be installed around the Carryduff roundabout.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.As the Carryduff Roundabout is situated on an arterial route carrying some 20,000 vehicle per day, it would not be deemed a suitable route when considering the introduction of traffic calming measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 32431/22-27, to detail the routes from Northern Ireland airports, which qualified for reduced long haul Air Passenger Duty, in the 12 month period up to June 2025.
answer_text: Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) data indicates that there were 5 destinations that were serviced by direct long-haul flights in the 12-month period to June 2025. These destinations were: Hurghada, Egypt; Melbourne Orlando, USA; Bridgetown, Barbados; St John’s, Canada; and Cancun, Mexico.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions her department can take to remove untaxed vehicles, which have remained on public roads for months.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (motor tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea, on behalf of the British Treasury. If you wish to request further information from the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the feasibility studies relating to the implementation of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, will be published, namely (i) Belfast to Dublin electrification; (ii) Knockmore to Antrim; (iii) Portadown to Armagh; and (iv) Portadown to Derry.
answer_text: Translink has submitted these studies to my Department. They are currently under consideration and I expect to publish the outcomes in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the scale and impact of the sale of illegal and counterfeit tobacco in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information in relation to the sale or seizures of illegal or counterfeit tobacco in Northern Ireland, as it does not fall within my Department’s remit to do so.HM Revenue and Customs record the tax gap and financial impact of illicit tobacco and cigarettes on the economy and on a UK wide basis.  The most recent report can be found at: Measuring tax gaps 2025 edition: tax gap estimates for 2023 to 2024 - GOV.UKTax evasion activity through the sale of illegal tobacco and cigarettes takes money away from the funding of public services; affects local businesses; and can harm communities as profits are used to fund further organised criminality.  Such illicit products can also cause physical harm.  The Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) is also, of course, alert to the issue and their most recent Annual Report and Threat Assessment contains information and case studies from operational partners who are involved in enforcement activity across Northern Ireland.  A copy of the report can be found at: Annual Report & Threat Assessment 2023/24

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will take action to simplify and strengthen the reporting and enforcement of the sale of illegal and counterfeit tobacco.
answer_text: HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) is the lead agency for enforcement of the sale of illegal or counterfeit tobacco across the UK and therefore it does not strictly fall within my area of responsibility.  I am aware that in January 2024, HMRC in conjunction with Border Force, published a UK wide strategy for dealing with the issue.HMRC work closely and collaboratively with all law enforcement colleagues on this issue on a UK wide basis.  Examples and case studies of how Organised Crime Task Force operational partners work together in Northern Ireland can be found in the most recent Organised Crime Task Force Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2023/24.  A copy of the report can be found at: Annual Report & Threat Assessment 2023/24

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to introducing statutory regulation for funeral directors, following the recent introduction of a mandatory Funeral Director Code of Practice in Scotland via the Department of Health and Social Care.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total value of non-domestic vacant rates relief, provided to sites inside the curtilage of the Tribeca site, Belfast.
answer_text: ANSWERThe value of Non-Domestic Vacant Relief, provided to ratepayers of properties inside the curtilage of the Tribeca site, Belfast, for the 2025/26 rating year is £378,144.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) powers; and (ii) functions, of the Advocate General for Northern Ireland, in relation to the operation of criminal law in Northern Ireland or any other aspect of devolved matters.
answer_text: The Advocate General for Northern Ireland is the chief legal adviser to the UK Government on NI law. The post is held concurrently by the Attorney General for England and Wales. As the Advocate General for Northern Ireland is a UK Law Officer, your question may be better directed at the UK Attorney General’s Office. My Department has never considered it necessary to compile and maintain a list of the powers and functions of the Advocate General for Northern Ireland in relation to matters within its remit, and to produce one would take considerable time and effort for no gain, whilst detracting from more pressing priorities. I am aware that the UK Government proposes a role for the Advocate General in the Northern Ireland Troubles Bill, which was introduced in Parliament on 14 October. According to the Explanatory Notes for the Bill, a new mechanism is proposed whereby the Solicitor General (as delegated by the Advocate General for Northern Ireland) will assess inquest cases which had not been assigned a responsible coroner, including those the Attorney General for Northern Ireland had directed to be held as inquests prior to 1 May 2024, and decide whether they should be held as an inquest or transfer to the proposed new Legacy Commission. However, as this is UK Government legislation, you may wish to direct any questions as to the envisaged role to the UK Government.I cannot speak in respect of other aspects of devolved matters; under the devolved arrangements, these are the responsibility of the relevant Ministers, to whom such enquiries should be directed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he has tabled a paper to the Executive, on new fiscal powers.
answer_text: ANSWERYes, I have tabled a paper with Executive colleagues on my approach to discussions with the British Government on progressing a full Fiscal Framework, with fiscal devolution a core part of that.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of fines; and (ii) other penalties, issued for breaches of Smoke Control Areas, in each of the past five years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: District councils are responsible for the enforcement of smoke control provisions in Northern Ireland. My Department does not receive details regarding the number of fines or other penalties issued for breaches in smoke control areas (SCAs). Requests for information on SCA fines and penalties should be made directly to the individual district councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he considered implications on Northern Ireland students applying for institutions in the Republic of Ireland, when considering plans to remove AS levels.
answer_text: My Department’s consultation on the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels is an opportunity for students, parents, educators, employers and wider stakeholders to contribute by sharing their views on how we can improve the structure, content and assessment of these qualifications. This includes a proposal to remove AS levels to reduce the pressure on students and teachers and free up time for teaching and deeper learning. My proposals reflect a clear ambition to ensure that CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels remain relevant both nationally and internationally. In relation to concerns about applying for higher education in the Republic of Ireland, I can confirm that, if the decision is made to remove CCEA AS levels, standalone AS levels from other awarding organisations would still be available for the very small number of students requiring them. Consideration will be given to any concerns raised through the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of meetings, or briefings, he has had on fiscal devolution.
answer_text: I have held a number of meetings where fiscal devolution, and the importance of the Executive being provided with additional tax powers has been discussed. This includes meetings with the Chancellor, Rachel Reeves MP; Secretary of State, Hilary Benn MP; and with Chief Secretary to the Treasury, James Murray MP. I have also held internal briefings with my departmental officials on fiscal devolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed developer levy, to fund wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure. The consultation ran for 14 weeks and sought views on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions – either on a voluntary basis or in the form of a compulsory levy – which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock the capacity of our sewerage network.293 responses to the consultation were received.  These responses are currently being considered with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the active travel schemes that have received funding, since the passage of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) the amount received by each scheme.
answer_text: A breakdown of active travel schemes that have received funding since the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 is included within the summary of overall active travel expenditure. This summary is available at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/details-spend-active-travel-introduction-climate-change-act-northern-ireland-2022.A further breakdown of support to councils for greenways and active travel related facilities is detailed below: CouncilFunding allocated/ committed 2022/23£k2023/24£k2024/25* £kAntrim & Newtownabbey Ballyclare Town Greenway78245-Antrim & Newtownabbey Valley Park active travel improvements--150Ards & North DownComber to Newtownards Greenway 24--Ards & North Down Newtownards to Green Road Bangor Greenway20-1,638Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon Dromore Town Park – Bridge & Boardwalk Greenway400--Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon Cycling facilities and monitoring40Belfast Lagan Gateway Greenway (Phase 2)3493578Belfast Active travel enablers and cycling facilities333196(15)Belfast Belfast Bikes Scheme--500Belfast Sailortown/ Titanic Quarter Bridge – Support for scheme development to RIBA stage 2--150Belfast Under The Bridges project --146Derry & StrabaneStrathfoyle Greenway (including DfC and DAERA contributions)2,029--Fermanagh & OmaghOmagh and Gortin Greenway feasibility9--Mid UlsterDungannon Park active travel Pedestrian/ Cycle Route improvements--6Mid Ulster Dungannon Park active travel route lighting upgrade--4Newry, Mourne & Down Newcastle active travel hub--335Newry, Mourne & Down Jane’s Shore Greenway 182--Newry, Mourne & Down Carlingford Lough Greenway 4604752Total**  3,4661,4023,675*To note spend for the financial year 2024-25 is provisional and subject to audit.** Totals subject to rounding. Further information on wider departmental activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists is also available on my department’s annual reports to councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to make the early detection of pancreatic cancer a priority.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether she raised the issue of access to justice, specifically with practising immigration solicitors, in discussions on (i) refugee integration; or (ii) racial equality strategies.
answer_text: I am acutely aware that individuals seeking asylum here are often fleeing circumstances of extreme danger and hardship. I am committed to ensuring that the services provided by my Department contribute to a safe and hospitable welcome, and—where relevant—support the promotion of positive community and race relations.As part of the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services, officials within my Department met with a range of stakeholders, including the Immigration Practitioners’ Group (IPG), to discuss how best to support individuals seeking legal advice and representation on immigration matters. These meetings were constructive and informative, and have helped shape our approach to improving access to justice in this area.Following these engagements, we are taking steps to enhance access to legal aid for issues relating to immigration, asylum, and human trafficking. This includes a commitment, set out in our draft delivery plan, to review the financial eligibility rules for legal aid to ensure they are simpler and fairer for those in need of such support. We are also working to better integrate legal assistance with the wider range of support measures available to promote refugee integration.While specific policy matters relating to refugee integration and racial equality strategies fall within the remit of The Executive Office (TEO), my Department continues to engage through the cross-cutting structures established by TEO. These structures help coordinate and provide accountability for the work of all Departments on these important issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proportion of students, on free school meals, leaving school at 18, who went on to higher education, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: The table below details the number and proportion of school leavers entitled to free school meals whose reported destination was ‘Institution of Higher Education’.  Data provided relates to leavers who attained at least one A-level (or equivalent qualification).  It can reasonably be assumed that the vast majority of leavers will be at least age 18 to have attained at this level.School leavers entitled to free school meals who attained at least one A-Level (or equivalent qualification) whose destination was recorded as Higher Education (1) (2)Academic YearInstitution of Higher Education (3)                     NumberInstitution of Higher Education (3)               %Total Leavers    Number2023/24114362.318362022/23122260.220302021/22131160.321732020/21154165.923392019/20145064.422502018/19132061.721402017/18128961.720882016/17130156.223152015/16134159.022732014/15116459.21967Source: School Leavers SurveyNotes:1. Excludes special and independent schools.2. Destination is as reported by the pupil and recorded by the school. It is not a record of enrolments in Higher Education.3. Destination is defined by Institution. An institution may provide courses at both Further and Higher Education levels.In line with overall trends among all school leavers in Northern Ireland, the proportion of those entitled to free school meals who progressed to an ‘Institution of Higher Education’ increased in both 2019/20 and 2020/21.  This may be attributed to the increase in the Maximum Student Number permitted in Higher Education Institutions in Northern Ireland, alongside a rise in the number of pupils achieving A-level grades that met admission requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he is supporting children from working class areas, whose parents wish to send them to an integrated school.
answer_text: The admission system is designed to support parental preference with all parents being able to apply for their child’s admission to any grant-aided school, including Integrated schools, through the Education Authority Admission Portal prior to the commencement of each school year.All schools have admissions criteria. If they receive more applications than they have places available, they use these criteria to decide who to offer places to. While the setting of admissions criteria is a matter for individual schools, my Department does provide schools with a list of admissions criteria it is recommended they use.  Post-primary schools are recommended to use a criterion that ensures applicants who are entitled to Free School Meals (FSME) be given priority so that the proportion of such children admitted is not less than the proportion of first preference FSME applications received within the total number of first preference applications received.My Department may also use Temporary Variation policy to provide additional places at Integrated schools should there be a need in an area for additional places within the sector.The Department of Education has a duty to encourage, facilitate and support the development of Integrated Education and this is reflected within the School Transport Policy which offers additional support to those who prefer Integrated Education by recognising Integrated schools as a distinct school category. This means that if a pupil wishes to attend an Integrated school and the distance criteria is met then transport assistance will be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department are taking to ensure that artists' voices are at the forefront of The Arts Council’s 10-year Strategic Plan.
answer_text: The development of the Arts Council’s 10-year Strategic Plan involved extensive consultation with the arts sector.  My Department has an established governance framework by which the activities of the Arts Council are assessed and monitored in line with the Arts Council’s statutory functions to develop and improve the knowledge, appreciation and practice of the arts and to increase public access to, and participation in, the arts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement that has taken place with the Minister for the Economy, on a multi-year funding and delivery plan.
answer_text: My Department engages as a matter of routine with the Department for the Economy on matters of mutual interest. In terms of the arts and culture, that includes in relation to the creative industries, broadcasting, and to common interests in respect of Northern Ireland Screen. 	There are real positives to be found in multi-year funding agreements and few downsides. The practical reality, however, is that, to be meaningful, a multi-year funding plan would need a multi-year budget.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they were asked to provide feedback on the performance of the Head of Civil Service, either formally or informally.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has confirmed that the position of Head of the NI Civil Service (HOCS) is unique in that the role has no line manager structure or reporting officer or countersigning officer to undertake the normal appraisal process as provided for under the NICS Performance Management policy. Therefore, the performance process applied to other SCS roles has not been applied to the HOCS role (i.e. current or previous post holders). "Performance management arrangements for all civil servants, including Senior Civil Service (SCS) are being reviewed."

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the need for speed reduction measures at Knockbreda Road, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that Knockbreda Road was last assessed for traffic calming measures in 2020. As the assessment is over three years old and in line with the current Departmental policy, I have asked officials to undertake a new assessment and to keep the Member informed of any outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the multi-year budget will include a bid for implementing the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, supported by the European Investment Bank (EIB), to consider the recommendations in the Review. Given the scale of the interventions and the magnitude of transformation involved, delivering the Review’s recommendations will be challenging and projects and initiatives will need to be prioritised to progress as funding opportunities become available. This work is still ongoing.I have, however, been able to provide Translink with a ring-fenced allocation of £1m in 2025/26 for the Rail Review studies, to ensure momentum of this work continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the deadline for her Department's announcement as to how future residents' parking schemes will be assessed.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost to the Civil Service of industrial tribunal payments, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department does not hold a centralised record of Industrial Tribunal payments (awards or settlements) for the whole Civil Service. From the records available to and collated by the HR Litigation Unit (HRLU) within NICSHR, the Department has identified 11 cases within scope of the request. The total amounts paid in settlements or awards (where available) for each year as follows:YearNumber of casesTotal amount paid in settlement/award and Counsel fees20212£20,00020220-20234£10,50020243£120,069.2120252£6,500Cases relating to live proceedings, including individual claims which, while resolved, are part of a wider group of ongoing cases or class actions, have been excluded from the figures above as the release of information relating to levels of settlement/costs could undermine the Department's position in dealing with the remaining claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any Fáilte Ireland branding has been extended into the north.
answer_text: My Department is working with Tourism NI, Tourism Ireland and Fáilte Ireland to bridge the visitor experience between the Causeway Coastal Route and the Wild Atlantic Way, as part of a €7.6m Shared Island funded project. A review of the current Causeway Coastal Route visitor offering has now been completed, following consultation with the public and industry stakeholders. The insights gathered have helped shape an Infrastructure Strategy for the Causeway Coastal Route and will inform the potential development of discovery points with signage concepts along the route. The project also includes delivery of a small capital investment scheme to improve interpretation provision and the visitor experience at existing visitor attractions along both routes. The brands are owned by Fáilte Ireland, and Tourism NI is in discussions with Fáilte Ireland in relation to extending these brands into the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 30735/22-27, to detail whether he has asked for Casement Park to be discussed at an Executive Committee meeting, since the Spending Review in June 2025.
answer_text: Responsibility for taking forward the project rests with the Communities Minister. Following the announcement, I wrote to the Communities Minister on several occasions seeking to discuss next steps in order to move this project forward as quickly as possible. As previously advised, I met the Communities Minister on 29 September to discuss Casement Park and other matters. I made it clear to the Minister that I and my officials stand ready to provide whatever support we can to make sure that the project is delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the oversight of the functioning of the Local Government Staff Commission, and its value for money.
answer_text: The Local Government Act (NI) 1972 provides the statutory basis for the Local Government Staff Commission (the Commission). The Commission’s annual Management and Dissolution Plan, which sets out the areas of operation, timeline and actions required by the Commission, is provided to my Department. In addition, my officials attend all Commission and Audit Committee meetings.The total council and NIHE contributions required to run the Commission in 2025/26 have increased by 2.4% from 2024/25 mainly due to higher payroll-related costs with the increase to Employers National Insurance contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Casement Park.
answer_text: The UK Government has now clarified its financial commitment to the redevelopment of Casement Park within its Spending Review of 11 June 2025.  There remains a significant gap between the cost for current plans for the redevelopment and the money that has been secured to date.It will be up to the GAA to consider what additional contribution they are prepared to make or how they will change their plans to fit the funding that is available in light of the Spending Review announcement. As Minister for Sport, I have repeatedly said that additional money coming to Northern Ireland for sport must be allocated on a fair and equitable basis, a principle first agreed as part of the 2011 Executive agreement and one I remain firmly committed to in the interests of fairness to all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on the plan to dissolve the Local Government Staff Commission; (ii) when the plan was first proposed; and (iii) the date the Department for Communities is working towards for the dissolution.
answer_text: Any decision to dissolve the Local Government Staff Commission (the Commission) would need to be considered by the Executive due to its cross-cutting nature.In October 2013, Minister Mark H. Durkan announced that the Commission would be dissolved in April 2017.  This was approved by the Executive in June 2014.The provisional date for the dissolution of the Commission is 31 March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the occasions when air pollution readings of high have been recorded in the last five years; and (ii) at which recording sites.
answer_text: Air pollution readings of high have been recorded on the following occasions in the last five years:Particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5) at North Down Holywood, 28 September 2025;PM2.5 at Newtownstewart, and Strabane Springhill Park (PM2.5 and PM10) on 11 December 2022; PM10 at Strabane Springhill Park on 14 September 2021.DAERA’s air pollution data is published on the ‘Northern Ireland Air’ website and can be downloaded from the ‘Data’ section. However, in order to understand the detail, strengths and limitations of this data, it is recommended that the DAERA ‘Air Pollution in Northern Ireland’ reports, also available from the Northern Ireland Air website, are reviewed as a starting point. These reports explain annual and daily averages, and how exceedances are determined in keeping with the legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his assessment of the effectiveness of the remit of the Local Government Staff Commission for Northern Ireland, in relation to its statutory powers.
answer_text: The Local Government Staff Commission operates within a framework of its enabling legislation consisting of a Management Framework Document and Financial Memorandum agreed with my Department, and an annual Management and Dissolution Plan which sets out the areas of operation, timeline and actions required by the Commission. In addition, my officials attend all Commission and Audit Committee meetings.I am satisfied that the Commission continues to ensure effective delivery of its statutory functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his assessment of the governance of the Local Government Staff Commission for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Local Government Staff Commission (Commission) maintains a sound system of internal governance that supports the achievement of the organisation’s statutory duties, aims, objectives and policies, whilst safeguarding public funds and its assets.The Commission provides my Department with Bi-annual assurance statements, and my officials also attend all Commission and Audit Committee meetings. I am satisfied with the standard of governance within the Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impacts of air pollution on public health in Belfast.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is the lead Department responsible for air quality policy and my officials work closely with colleagues in DAERA in relation to public health. My department has not assessed the impacts of air pollution on public health in Belfast.DAERA produce and publish annual reports on air pollution in Northern Ireland which are available on the DAERA website. In terms of local air quality, under The Environment (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, District Councils assess local air quality against air quality objectives. Where this assessment indicates that an air quality objective is unlikely to be met, the council must declare an air quality management area and prepare an Action Plan. DAERA, through the Local Air Quality Management grant, financially supports all district councils to carry out air quality reviews, assessments and to prepare and implement related action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the enforcement of Smoke Control Areas.
answer_text: Environmental Health Officers within the district councils are responsible for the enforcement of smoke control provisions in Northern Ireland. My Department does not receive quantitative data from the councils about enforcement action being undertaken.  My officials continue to work closely with district councils to bring about air quality improvements and provide financial support to the councils through the Local Air Quality Management grant.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what plans her Department is taking to alleviate parking issues in the Stranmillis area of South Belfast.
answer_text: The Department is currently preparing the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP) which will set out proposed changes to the transport network in Belfast. The road network in Belfast is close to or over capacity at peak times and, with limited options to expand it, the ETP proposals will aim to rebalance the transport network in favour of sustainable modes such as public transport and active travel, before this issue becomes a constraint to future growth in the city. Testing of the ETP proposals for Belfast is underway with public engagement planned for later this year.In addition, I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes and it is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course. The Stranmillis area is currently scheduled as part of a routine enforcement patrol, Monday to Friday between 8:00 and 18:00.  As a result of requests from residents, this area is also randomly patrolled in the evenings. Our enforcement approach is flexible and should you wish to highlight a particular problem area please contact the parking enforcement team at Parking.request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the value for money of subsidising the reduced rate of long-haul Air Passenger Duty.
answer_text: ANSWERLong-haul Air Passenger Duty (APD) is not subsidised. The Executive took a tax decision in 2012 to devolve and reduce the rate that applies to direct long-haul flights to zero. In line with the realities of tax devolution, we bear the financial consequences of the decision by the Executive that was in place at that time, and our block grant has been adjusted accordingly. This is consistent with the approach to tax devolution in Scotland and Wales.The latest data available indicates that over 21,000 passengers departing on direct long-haul flights from the North, in the 12-month period to June 2025, benefited from the zero rate of APD. This measure also supports the aviation industry locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the continued subsidy for reduced long haul Air Passenger Duty.
answer_text: An assessment of impact of the reduction in long haul air passenger duty will form part of my Department’s aviation policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her response to the Audit Office report on Active Travel in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report into active travel in Northern Ireland, which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North. I can confirm that the report will be considered very carefully and a response to the recommendations will be provided in line with the established process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any consideration his Department has given to extending the Initial Teacher Education bursary to (i) current PGCE students; and (ii) subject areas outside of the remit of the scheme.
answer_text: My Department recognises the dedication of students who have commenced Initial Teacher Education (ITE), including the financial commitment required. Their decision to pursue a career in teaching is deeply valued and appreciated.As announced, the ITE Bursary Scheme will apply to new entrants of relevant subjects, beginning their ITE programmes from September 2026 onwards. The purpose of the bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages.   Extending eligibility of the scheme to current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the pilot. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of approximately £279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.The pilot scheme will be subject to a full evaluation and future strategic workforce planning will continue to monitor teacher supply closely. This will help identify emerging needs and inform any necessary adjustments to the scheme’s remit to ensure it remains responsive to the areas of greatest shortage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of people who have died of (i) heat stroke; and (ii) heat-related conditions, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWEROfficial statistics on death registrations are produced by the NISRA, using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO).To identify deaths from heat stroke and heat-related conditions, NISRA used the ICD-10 framework to identify which cause of death codes were heat-related. The identification process for parts (i) and (ii) of the question were carried out as follows:(i) Deaths were determined to be from heat stroke when the code T67.0 (heatstroke and sunstroke) was present on the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD). (ii) Deaths were determined to be from heat-related conditions where at least one of the following codes was present in the list of causes: E86 (Dehydration), L50.2 (Urticaria due to cold and heat), L55 (Sunburn), X30 (Exposure to excessive natural heat excl. excessive heat of man-made origin), X32 (Exposure to sunlight), and X54 (Lack of water). As the list of codes used may not be exhaustive, the identification process was supplemented by a key word search of the cause of death information held on the MCCD text fields  using the term ‘heat’; resulting cases were then manually reviewed.Based on this approach, there were no cases found of deaths involving heat stroke or heat-related conditions, during the years 2019 to 2023 (the most recent five years for which data are available). As recognised by the World Health Organisation, heat stress can exacerbate a range of underlying illnesses such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, asthma etc and can increase the risk of accidents and transmission of some infectious diseases. However, unless heat was identified as a contributor to a death and documented on the MCCD it will not be captured in the above information.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a breakdown, by role, of the officials that accompanied the deputy First Minister on her trip to London for the state banquet for President Trump.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister was not accompanied by an official.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering a reform of the Waiting list Reimbursement Scheme to allow patients to access the support prior to their procedure, as opposed to the current system of patients accessing the support post procedure.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount spent on the deputy First Minister's visit to London for the state banquet for President Trump, broken down by (i) flights; (ii) accommodation; and (iii) other or miscellaneous costs.
To ask the Minister of Health what supports are in place for families with dyslexic children, in terms of organisations that can provide learning supports or toolkits.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) through the Local IMPACT Teams supports settings, schools and families in the area of literacy difficulties through a range of approaches:resource homepage at Literacy Resource accessible newsletters for families on essential literacy topics available at  Useful Resources and Links - Literacy | SEND Plan.assistive technology software such as Clicker 8, Texthelp, Read and Write via Read&Write For Education - Reading, Literacy & Assistive Software | Texthelp and also Literacy Guidance - Supporting pupils using Assistive Technology.pdfLiteracy Inclusion Toolkits for schools and families developed to assist educational settings in Northern Ireland to support those pupils who may be at risk of developing literacy difficulties Literacy-inclusion-ToolkitSupporting Learning at Home | Education Authority Northern Ireland provides useful support and guidance for families and links to a range of sources of support such as Learning | Parentkind

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans her Department has for providing concessionary travel for those with the half fare SmartPass.
answer_text: In June 2024, following a public consultation on the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, Minister O’Dowd announced that, due to the financial pressures facing the Department, he was not in a position to extend the half-fare concession to free travel for people with disabilities, but would reconsider introduction of the option in the future if the financial position allows. While the budgetary outlook has not changed, I had planned to reconsider the option to extend half fare travel to free travel again as part of the budget allocations decisions in this financial year but due to a major ticketing systems upgrade in Translink, it was not possible. Please be assured that the Department is actively working in close collaboration with Translink to consider how this policy could be implemented should budget be available. I remain committed to carefully considering the introduction of free public transport for people with disabilities and fully recognise the societal benefits such a measure could bring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the annual cost of (i) creating; and (ii) issuing (a) SmartPasses; and (b) replacement SmartPasses.
answer_text: Translink have advised that they do not disaggregate the costs of creating from issuing Smartpasses.  In 2024/25 the cost of creating and issuing new Free and Half-Fare Concessionary Smartpasses was £183,166; and £501,395 for replacement Free and Half-Fare Concessionary Smartpasses (this includes the ongoing renewal of all Concessionary Smartpasses when they reach their programmed expiry date 5 years from date of issue).  These costs include the cost of blank smartcard stock, supplier printing, supplier encoding, system software maintenance, Translink staff time to process applications and customer queries, postage of cards to customers etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what financial supports are in place for families to help with care home costs.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, financial support for care home costs is provided through a means-tested system administered by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. When an individual requires residential or nursing care, the Trust undertakes both a care needs assessment and a financial assessment to determine eligibility for support.The financial assessment takes into account the individual’s income, savings, and assets. As of current thresholds, individuals with capital exceeding £23,250 are expected to self-fund their care. Those with capital between £14,250 and £23,250 may receive partial support, while individuals with capital below £14,250 are eligible for full financial assistance, with their capital disregarded in the assessment.The value of a person’s home may be included in the financial assessment if they are entering permanent care, although at present this is subject to a 12-week disregard period. Importantly, if a spouse, partner, or dependent relative continues to live in the property, its value may be excluded from the calculation.For those eligible people residing in nursing homes, the HSC Trust provides a weekly nursing care payment to help cover the cost of nursing care. As of 2024, this payment is set at £100 per week. This contribution is made directly to the nursing home and is intended to offset the cost of clinical care provided to residents who have been assessed as requiring nursing-level supportIn addition, individuals may retain certain benefits, such as Attendance Allowance or the mobility component of Disability Living Allowance, depending on their circumstances. A Personal Expenses Allowance, currently set at £34.10 per week, is also protected to allow individuals to meet personal costs while in care.The Department remains committed to ensuring that individuals and families are supported in navigating the financial aspects of care provision and that assessments are carried out fairly and transparently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what mental health support is in place for parents who have lost a child.
answer_text: The loss of a child is one of the most devastatingly profound experiences a parent could face. One which can have implications for a parent’s mental health.Health and Social Care Trusts do not have a dedicated support service for parents who have lost a child. However, in accordance with a stepped model of care for mental health, support may be accessed via a number of pathways.Self-directed support is the first step of the stepped care model. There are various organizations which provide specific support to those who have experienced bereavement, including parents. The Northern Ireland Bereavement Network comprises of a range of organizations, including; HSC Trusts; Public Health Agency; Patient Client Council, Department of Health; Cruse Bereavement Support; Sands; Marie Curie; NI Healthcare Chaplains Association, among others. Its website, Bereaved NI (bereaved.hscni.net) provides information on bereavement services and guidance on how such services may be accessed.GP services within primary care would be the next step to accessing support. If a GP determines that additional support is necessary then a referral can be made for psychological therapies, or onto the Community Mental Health Service if they have concerns regarding mental illness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how she is tackling issues relating to (i) modern slavery; and (ii) human trafficking.
answer_text: My Department’s Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Child Exploitation Branch are responsible for Northern Ireland’s strategic response to tackling the issues of slavery and trafficking and for ensuring that victims identified in Northern Ireland receive the care and support they require for their recovery.On 10 July 2024 I published the Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027. This three-year Strategy serves to build on the achievements of previous strategies and to enhance Northern Ireland’s system wide collaborative response to tackling these heinous crimes. The Strategy includes a range of commitments and actions for a broad range of stakeholders, including my Department, the PSNI, PPS and those at the frontline of our social services. The implementation of the three-year Strategy is overseen by the Organised Crime Task Force Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Sub-group, with significant input from voluntary sector partners via our NGO Engagement Forum.Victims of slavery and trafficking are often some of the most vulnerable people in our society. Those identified as being potential victims of Slavery and trafficking in Northern Ireland are eligible for a range of support funded by my Department and delivered by our contracted support providers, Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid and Migrant Help. Support can include, among other things: safe and secure accommodation; access to health and social care services; counselling; and living expenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to ensure those from marginalised communities can have confidence in engaging with the PSNI.
answer_text: I welcomed the findings of the recent survey on public confidence in the PSNI.The Research and Statistical Bulletin, ‘Experience of Crime and Perceptions of Crime and Policing and Justice: Findings from the 2023/24 Northern Ireland Safe Community Telephone Survey’, was released in July 2025 and it is encouraging to see that public confidence in the police and police accountability arrangements has remained high at around 80%. This is particularly welcoming when considering the challenges PSNI have been facing in terms of workforce levels. While there is no room for complacency it is reassuring to note that public confidence in policing remains high.I have been working closely with the Chief Constable and my Executive colleagues to ensure PSNI receives the funding it requires to recover its numbers. I will continue to push for more funding and am pleased that we now have a clear roadmap for delivering the officer numbers set out in the PSNI recovery plan.However, the efforts by myself to address affordability remains a central focus in discussions at the Executive. I have continuously engaged with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues to advocate for a better financial settlement for my Department, to ensure the PSNI receives sufficient funding to stabilise and recover staffing numbers back to a sustainable level.  I will continue to engage and advocate as necessary.The Department of Justice along with Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) provide £4.25 million of annual funding for Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).PSNI serve all communities in Northern Ireland: however, where it has been identified that specific groups in society feel marginalised, targeted PSNI engagement may be delivered as part of the PCSP programme of work to ensure those people have confidence in PSNI. One example is Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon PCSP delivering events with the Migrant Centre and Portadown NPT. In 2023/2024 two sessions were held with children and young people from families of different ethnic minority groups so that they could get to know who their local Police were, and an information session on drugs issues and staying safe. One of the Neighbourhood Police Officers reported on seeing some of the event participants when out and about patrolling were willing to stop and engage with them. In 2024/2025 Portadown NPT held an event with women of differing nationalities - Portuguese, Brazilian, Romanian, Bulgarian and Syrian, to help them understand the laws to protect domestic violence victims, with those involved reporting they were more likely to engage with PSNI. Additionally, the Hate Crime Advocacy Service (HCAS) is also jointly funded by my Department and the PSNI to assist reporting of hate incidents to the police and provide support to victims throughout the investigative / criminal justice system process. It is delivered through an independent partnership coordinated by Victim Support NI and includes Hate Crime Advocat

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how she is ensuring access to legal aid for those dealing with issues related to (i) modern slavery; and (ii) human trafficking.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the essential role that legal aid plays in ensuring access to justice for people who are experiencing legal issues associated with their immigration status, including those who have arrived here as refugees, and those who have been victims of human trafficking.  I have, therefore, increased the limits on the amount of advice and assistance to which a person is entitled without the need to apply for an extension to the Legal Services Agency, and have committed to increasing rates of pay for legal aid work on asylum, immigration and human trafficking issues to help ensure the availability of legal representatives specialising in these important areas.  In addition, I will shortly consult on reforms that will lead to a simpler, fairer system of financial eligibility testing for legal aid that will include specific provisions to provide a simpler, fairer route to access legal support for those with issues related to immigration, asylum and human trafficking.  I have also committed, through the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, to work with the legal profession, and the community and voluntary sector, to explore provision of enhanced specialist support for people experiencing these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department calculates the cost of procedures for the purposes of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Costs are calculated by sending an applicant’s clinical details to the clinical coding team, managed through Digital Healthcare Northern Ireland (DHCNI) to generate an HRG code relative to the surgery. This code take into account and any co-morbidities the applicant has. The HRG code is sent to a finance team, who cross reference the associated coding through NHS tariff costs. Reimbursement costs are the NHS equivalent for treatment. These details are provided to applicants, in their prior approval letter, to allow them to make an informed final financial decision, as it is expected that there will be a cost deficit in the level of reimbursement. This will always be the case when choosing accessing care through a private provider compared to the NHS tariff rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) any engagement she has had with the Probation Board for Northern Ireland, to discuss the recent changes to probation guidelines, as proposed by the UK Government for England and Wales; and (ii) the feasibility of implementing similar changes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In answering, I am presuming the question refers to the UK Government’s Sentencing Bill, which was introduced to the House of Commons on 2 September. I note the content of the Bill, which will give effect to many of the recommendations arising from the Gauke Review, and aims to deal with more low-level offenders within the community, using suspended and community sentences, rather than short custodial sentences.My Department will consider these proposed changes to the sentencing framework in England and Wales as part of my planned review of review of further sentencing issues, due to commence this autumn.  Any consideration of introducing equivalent provision will require consultation with the Probation Board for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the consultation regarding future support of low carbon heat technologies in residential properties.
answer_text: The consultation on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties closed in January this year. Officials are currently finalising the analysis of responses provided, which will be used to inform policy decisions on the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat. My department plans to publish a response to the consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on shared care agreements for patients with ADHD.
answer_text: There is currently no specifically commissioned service for ADHD in Northern Ireland. While there is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system which has evolved in response to population demand and expertise within Health & Social Care teams, it is inconsistent and there are lengthy waiting lists across Trusts. In response, my Department has commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD. The findings of which will help inform options for the development of a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts.  The needs assessment will be published in November 2025 and will include recommendations about shared care arrangements.The implementation of recommendations and commissioning of service provision will be dependant on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to use (i) local council; and (ii) Policing and Community Safety Partnerships' data, obtained through speed indicator devices, in its planning of road repair and traffic management.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department currently has no plans to use any data collected by Speed Indicator Devices, which is held by local Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), to determine how road repairs or traffic management are decided.  My Department considers that Speed Indicator Devices are used to inform drivers of what is already displayed on the vehicle speedometer.  Speed Indicator Devices are normally provided by groups such as the local PCSPs, however, my Department does support PCSPs to facilitate their safe installation on existing road assets. Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the 2026-29 multi-year budget.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the effectiveness of the existing 20mph speed limit zones; and (ii) whether her Department has engaged with any similar schemes in other nearby jurisdictions where 20mph speed limit zones have been rolled out more widely, such as in Wales.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.The introduction of 20mph speed limit zones can significantly enhance road safety by reducing vehicle speeds and lowering the severity of collisions. These zones also support active travel by creating safer conditions for walking and cycling. In addition, they contribute to environmental improvements by reducing vehicle emissions and noise pollution. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year. Officials are engaging with counterparts in England, Scotland, Wales and the south as well as the PSNI as part of this work.  Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any actions her Department is taking to help alleviate parking congestion in the (i) Queens; and (ii) Stranmillis, areas in South Belfast.
answer_text: Many of our streets were not designed to accommodate today’s volumes of car ownership.  Being able to alleviate parking issues in areas such as Queens and Stranmillis will require the holistic consideration of parking alongside other transport issues.  The Department is currently preparing the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP) which will set out proposed changes to the transport network in Belfast. The road network in Belfast is close to or over capacity at peak times and, with limited options to expand it, the ETP proposals will aim to rebalance the transport network in favour of sustainable modes such as public transport and active travel, before this issue becomes a constraint to future growth in the city. Testing of the ETP proposals for Belfast is underway with public engagement planned for later this year.In addition, I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes and it is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course. The Stranmillis area is currently scheduled as part of a routine enforcement patrol, Monday to Friday between 8:00 and 18:00.  As a result of requests from residents, this area is also randomly patrolled in the evenings. Our enforcement approach is flexible and should you wish to highlight a particular problem area please contact the parking enforcement team at Parking.request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any artificial intelligence tool has been used to generate or edit all or part of any statement he has made to the Assembly.
answer_text: No information is held as to whether any statement has been edited using artificial intelligence.The Northern Ireland Civil Service recognises the potential benefits of using artificial intelligence to improve efficiency and productivity in the workplace. The Department of Finance provides guidance to ensure a consistent approach across government departments and to make sure civil servants use AI responsibly and ethically.It is entirely right that staff within my department embrace the opportunities presented by emerging technologies, especially where they can enhance efficiency and productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AWQ 28788/22-27, for an update on the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes.
answer_text: Please refer to my previous answer to this question, AQW 30870 22/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether all, or any part, of his oral statement of 16 September 2025 entitled Funding for Special Educational Needs Capital Investment was composed using artificial intelligence.
answer_text: My Oral Statement was composed using a detailed range of official statistics, internal Departmental and EA data and policy papers, previous departmental publications and statistics obtained from the Irish government not AI generated material.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of civil servants who received a performance-based pay rise in the last financial year.
answer_text: The 2023 Civil Service pay award, implemented retrospectively in 2024/25, included two main elements: a percentage uplift to pay scales and step progression for eligible staff.Eligibility for step progression is linked to a satisfactory performance review in the relevant reporting year. Under this process, 6,781 civil servants received performance-based pay progression.Those who did not progress were typically already at the top of their pay scale, on a single-point scale, or ineligible for the pay award for other reasons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has responded to the letter he received from the Minister of Finance on 10th June 2025, regarding the funding for Casement Park.
answer_text: I still await clarity on the £50m FTC allocation following the spending review. I will be meeting with the Finance Minister shortly to discuss this issue.
