question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps his Department is taking to support families in South Belfast who require respite services.
answer_text: My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The regional needs of Children with Disability are being considered across specific workstreams focusing on Residential Care (Workstream 3), Fostering (Workstream 4), Children with Disability (Workstream 5), and Family Support (Workstream 9). The Strategic Reform Programme aims to ensure that we have the right services to meet the holistic needs of young people (including those with disability), now and in the future.In October 2024, I had announced £13.1 million of recurrent funding with up to £2m funding available in year, to improve services for Children with Disability. This is in acknowledgement of the pressures in this area, which were also highlighted in the BBC Spotlight programme “I am not okay.”With this funding in 2024/25, the Health and Social Care Trusts have focused on meeting initial short-term priorities. The funding has been utilised to develop various initiatives across all five Trusts (which includes the Belfast HSC Trust) to improve Family and Community Support Services for Children with Disability, enhance Short Break provision and expand targeted care programmes. Establishment of Forest Lodge Temporary Children’s Home for Short Breaks The Belfast HSC Trust has progressed the opening of a temporary residential short breaks Children’s Home at Forest Lodge. This provides 1 bed x 2 nights per week when the children’s short breaks nursing home at Forest Lodge is not operational. These short breaks are for children who have a severe learning disability with other comorbid diagnoses, are on the edge of care, family circumstances are at risk of breakdown, and the children have higher need/ risk issues.  There are currently 5 children availing of short breaks in the temporary Forest Lodge Short Breaks facility, with a 6th child due to join following their discharge from hospital. The Belfast HSC Trust has had discussions with staff and parents, and to date there has been positive feedback received and clear evidence of young people having positive experiences within the service.The Belfast Trust has also commissioned short breaks from a voluntary/community sector provider.  This organisation is currently providing 30 bed nights per month for children with less complex needs.  Expansion of the Children’s Inreach Support Service.The Belfast HSC Trust’s Children’s Inreach Support Service work with children who are at imminent risk / approaching the edge of care and their families. One of the key objectives of the service is to work with and provide support to children and families in their own ho

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any actions his Department is taking to address invasive species such as Himalayan balsam in South Belfast.
answer_text: Officials rely on members of the public submitting records of invasive species either directly or via CEDaR at https://www2.habitas.org.uk/records/ISI  and/or using the iRecord app https://irecord.org.uk/app  . Reports of ‘new’ invasive species are risk-assessed and investigated according to degree of threat. DAERA designated 12 Widely Spread Species (WSS) under The Invasive Alien Species (Enforcement and Permitting) Order 2019. WSS include Himalayan balsam, giant hogweed and American skunk cabbage. Landowners are obligated to implement management measures if a WSS is reported on their land. Where their presence is confirmed, officials will contact the landowner to ensure steps are taken to prevent their spread and ultimately, their eradication. The NIEA Invasive Non-Native Species Team assist landowners with instigating site specific management measures by providing a wide range of management options on their website - https://invasivespeciesni.co.uk/site-management-resources/ A significant proportion of NIEA WSS enforcement cases are in the Lagan Valley region of south Belfast, where Himalayan balsam and giant hogweed are long established. The NIEA INNS Team work with Lagan Valley Regional Park, the Department for Infrastructure, Belfast City Council and the National Trust to actively control invasive species on the sites they manage in this locale. Landowners/managers are provided with detailed guidance - https://invasivespeciesni.co.uk/site-management-resources/ so their methodologies can be site specific and free from herbicides where possible. Additionally, DAERA Forest Service monitors and manages invasive species in Belvoir Forest in compliance with sustainable forest management standards.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of underspent Financial Transactions Capital in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) underspend for 2024-25 totalled £52.5million. At provisional outturn it was anticipated that £1.3 million would be carried forward to 2025-26 under Budget Exchange rules and that the remaining £51.2 million would be used to offset future repayments to the Treasury. As the Executive was required to repay 80% of allocations received from the Treasury, it had been agreed that underspends could be used to reduce these repayments.My officials recently received confirmation from the Treasury that all FTC previously provided on a gross basis has now been repaid. With FTC in the Spending Review being provided on a net basis, meaning that repayment to the Treasury is not required, this means that the £51.2 million is not currently required to offset future repayments. Should future FTC be provided on a gross basis this will be revisited with the Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the completion of the outstanding work for traffic calming measures on McClure Street, Belfast.
answer_text: To facilitate the re-opening of McClure Street and Cameron Street, the Department requires confirmation that the construction works undertaken by the developer meet all of the Department’s requirements.  However, at this time, the Developer has not yet provided all of the requested data to enable approval of the works.  My officials have, this week, received assurances from Apex Housing, their designers and contractors that the outstanding issues will be addressed as a matter of urgency however, McClure Street will remain closed to through traffic until the developer completes all the works to the appropriate specification and to the satisfaction of the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 28788/22-27, for an update on the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when public realm works associated with Belfast Grand Central Station will be completed.
answer_text: It is currently envisaged that public realm works associated with Belfast Grand Central Station will be completed by autumn 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any discussion on Casement Park has taken place in the Executive since the June Spending Review.
answer_text: The Executive has discussed Casement Park since the June Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures reasonable adjustments are provided for neurodiverse people accessing services through Health and Social Care.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that reasonable adjustments are consistently made for neurodiverse people accessing Health and Social Care (HSC), so that services are accessible, inclusive, and effective for everyone. We continue to work proactively with HSC Trusts to reduce barriers, meet individual needs, and fulfil statutory duties. A key focus currently is around embedding greater consistency and equity across the region.To support this, we hold regular monthly meetings with Heads of Service within Children and Young People services responsible for Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) pathways and Adult Autism pathways to align pathways and identify steps that can be taken to remove barriers that may prevent fair treatment or access to services.We also recognise that both staff and service users may be neurodiverse. In response to an identified training gap, we recently commissioned Middletown Centre for Autism to develop Neurodiversity Awareness training for HSC staff.Co-produced with neurodiverse staff at Middletown and shaped by service users with lived experience, this training is now available to all HSC staff via the Learn HSCNI platform. It focuses on three key areas:Autistic Differences – Understanding how autistic individuals experience the world and reflecting on how they may perceive HSC environments and services.Supportive Environments – Exploring how physical and social environments impact autistic service users, with practical guidance to create more inclusive spaces.Neuro-affirming Culture – Promoting inclusive language and practices that support neurodiverse staff and service users.The training has been live on HSC Learning platforms since 8 August 2025 and we are planning to promote these resources formally on World Mental Health Day on Friday 10 October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any further correspondence or conversations he has undertaken regarding the construction of Casement Park since the June Spending Review.
answer_text: Following the Spending Review announcement on 11 June, I wrote to the Communities Minister to say that I, and my department, stood ready to play a full and positive role in taking forward the Casement Park project and suggested a meeting with the Minister to discuss next steps in terms of delivery. My officials and I have been in conversations with the NIO, Treasury and Department for Communities in respect of Casement Park. Most recently, I met the Communities Minister on 29 September to discuss Casement Park and other matters.At this meeting I reiterated my desire to see the project move forward as quickly as possible and restated my commitment to do whatever I and my department can do to make sure this happens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline all works undertaken on Horn Drive, Belfast, over the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department is currently completing a carriageway and footway resurfacing scheme at Horn Drive, Belfast.  In advance of this my Department completed an upgrade of the street lighting in the area. Prior to these infrastructure upgrades three surface defects were repaired following routine inspections.  My Department is also aware of eleven works by utility companies that have been completed in the Horn Drive area in the last twelve months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Metro bus service journeys that take place each month.
answer_text: The number of Metro journeys by month for the previous 12 reporting periods is set out below.MonthNumber of Metro JourneysAugust 20241,378,345September 20242,250,351October 20241,860,778November 20241,752,597December 20242,035,734January 20251,432,106February 20251,690,238March 20252,215,007April 20251,556,100May 20251,712,833June 20252,119,149July 20251,295,512

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department followed any precedent from another jurisdiction when widening the definition of active travel spend, under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, to include items such as street lighting and footpath maintenance.
answer_text: The member will appreciate that it is incumbent on my department to consider all of its obligations in relation to Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 legislation. The department has not changed what it considers to be active travel, which is travelling by physically active means such as walking, wheeling, or cycling. A definition of active travel that includes walking, wheeling and cycling is common across these islands and beyond. Officials engage regularly with colleagues in other jurisdictions, and these discussions are invaluable in helping shape our thinking on those elements and activities that are beneficial to pedestrians and cyclists. Informed by these engagements it is also worth noting that direct comparisons between jurisdictions are complicated. Budgets are often developed and administered differently, and the split of functions between central and local government also differs significantly across these islands, with local councils in other jurisdictions responsible for many functions that are carried out by the department here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 26969/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider introducing festive fares on Metro services, in order to reduce the traffic congestion in Belfast during the Christmas season and during the continued disruption associated with the construction of Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to introduce festive fares on Metro or other Translink services.As public realm work continues around Grand Central Station, I have instructed my officials to advance a number of traffic measures to keep Belfast moving, particularly in the run-up to the busy festive period.A Communications Strategy has also been developed to help inform the public of the various measures being taken and to promote alternatives to private car use.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Departments assessment of when work will begin on the North South Interconnector.
answer_text: NIE Networks commenced construction works in respect of the North South Interconnector in Q4 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the proposals to strengthen legislation to protect inspection of schools were submitted to the Executive for discussion.
answer_text: It is not surprising that those who have engaged in non-cooperation with inspection as part of industrial action wish to retain that right. However, this is a question for wider society to consider.  Let us be clear: do we, as a society, believe that schools should be inspected?  If the answer is yes - and it must be - then ask yourself: should that inspection be voluntary?  Of course not.  No serious education system in the world leaves inspection to chance. Inspection is not a negotiable workplace duty.  We need a legal framework that guarantees consistent, independent oversight of educational quality, child protection and safeguarding.At the heart of any education system that truly promotes social mobility is a simple but powerful principle: every child deserves access to a good school.  Yet in Northern Ireland today, that principle is under threat.Due to prolonged industrial action, many schools have gone without inspection - without the external scrutiny support and challenge that are essential to driving improvement and safeguarding children. This is not a minor administrative issue. It is a systemic failure that puts standards and equity at risk.We cannot continue with the constant threat - and reality - of disruption. Disruption that is unmatched anywhere else in the UK or Ireland. Northern Ireland stands alone as an outlier. Every other jurisdiction has clear, enforceable legislation requiring cooperation with school inspection. We do not. That must change.Whilst I note the views of the Teaching Unions, I will proceed with this legislation. The rights of teachers to take industrial action must not override the rights of children to high-quality education and robust safeguarding procedures.I initially brought proposals on inspection to the Executive last November.  I am now preparing to return to the Executive with further evidence and to seek permission to introduce the inspection legislation to the Assembly. This is a critical step in restoring confidence, raising standards and protecting children.I trust both Executive Colleagues and Members will give this Bill the careful consideration it deserves and support its passage.  Together, we must close this legislative gap and protect the inspection process - and, in doing so, protect the children and young people we serve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether his Department asked if the £51 million in Financial Transactions Capital underspend for 2024-25 could be (i) added to the funding awarded for Casement Park; or (ii) used for other stadia projects.
answer_text: The positive allocation of £50 million to Casement Park as part of the Spending Review on 11 June marked a significant step forward for the Executive Flagship Project. The British Government has joined the Executive, the Irish Government and the GAA in the commitment of new and additional funding.	This additional funding commitment brings new momentum to this vital project which will deliver a modern sporting venue for generations to come, as well as being an economic driver for the surrounding communities and region.	The Financial Transactions Capital funding for Casement Park, is new funding and not a reallocation of the 2024-25 underspend. The FTC underspend for 2024-25 totalled £52.5 million. Of this, £1.3 million can be carried forward to 2025-26 under Budget Exchange rules. The remaining £51.2 million of underspend, which exceeds the carry forward limit, can no longer be reallocated to other eligible FTC projects now that the financial year has ended. Instead, the £51.2 million will be used to offset future FTC repayments to the Treasury. The Executive is required to repay 80% of FTC allocations received from the Treasury. The Department of Finance has an agreement in place that allows FTC underspends to be used to reduce these repayments. As a result, this funding will not be lost but will instead help to reduce future liabilities. 	I, and my Department, stand ready to play a full and positive role in the next steps to get Casement built. I have written to the Communities Minister seeking a meeting to discuss the delivery of Casement Park.	The Communities Minister’s bids in 2025-26 for both Casement Park and Sub Regional Stadia were both met in full.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what metrics will be used to measure the success of the upcoming phone-free pilot in schools.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of commissioning an independent evaluation of the phone-free pilot. This evaluation will aim to assess the practical aspects of this approach, as well as the impact of restricting mobile phone use during school hours on areas including pupils’ engagement with learning, participation in school life, behaviour, and overall wellbeing. It will provide valuable insights into the practicalities, challenges, and benefits of this intervention, as well as providing an evidence base that will help to inform my future policy decisions in this area. The evaluation will be informed by the experience of pupils, parents / carers, and education professionals within the nine schools participating in the pilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has had any contact with Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council in relation to the Carryduff Greenaway project.
answer_text: Delivery of greenways are generally council led and in providing support, my officials regularly engage with all councils on their greenway priorities. I am aware that Carryduff Greenway is currently being considered by Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council and my officials will continue to engage with the council as they progress this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether his Department has carried out any engagement with charities that did not receive Core Grant Funding last year regarding the upcoming Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26 and the application process.
answer_text: The Core Grant Scheme was reviewed by my officials and redesigned in partnership with the Community and Voluntary Sector in a process facilitated by the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action in the months leading up to the launch of the 2025/26 Scheme in November 2024.This redesign process was open to all community and voluntary sector organisations and included several engagement events which community and voluntary organisations attended and participated in, leading to a number of agreed “key elements” for the scheme being proposed and agreed by me.The application form and guidance documents which provided the details of the application process were publicly available on the DoH website when the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025/26 was launched in November 2024.Following the finalisation of the assessment process, all applicants have been offered detailed feedback on their application, and I hope that this will assist them in applying to future funding rounds. My officials have been offered general feedback from the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action in relation to the application and assessment process for 2025/26, to inform future Scheme decisions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the Deputy First Minister’s attendance at the Royal Box at Wimbledon on 2 July 2025 was in her official capacity as a Minister.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister attended in her official capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the estimated collective value of all properties owned by the Northern Ireland Civil Service that are currently vacant or unused.
answer_text: There are currently 14 vacant or unused properties owned by the Department of Finance. This figure includes 4 vacant office properties that are for sale (Clarence Court, Victoria Hall, 34 College Street and Fermanagh House). There are 5 further vacant office properties of which 3 are on the Stormont Estate (Dundonald House, Stormont House and Massey House), one is in Limavady (86 Main Street) and one is in Derry (Waterside House) and options for these will be progressed in line with the Department’s published Office Estate Strategy.The Department also owns 5 smaller vacant “non office” properties of which 4 are on the Stormont Estate (Courier HQ, Villa 1, Villa 2 and Stoney Road Bungalow) and one at Ballymena County Hall (Groundsman’s House).The collective current value of these 14 properties as recorded on the Department’s Asset Register as of 31 March 2025 is £30.9m

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any mitigations his Department is pursuing to safeguard those living with disabilities, in light of the recent vote in the House of Commons to reform Personal Independence Payments and Universal Credit.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the concern and uncertainty that the recent proposals in the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill have caused, particularly the reforms to Personal Independence Payment. I am pleased that the UK Government has now made a significant number of concessions to the Bill and the most punitive proposals in relation to Personal Independence Payment have been dropped. Universal Credit rates will be adjusted to make sure all existing recipients of the Universal Credit health element – and any new claimant meeting the severe conditions criteria and those who qualify under Special Rules for End of Life – have the combined rate of their standard allowance and health element protected in real terms. The revised Bill will therefore offer clearer protections to those with long-term disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what legal advice she received on potential grounds for appeal of the High Court ruling on the incapability of the planned A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I am and will continue to be guided by legal counsel on the potential grounds for appeal. All the legal advice which they have provided and will continue to provide is subject to professional legal privilege and therefore will not be published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will be engaging with the upcoming UK Government's review into the parental leave and pay system.
answer_text: The Department of Work and Pensions and the Department of Business and Trade (DBT) have jointly launched a review of the parental leave and pay system in Britain. This review is expected to run for an 18-month period and includes a 2-month call for evidence which is now live. Regular engagement at Ministerial and official level takes place with our DBT counterparts to discuss policy and legislation proposals. This engagement will continue throughout the life cycle of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children on the waiting list for a Statement of Special Educational Needs at the end of the academic year (i) 2020-2021; (ii) 2021-2022; (iii) 2022-2023; (iv) 2023-2024; and (v) 2024-2025.
answer_text: The statutory assessment process does not have a waiting list for a statement of special educational needs (SEN).  Not all children who undergo statutory assessment will require a statement of SEN.  The Education Authority has provided the following information on the total number of open cases at the end of the academic years requested:	Academic YearMonth / YearTotal Open Cases2020-2021August 202122012021-2022August 202225152022-2023August 202327042023-2024August 202426182024-2025June 20252679

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a timeline for providing the terms of reference for the review of higher education funding.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with the sector over the past number of months regarding the review and I am currently considering the draft Terms of Reference. When finalised, these will be provided to relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 27458/22-27 and AQW 27564/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 27458/22-27 was answered on 18/07/2025 and AQW 27564/22-27 was answered on 08/07/2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the specific ministerial (i) actions; or (ii) decisions that her Department has taken in relation to the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: 17/7/24 - approved the publication of the AISRR31/7/24 – published final report and supporting documentation as part of the Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) process21/3/25 noted the progress on EIB work including the draft Project Prioritisation StrategyAs highlighted in AQW 27762 22-27, the Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank (EIB) Advisory Services to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations. With the assistance of EIB consultants, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority to consider the Review’s recommendations. This work has included the preparation of a Project Prioritisation Strategy, which identifies those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035. The EIB consultant team are currently focused on conducting a more detailed analysis of key major projects. Following completion of this work, the draft Project Prioritisation Strategy, which has been broadly agreed by the project Steering Group, will be reviewed and published later this year.  My officials are also working closely with Translink who are undertaking several feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report in the coming months. These include studies on reopening the Lisburn-Antrim line, with a link to Belfast International Airport; reinstating the Portadown-Armagh line; a new line from Portadown to Derry; and electrification of Belfast to the border.The outcomes from these studies and the work with our southern colleagues will help inform my prioritisation of projects to progress as funding opportunities arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to reduce Health and Social Care Trusts’ dependency on private ambulances.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) currently utilises Independent Ambulance Services (IAS) to support the delivery of routine, non-emergency Patient Care Services (PCS). These services are used to supplement NIAS’s own PCS crews. Health and Social Care Trusts also use the Independent Ambulance Sector to support non-emergency transport of patients from care settings to aid timely discharge.In addition, the independent sector supports NIAS emergency response in three ways:Relief at Emergency DepartmentsConveyance of lower acuity emergency patients to hospital, and Planned transfer of patients from one location to another.NIAS has developed an extensive internal process of monitoring, reporting, governance and assurance in relation to the use of private ambulances through a structured assurance framework. This includes twice-yearly unannounced inspections of provider vehicles and quarterly governance meetings with all IAS contractors. These meetings include both plenary learning sessions and individual provider reviews. There are currently 15 IAS contractors on the NIAS Framework, accessible to NIAS and other Health and Social Care Trusts. In response to recommendation three of the Northern Ireland Audit office (NIAO) report on Ambulance Handovers, published in March 2025, the NIAS is in the process of conducting a cost-benefit analysis comparing the private ambulance sector with in-house capacity.While NIAS has enhanced its oversight role following the recommendation by NIAO, it will also be important to explore stronger regulation of this sector. This is also an emerging theme from early work on the review of the Health and Social Care’s (HSC) Non-emergency Transport Strategy, which I initiated earlier this year.  While private ambulance regulation is not directly related to the review, I anticipate that this will be progressed by the policy team as soon as possible, subject to available resources. NIAS are working with my Department to carry out a review of the current service delivery model which will also provide a ten-year workforce plan, setting out the direction of travel for the organisation. In support of this work, my Department has commenced a paramedic workforce review which will be completed in the next 12-18 months. My Department has provided funding to NIAS to recruit the first cohort of graduates from the degree programme, with recruitment of the second cohort currently progressing. In advance of the paramedic workforce review, my Department has this year increased the number of places on the degree programme from 50 to 65, thereby increasing the available workforce into the future. By increasing the number of locally trained paramedics, the long-term aim is to grow the NIAS workforce and therefore reduce reliance on the Independent Sector into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what actions her Department is taking to improve the Metro 7 service travel corridor, including outlining the number of meetings she has held with (i) Translink; and (ii) residents.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink has engaged with elected representatives from many of the political parties and proactively organised a Public Information Drop-in session on 24th June at Saintfield Rd Presbyterian Church Hall, attended by local residents, customers and Councillors from a number of political parties. Translink has focused on service performance on the 7 corridor and has put in place arrangements to improve service performance and significant improvements in reliability and punctuality performance have been seen on the corridor throughout 2025 to date. Translink has and will continue to review timetable performance, resource deployment and monitor customer sentiment through independent monitoring and use this as the basis for continually improving performance. Given current financial funding challenges facing Translink at this time, enhanced service frequencies will not be deliverable in this financial year. The corridor, along with other South Belfast services, was particularly impacted by city centre road schemes and traffic congestion during the latter half of last year (2024). Translink continues to work in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure Roads team to mitigate the delays due to traffic congestion, particularly in the Autumn period focusing on extending and enhancing bus priority measures, enforcement of bus lanes and on street parking and planning around road traffic schemes which may give rise to disruption. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any schools in South Belfast were considered for inclusion in the latest tranche of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for InfrastructureThe programme of schools to be treated this financial year was developed, based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Our approach to selecting schools is outlined in our interim policy document DEM 194/25 ‘Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools – Tranche 3 2025/26’ which is publicly available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools-tranche3-2025-26-dem-19425.pdf Schools in the South Belfast area were considered but did not score as highly as other locations and were not included in this tranche.  However, these schools will be considered in any future extensions of the programme.I have also asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail why there was a 13-month period between the Assembly supporting a motion calling for the legalisation of no-fault divorces on 20 May 2024, and the launch of the Consultation on Divorce and the Dissolution of Civil Partnerships on 23 June 2025.
answer_text: During the Assembly debate in May 2024, my predecessor, Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA, advised the Assembly that the Department had limited resources in this area and that these were already largely committed. Nonetheless, she was keen for work on the law of divorce and dissolution to progress during the present mandate. Since taking over as Finance Minister earlier in the year, I have been similarly committed to taking forward work in this area. Following the Assembly debate, in summer 2024, my officials reviewed the relevant legislation in other jurisdictions and, towards the end of the year, held a series of initial meetings with relevant local stakeholders to obtain their views on the existing arrangements and on options for change.  Only following these essential preparatory stages could a consultation document be drafted and an online consultation survey developed for launch on 23 June. Consultation will continue until 26 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the coverage of the HPV vaccination, broken down by constituency.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what steps she has taken to help alleviate the (i) volume of traffic; and (ii) size of vehicles, on Greystown Park, Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resource, traffic calming schemes are taken forward through the prioritisation of those roads deemed most in need for intervention in line with the Department’s policy.I can advise that officials have recently carried out two traffic calming assessments on Greystown Park Belfast, with average speeds of 23 and 26 mph being recorded, and an average traffic volume of 28 vehicle per hour. No HGV movements were noted during the surveys. As such Greystown Park would not have sufficient priority to be considered for traffic calming measures at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the number of officials working on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, calculated on a full-time equivalent basis.
answer_text: The Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank (EIB) Advisory Services to appoint a consultant team to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations. My officials sit on a Steering Group with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority, to review and oversee the work of the consultant team. My Department has four staff at various grades working on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review alongside other duties.  The time spent on the Rail Review for these staff when combined currently equates to 0.3 FTE. This includes attending Steering Group meetings, reviewing consultant outputs, managing correspondence regarding the Rail Review, and attending other related meetings, including with rail stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the actions her Department has taken to support individuals who work on Botanic Avenue, Belfast, who are facing issues with parking.
answer_text: My Department is committed to providing safe, attractive travel alternatives to the private car.  As part of the implementation of the Belfast Cycling Network, proposals are being developed for active travel improvements between Queen's University and the City Centre via Botanic Ave and Dublin Road.  Public engagement on the preliminary proposals is scheduled for later this year.More widely, the Department is currently preparing the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP) which will set out proposed changes to the transport network in Belfast. The road network in Belfast is close to or over capacity at peak times and with limited options to expand it. The ETP proposals will aim to rebalance the transport network in favour of sustainable modes such as public transport and active travel, before this issue becomes a constraint to future growth in the city. Testing of the ETP proposals for Belfast is underway with public engagement planned for Autumn 2025. I can advise that officials are not aware of any recent issues associated with parking on Botanic Avenue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a response to the Northern Ireland Anti-Poverty Network’s critique of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The Anti-Poverty Strategy is the culmination of hard work and collaboration from a broad range of stakeholders across government and representative groups.I have listened carefully to the recommendations made, many of which have been included and actioned in the Anti-Poverty Strategy.The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy is currently undergoing public consultation until 19 September, providing opportunities for a wide range of organisations to engage with and provide comment on the Strategy. I would encourage all organisations and individual with an interest or view on poverty to engage with the consultation on the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what steps she will take to encourage more people from a Catholic background to join the PSNI, after Catholic recruitment to the PSNI fell to a record low of 17.1 per cent in 2024.
answer_text: There is an appropriate legislative framework in place to encourage applications from all under-represented groups and I have been proactive in calling for a fully representative police service and encouraging recruits from under-represented groups, including during my time as Minister.  Clearly the security threat faced by officers is a deterrent to many and the sacrifices which are often required by those who come from a Catholic or Nationalist background remain a significant barrier to recruitment and retention. It is, therefore, incumbent upon us all, as elected representatives, to encourage all members of our community to consider joining the PSNI and to help to create the societal conditions where that is most likely to occur. Regrettably, rather than supporting that effort, some Members have recently used their public platforms to disparage the work of the PSNI especially in relation to those from a perceived Catholic or Nationalist background. Such statements only serve to deter consideration of a career in policing and are contrary to the findings of recent surveys which show that the PSNI is widely respected and accepted across the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps her Department will take to prevent further criminal damage against 5G masts.
answer_text: I condemn the wanton destruction of this essential communications infrastructure; it is despicable and has serious consequences for local communities. I am aware that the mast attacks are under investigation by the PSNI, and I would urge anyone with any information to pass it on to the police. I understand mobile industry representatives are liaising with the PSNI to help improve security around this essential infrastructure. I am scheduled to meet with mobile industry representatives later this month, the mast attacks will be discussed. My officials are leading on a number of initiatives to help improve mobile connectivity here. These include the Mobile Action Plan (MAP) and the Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT). This work includes delivery of a programme of information regarding mobile technologies, part of which is work at addressing perceived public health concerns, which may be motivating attacks on masts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any plans to reform Northern Ireland's hate crime legislation, following the recent hate crime in Ballymena against the Mid and East Antrim Pride parade.
answer_text: I condemn the spreading of slurry on the streets of Ballymena ahead of the Mid and East Antrim Pride parade, and act which the Police Service of Northern Ireland are treating as a hate crime.  There is no place in our society for hatred and bigotry.  I am encouraged by the response of the local community who came together along with the council and other volunteers to assist in cleaning the streets to ensure the parade could take place unhindered.As you will be aware, it was not possible to introduce any legislation or progress key policy decisions in the absence of Ministers, the Executive and NI Assembly during a significant proportion of the early part of this mandate.  I, therefore, had to review and re-prioritise my previous, more ambitious legislative plans upon my return as Minister of Justice at the beginning of last year.Officials are currently progressing work on two Bills which will include hate crime provisions:The Sentencing Bill, planned for introduction in Autumn 2025, which will include a statutory aggravation model for hate crimes whereby all existing crimes could be aggravated by hostility if evidence shows the crime is motivated by hostility or hostility is demonstrated. The statutory aggravator model will remain the foundation for all other future provisions.A Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill, planned for introduction in early 2026, which will include provisions of automatic eligibility for consideration of special measures and the prohibition of in-person cross-examination in cases involving hate crime.In the limited time available in this mandate, I believe that these are the most impactful provisions from Judge Marrinan’s Review which can be advanced in this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the findings of the Summary Position Paper joint document, commissioned by the Northern Ireland Chamber of Commerce and Industry, the Construction Employers Federation and the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations, into the current state of NI Water.
answer_text: As I have advised previously, I will listen to all advice and solutions for addressing the historic funding deficit in our water and wastewater infrastructure. In this sense, I thank the NI Chamber, Construction Employers Federation and the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations for commissioning this report and providing the additional analysis it provides in this regard.While the report does indeed introduce solutions, the fundamental conclusion is the proposition of an increase to the household rates bills to secure additional revenue for NI Water. This means the introduction of a separate charge specifically for water onto household rates bills, and potentially also an increase to business rates. My position remains in that I will not impose further financial burden on families and households in the midst of a cost of living crisis. I am however, making significant progress with my department’s three pronged approach in securing Executive support for additional funding for wastewater infrastructure to unlock sewerage capacity for housing and in working collaboratively with NI Water to progress innovative solutions to unlocking long-standing wastewater infrastructure constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how many children are still awaiting a school place for the academic year 2025-26.
answer_text: As at 1 July 2025 there were 38 children who had applied through the competitive admissions processes for September 2025 admission and who are awaiting a school place, covering pre-school, primary and post-primary admission and transfer to senior schools within the Dickson Plan area.  This figure includes children who have been offered a place but whose parents have not yet formally accepted it.As at the same date there were 103 children with a statement of special educational needs awaiting a school place for the academic year 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps she will take, following the announcement by NI Composite Economic Index, that the Northern Ireland economy contracted by 0.6 per cent over the quarter to March 2025.
answer_text: While the latest NI Composite Economic Index does indicate a decrease in economic output in the first quarter of 2025, it is important to recognise that these changes can be largely driven by external factors outside of our control. Moreover, longer-term data from the Index, which is generally more useful for assessing material changes and trends, suggest a more positive position. For example, the North had stronger growth over the year to March 2025 than Britain (1.6% vs 1.3%) and over the rolling annual average period (2.7% vs 1.3%). My officials will continue to monitor the NI Composite Economic Index and other key economic metrics, and I will continue to pursue my four key economic priorities of creating more good jobs, raising productivity, improving regional balance and and reducing carbon emissions, including through the actions outlined in my Department’s 2025/26 Business Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will publish any legal advice it received on the planned A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme's compliance with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 prior to the recent High Court ruling.
answer_text: The Department has been guided by senior and junior counsel over the course of the public inquiries held in 2020 and 2023 and throughout development and drafting of the Departmental Statement. All the legal advice which they provided is subject to professional legal privilege and therefore will not be published.Recommendations 14 -17 of the Departmental Statement detail my Departments approach to the Decision whilst the first Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan (CAP), was being developed.  Departmental Statement 2024 (v3.0) | A5 Western Transport Corridor Hub Site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many families have been displaced from their homes due to the recent racially-motivated disorder.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “The Housing Executive has assisted households who have had to leave their homes as a result of civil disorder across a number of areas including in counties Antrim, Londonderry and Belfast.  The Housing Executive is aware that other households who were affected have made their own arrangements and have not engaged with the organisation.The Housing Executive has had contact with 60 households and has provided temporary accommodation for 18 households in order to offer emergency shelter and safety over this period.’’For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans her Department has to reduce the 50mph speed limit on Purdysburn Road, Belfast.
answer_text: The road markings on Purdysburn Road have recently been reconfigured and pedestrian islands enhanced during a resurfacing scheme to encourage drivers to lower their speed.In light of these changes to the characteristics of Purdysburn Road, I have asked my officials to carry out a speed limit assessment for this location in line with Departmental policy, RSPPG_E051 Setting Speed Limits in Northern Ireland. The assessment, which will involve consultation with PSNI Road Policing Unit, will be carried out as soon as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is exploring any changes to Carer's Allowance, to allow greater flexibilities for carers to pursue work and study.
answer_text: The Department is not currently exploring any changes to the eligibility rules in Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what provisions Land and Property Service have to direct unpaid non-domestic rates bills from a now closed office to a new business set up by the previous office’s owner.
answer_text: When a ratepayer moves from one property to another, Land & Property Services (LPS) will update the rate account to reflect the change in liability. As part of this process, LPS collects relevant information from both the outgoing and incoming ratepayers.If a ratepayer leaves a property with an outstanding balance, LPS will issue bills to their new address. If the new address is not immediately available, LPS undertake investigations to locate the individual or company. These efforts can include:Contacting solicitors involved in the property transferReaching out to estate agents or letting agents who managed the sale or rentalChecking third-party sources such as the electoral register or Companies HouseConducting physical visits to the property and performing desk-based searchesThese steps help LPS identify a new address to ensure that any outstanding rate liabilities are appropriately billed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when an updated list of Ministerial Directions will be published.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance (DoF) arranges for updates to the list of Ministerial Directions on the DoF website following scrutiny of relevant information from Departments by the Comptroller and Auditor General, and within one week following discussion at the Public Accounts Committee.  The list of Ministerial Directions which is currently available on the DoF website reflects the up-to-date position in line with DoF’s guidance on Publication of Ministerial Directions issued under cover of DAO (DoF) 01/22.Ministerial Directions All Departments 2007 - Date

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on funding available for a 24/7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: It is estimated that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand the current thrombectomy service to a 24/7 service. My officials are currently progressing a business case to refine this estimate which is expected to complete shortly. While I would like to be in a position to commit to allocating the additional funding required to expand the thrombectomy service to 24/7, the current financial challenges imposed on my Department mean that I am unable to do so. I am therefore unable to commit to a timescale for the implementation of 24/7 thrombectomy at this time. I will however continue to keep this position under review in the context of competing priorities and future budgets made available to my Department.In the interim I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2023/24 was 5.9%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support her Department provides to students from Northern Ireland, studying in the United States of America, who have had their study visas revoked or otherwise impacted by recent changes in immigration policy by the US Government.
answer_text: I am aware of the impact of visa application restrictions, imposed by the US Government, on students.The British Council, which administers the Study USA scheme on behalf of my Department, have maintained regular contact with the US Consulate in Belfast, provided advice and support to participants and provided regular updates to the Department and local higher education institutions. While some participants are still awaiting visas, none have been revoked. The Department has not been approached by any students, outside of the Study USA scheme, who have been impacted by this matter.
