EPPOC is an ambitious, multi-disciplinary and transformational change programme working to deliver the NI Executive’s priority of achieving safer communities, resilient to paramilitarism, criminality and coercive control.  Through a mix of innovative local expertise and internationally recognised methods, the Programme helps people at risk of paramilitary exploitation through early, preventative interventions, as well as providing bespoke support and signposting for people already suffering harm. The Programme operates throughout Northern Ireland and projects vary in scale, focus, location, target group and methodology depending on assessed need. Projects must offer some form of ‘additionality’.  In September 2024, the Executive agreed to extend the Programme for a further two years, to March 2027. This decision recognises that addressing paramilitarism is a long-term endeavour and allows for a continued focus on tackling the issues, ensuring that individual Departments have time to absorb and capture good practice and learning. It also allows for the embedding and scaling of existing successful/impactful projects and interventions.   At its core, through its interventions and strategic enablers, the Programme has adopted and operationalised a ‘public health’ approach to violence reduction – the first of its kind in Northern Ireland. So far, the emerging Programme data and evidence gathered confirms the findings of other studies that point to significant levels of trauma in communities where paramilitary activity is prevalent, and among those who are receiving or seeking support from the Programme. For that reason, the Programme is seeking to be trauma informed in all that it does; this means giving due consideration to the impact that trauma has on victims of paramilitarism, as well as those who help and support them.  In agreeing to the two-year extension of EPPOC the Executive also committed to a parallel focus within the Programme on scoping the operationalisation of a public health approach to violence and harm reduction (VHR). This would enable a more long-term basis for addressing multiple types of violence and harm (including paramilitarism) beyond 2027. This approach should put early intervention, prevention, and sustainability at its core, and has informed the content of the Programme for Government which highlights that the Executive remains committed to tackling paramilitarism and organised crime and will continue to support the cross-Executive efforts to address them, building on the work that has been done to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of active travel projects in the South Belfast constituency were (i) submitted; and (ii) considered for funding in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Monitoring of active travel projects is not broken down by parliamentary constituency, my department is structured to broadly align to the council boundaries. The South Belfast constituency falls within the Belfast City Council and works in this area are undertaken by my Department’s Eastern divisionThe projects being delivered by the Department through direct active travel funding are detailed below:Belfast Cycling Network (BCN)Route NoDetails4Comber Greenway Lighting – ongoing ecological monitoring23Active travel contribution to the Ballysillan project29Active travel contribution to Belfast Grand Central Station cycling enhancements3Lagmore Avenue 56Stranmillis Embankment – enhancement of existing networkVariousDesign of short term BCN schemesEastern Division DetailsChurch Road, Castlereagh- New footwayUpper Springfield Road, Belfast (Hannahstown Hill to Glenmeen Close) – New footwayVarious dropped kerb schemesVarious controlled crossings Active travel contribution to McKinstry Road / The Cutts projectExpenditure is determined by budget availability and capacity in any given year.In addition to direct divisional delivery, Belfast City Council submitted three requests for funding in the current financial year through The Greenways and Active Travel Enabling Projects capital grant funding round to councils.  The Department will be writing to the Belfast City Council shortly to advise on the outcome of these requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22667/22-27, to detail the environmental impact assessment that her Department carried out before removing the street lighting on Newforge Lane; and (ii) whether a risk assessment was carried out before the lighting was removed to give consideration to the potential impact on public safety.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the provision and maintenance of lighting on adopted public roads and footways.As Newforge Lane is only partially adopted, in line with existing policy, lighting on the unadopted portions of the road, were removed and will not be replaced. This policy helps ensure that replacement lighting is provided where it is most needed, reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.My Department has no legal responsibility to provide lighting along the unadopted sections of Newforge Lane and as such there was no requirement to undertake an environmental impact or risk assessment before its removal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in line with the recommendations of the Programme for Government 2024-2027, whether there will be ring-fenced funding included in the 2025-26 Budget for (i) the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Regional Change Fund; and (ii) the challenge fund to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Executive agreed a ring-fenced allocation of £2 million for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls in draft Budget 2025-26, to The Executive Office (TEO) towards the implementation of Phase1 of their delivery plan.In addition to this earmarked allocation TEO has set aside a further £1.2 million from its baseline to fund an Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Change Fund for community and voluntary sector organisations and a partnership with local councils. As part of this wider Change fund, a Regional Change Fund initiative was announced on 21 January 2025 by the First Minister and deputy First Minister, aimed at tackling violence against women and girls.TEO intend to use the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) Challenge Fund to find innovative data and tech innovations to address violence against women and girls. They will be developing a business case to support the drawn down of SBRI Challenge funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in line with the recommendations of the Programme for Government 2024-2027, whether there will be ring-fenced funding included in the 2025-26 Budget for the redevelopment of Casement Park.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park is an Executive Flagship project, with £62.5 million previously having been committed for this. The Department of Communities bid for £28 million for the incoming financial year to enable work to progress. This bid was met in full by the Executive and is included in the draft Budget 2025-26.I, and my department, will provide all support possible and I look forward to seeing the completion of the redevelopment of Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the effect that high parking prices at public transport facilities being used as a park and ride has on the efforts to encourage behavioural shifts towards increasing public transport use and promoting sustainability through park and ride facilities as outlined in her recent Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 statement.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:All of Translink’s dedicated Park and Ride sites offer free parking apart from Lanyon Place Station. Since the relocation of the Enterprise Service to Belfast Grand Central station, the Lanyon Place is no longer viewed as a typical Park and Ride facility with parking charges that are comparable with other city centre carparks.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail when the cross-governmental strategy on reducing reoffending will be published.
answer_text: I am pleased that the cross-governmental strategy features in the new Programme for Government.  My officials remain at the early stages of scoping the strategy and are due to meet with senior management teams across key Departments shortly, to inform further Executive engagement and early strategy development.As you will appreciate, there is a balance in developing and delivering evidence based, informed policy at pace.  Given the level of cross-governmental and wider engagement required I anticipate that by the end of this mandate, we will have an agreed Executive approach to offending and reoffending, and publication thereafter.  In the interim work progresses under other key strategies and projects to address offending and reoffending.  As the Programme for Government indicates, and as with other cross-cutting priorities, the buy in and commitment from Executive colleagues will be critical to success and delivering better outcomes and ultimately safer communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 21044/22-27, for her assessment of the efficacy of her Department’s public service agreement with Translink to incentivise park and ride facilities.
answer_text: The PSA is designed to secure the provision of accessible, efficient, safe, sustainable and high quality public transport services that contribute to Programme for Government outcomes and support wider policy objectives. The current PSA does not specifically include the provision of Park and Ride facilities which are managed by Translink on a commercial basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the date on which she signed off the text of her Department’s contribution to the 2025-26 Draft Budget document.
answer_text: My Department made two separate contributions to the 2025-26 Draft Budget document in line with the requests of the Department of Finance for:1. ‘High level information on the work of the department’; and 2. ‘Draft Budget outcome’ as follows:I approved the text on pages 63-64 of the document which relates to ‘High level information on the work of the department ‘on 14 November 2024.I approved the text on pages 64-74 of the document which relates to ‘Draft Budget outcome’ on 8 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5972/22-27, to detail any further work undertaken to improve the appearance of Finaghy bridge.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with Belfast City Council to develop proposals to improve the appearance of the bridge, without compromising the safety of road and rail users.Officials have agreed to assist with any necessary legislative processes, lane closures, utility coordination and the issue of any required consent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the date on which the 2025-26 Draft Budget document was signed off and sent to be printed.
answer_text: ANSWERThe draft Budget 2025-26 was agreed by the Executive on 19 December 2024. On the same day a Written Ministerial Statement, Budget Factsheet, Budget Overview and Equality Impact Assessment were published online on the Department of Finance website.Following the receipt of information from departments, the 2025-26 draft Budget document was published online on 31 January 2025 and sent to be printed on 6 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in line with the recommendations within the Programme for Government 2024-2027, when the membership of the public-sector transformation board will be confirmed.
answer_text: ANSWERWhile the Programme for Government 2024-27 highlights how the Transformation Fund will deliver important improvements in public service delivery, it does not contain any recommendations in relation to the Public Sector Transformation Board (the Board).Nevertheless, this is a matter I am urgently progressing. Following my appointment as Finance Minister, I met with the Secretary of State and finalised the terms of reference for the interim Board on 18 February 2025. This enabled me to announce £129m of funding for six successful transformation projects on 4 March 2025. The terms of reference and membership for the final Board will build upon those agreed last month. As I stated in the Assembly on 4 March 2025, I intend to supplement the current Board with two additional individuals, one with expertise in data and science, and one with expertise in value for money. I will bring my recommendations on this and the final terms of reference to the Executive as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the current adult and youth reoffending rates are higher or lower than the rates in 2010.
answer_text: In the 2010/11 financial year, 30,576 adults were released from custody or given a non-custodial disposal. Of these individuals, 4,875 (15.9%) committed a proven reoffence. During the same time period, 3,248 youths were released from custody or given a non-custodial disposal, with 772 (23.8%) reoffending.For the most recent cohort (2021/22), 18,335 adults were released from custody or given a non-custodial disposal, with 3,184 (17.4%) reoffending. Additionally, 861 youths were included in the 2021/22 cohort, with 202 (23.5%) committing a proven reoffence. From 2010/11 to 2021/22, the adult reoffending rate has increased by 1.5 percentage points, with the annual adult cohort and number of adults reoffending decreasing by 40.0% and 34.7% respectively.During this time, the youth reoffending rate has decreased by 0.3 percentage points, with the annual youth cohort and number of youths reoffending also decreasing by 73.5% and 73.8% respectively.Note:Reoffending is measured on a financial year basis. The data for each cohort relates to the offending behaviours of all those who have been given a non-custodial disposal or who have been released from custody between 1st April and 31st March within the given financial year.Figures provide information on the one-year proven reoffending rate; this looks at whether an individual has reoffended within one year of release from custody, with an additional six month period given to allow the offence(s) to be proven in court. An adult is defined as someone aged 18 or over at the time of release from custody or receipt of non-custodial disposal, while youths are defined as anyone aged 17 or under at this point. A reoffence is counted if it (i) occurs within the one year observation period, (ii) has been committed within Northern Ireland, (iii) is prosecuted via PSNI and not a third party, (iv) is not a breach offence, and (v) has been proven in court within 18 months of entry to the cohort.The one-year observation period for some individuals within the 2021/22 cohort overlaps with national Covid-19 lockdown restrictions; this should be taken into consideration when interpreting findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with the PSNI in relation to (i) the positive impact of alleygating in designing-out antisocial behaviour; and (ii) the potential for further funding for Belfast City Council to install more gates.
answer_text: My Department regularly engages with the PSNI in relation to anti-social behaviour and officials work with colleagues from a wide range of statutory bodies and relevant stakeholders to identify measures that can be taken to address the issue.The Department fully recognises the negative impact ASB has on communities and, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides funding of £4.25 million to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level, including ASB. PCSPs liaise with the local community and other key partners, including statutory bodies /agencies and the voluntary sector to develop appropriate actions to tackle and reduce the impact of ASB.In respect of alleygating, it is one of a number of measures that has been used to design out anti-social behaviour. You will be aware that primary responsibility for alleygating lies with local councils, with support from the Departments for Infrastructure and Communities, who may in some cases have landowner responsibility. My Department has no specific funding provision for alleyway gates and given the ongoing priorities within the remit of the Department of Justice, and the extraordinary constraints on our budget, that position is unlikely to change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why the cost of reduced long-haul Air Passenger Duty is no longer treated as a centrally held item.
answer_text: ANSWERThe cost long-haul Air Passenger Duty was not treated as a centrally held item in the draft Budget 2025-26 as it had already been adjusted for in the Spending Review outcome for 2025-26 notified by the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in line with the Programme for Government 2024-2027, whether the Tackling Paramilitarism Programme has been extended to March 2027.
answer_text: The Executive has agreed to extend the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime for an additional two years to March 2027.  This aligns with the commitment set out under the Safer Communities Priority in the Programme for Government.  At present, UK Government match funding has not yet been confirmed for 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of her decision to not extend the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 to Carryduff, to detail any actions her Department will take to increase bus service provision.
answer_text: On 25 February 2025 I made an announcement on the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 Project, which will see the Glider service extended to north and south Belfast.  However, the timetabling of services is primarily an operational matter for Translink.My announcement provided detail on what my Department could deliver with the £35m funding made available to us from our Belfast Region City Deal partners.  This included additional bus priority measures on the Southway route to Park Road.  The termination point on the Southway route will be at the Cairnshill Park and Ride.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in line with the Programme for Government 2024-2027 and New Decade, New Approach, whether she intends to expand the Ulster University’s Magee campus to accommodate 10,000 students by the end of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I remain committed to the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus in line with the findings in the Ulster University Magee Expansion Taskforce final report and action plan, launched December 2024, which outlined the necessary steps and timescales required to expand to 10,000 students by 2032.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether businesses affected by racist attacks in August 2024 will be encouraged to avail of the recent £200,000 funding announcement on the same terms as the other businesses.
answer_text: My department is providing funding to Belfast City Council for the Sandy Row Revitalisation scheme.  This scheme will be administered and delivered by Belfast City Council including advertising and promoting the scheme.  Funding will be allocated following an application process and assessment against criteria set by the Council.  All applications will be assessed in line with the set criteria.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Executive discussions on the Programme for Government have taken place (i) since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; and (ii) since the publication of the draft Programme for Government in September 2024.
answer_text: The content of Executive papers and all aspects of Executive business are regarded as confidential, and Ministers should at all times observe the need for confidentiality in relation to the conduct of Executive business and the proceedings of its meetings, other than by means of any agreed statement which may be issued following a meeting.  Ministers should ensure that the necessary steps are taken to protect the detailed content of papers and Executive minutes which are to be, or have been, considered by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of meetings she or her predecessors have had on issues facing frontier workers on the island of Ireland during the 2022-2027 mandate; and (ii) whether any proposals are being produced in relation to this issue.
answer_text: On 31 March, I met with the research team of the Labour Employment and Economic Forum Shared Island Working Group which published a study on cross-border workers in November 2024. The report raises a number of issues that need to be resolved between the British and Irish Governments in order to improve cross-border working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the Sexual Orientation Strategy during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The Minister for Communities is the lead Minister for development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy.  There has been no request to date from the Minister for Communities for my engagement around development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy and the Ministerial Steering Group has not met since restoration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the earliest date for the full implementation of Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2.
answer_text: The Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project requires a significant investment of some £142-£148m for full scheme delivery.Utilising the £35m from Belfast Region City Deal, alongside an additional £13m of departmental funding, I hope to deliver the first phase of these works, as outlined in my Assembly statement of 25th February 2025, in the 2027/28 financial year.The remainder of the scheme could be delivered by 2030, if the necessary funding to address the £100m shortfall was addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total (i) Capital DEL; and (ii) Resource DEL provided to the Executive budget in each of the last ten years.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools in the South Belfast constituency on his Department’s list for Major Capital Works Projects; and (ii) how long each school has been on the list.
answer_text: The following schools in the South Belfast constituency are currently on the Department’s Major Capital Works programme. The date in brackets indicates when the schools were announced under the Programme.  Breda Academy (June 2014)Scoil an Droichid (June 2014)Hunterhouse College (March 2022)Malone Integrated College (March 2022)St Joseph’s College (March 2022)Forge Integrated Primary School (former Fresh Start Project transferred to the Major Capital Works programme in February 2024)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether businesses affected by racist attacks in August 2024 can avail of the recent £200,000 funding announcement on the same terms as the other businesses.
answer_text: My department is providing funding to Belfast City Council for the Sandy Row Revitalisation scheme.  This scheme will be administered and delivered by Belfast City Council with funding allocated following an application and assessment against criteria set by the Council.  All applications will be assessed in line with the set criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he was first aware of the requirement to bring SR 2025/11 The Animals (Identification, Records, Movement and Enforcement) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 before the Assembly.
answer_text: The Department has been working, from before the restoration of the Assembly, on legislation to implement livestock identification, registration and movement (IRM) requirements contained within European Regulation (EU) 2016/429, commonly referred to as the “Animal Health Law (AHL)”.  Our original intention was to make and lay a wide-ranging Statutory Rule which would, amongst other provisions, have restored the legal certainty to the Department’s enforcement actions for IRM offences.I was first advised that domestic IRM legislation was considered inoperable on 7 June 2024 and that the wide-ranging Statutory Rule, which would fully implement AHL, was expected to be ready for laying in the autumn.In October 2024, it became clear that, due to the complexity of the drafting needed, it would take further time to complete this work.  This was the first time such legislation was being progressed in the absence of complete support of Whitehall.I therefore decided to progress interim regulations, amending existing domestic legislation, to ensure my Department has an operable statute book, while work continues on the wider, replacement legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget her Department bid for to fund the implementation of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, supported by the European Investment Bank (EIB), to consider the recommendations in the Review and identify those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035.  The outcomes from the ongoing EIB work will help inform which projects to prioritise. As this work is not yet complete, the Department has not submitted a bid for funding for the implementation of the recommendations in the Review in this year’s budget.  Given the scale of the interventions and the magnitude of transformation involved, delivering the Review’s recommendations will be challenging and the Department will need to explore all avenues of funding. This will include seeking support from Executive colleagues to fund interventions, given their importance in terms of economic activity, connectivity, regional balance and the environment, as well as availing of Irish or British Government investment initiatives such as the Irish Government’s Shared Ireland Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total spend on Reinvestment and Reform Initiative interest in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: ANSWERThe total spend on Reinvestment and Reform Initiative interest in the last ten years is detailed in the table below.	YearRRI Interest Payments£m2015-1655.12016-1757.02017-1855.82018-1953.62019-2051.22020-2147.92021-2245.02022-2343.02023-2450.32024-2555.1Total514.0

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have received any briefings on the Castlereagh Foundation.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (The Act) for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely:The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.The Act also provides for a further body, the Castlereagh Foundation, which can be established by either the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression or the Secretary of State. We have been briefed by our officials on our responsibilities under the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps she is taking to increase the number of bus journeys being taken.
answer_text: My department has developed and delivers messaging on the many benefits of using public transport, including Park & Ride and the Glider and we will continue to work with Translink and others to encourage modal shift.We all have our part to play and it is incumbent upon all of us to think about how, where possible, we can make little changes to how we make our everyday journeys, not only to improve our own travelling experience and well-being, but to help reduce carbon levels and ease congestion.Aligned with Translink's "Better Connected" strategy, its Marketing and Communication plan incorporates climate campaigns designed to promote modal shift and raise awareness of the benefits of using public transport.  A recent example is the launch of Translink's Better on Board Charter, an initiative designed to drive a modal shift in transportation across the North, encouraging more people to choose public transport, reducing congestion, improving air quality, and creating a more sustainable future for everyone. Partners signed up to the Charters collectively represent more than 100,000 people across the North.The promotion of any new Translink service also plays a vital role in the Marketing and Communication plans.  To promote Late Night Services, Translink, coupled with an integrated advertising plan, worked with a range of city stakeholders in supporting the important night-time economy during the busy festive season and encourage more use of public transport. Translink's late night festive public transport provision was heralded as a huge success with almost seventeen thousand passengers opting to use the bus and train as their convenient and reliable way home representing an increase of more than 50% compared to the same period during Christmas 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of active travel funding that has been spent on (i) installing new infrastructure; and (ii) updating existing infrastructure in each of the last two years.
answer_text: My department does not monitor spend in the format you have requested. Active travel benefits can be achieved through provision of new infrastructure or by upgrading infrastructure within a street.The link provides a comprehensive breakdown of spend on active travel projects delivered over the period requested.  Active Travel Breakdown spend.  There has been no change to the provisional outturn figure listed for 2023-24. In addition to direct spend on active travel projects, my department also routinely undertakes many activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists that are not currently included within the figures above. Work is currently ongoing to analyse all areas of this spend within the overall transport budgets to report a comprehensive position of active travel spend going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for the decision taken in relation to funding for Positive Life NI from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: The progression of 25 organisations to stage 2 in the Core Grant application process was based solely on how organisations scored against the following criteria, which is listed and explained in the guidance for applicants, available on my Department’s website:Evidence of how an organisation supports the department’s strategic objectives;Evidence that an organisation is involved in carrying out relevant activities;Evidence that an organisation will deliver clear outcomes, including how those will be measured and the targets that will be met;Evidence that an organisation has delivered similar outcomes previously; Evidence that an organisation has suitable monitoring and control systems in place, and suitably qualified and experienced staff; andEvidence that an organisation will work in partnership across the statutory, community and voluntary sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the decision not to award funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26 to Positive Life NI which is the region’s only dedicated HIV charity, to detail the steps his Department is taking to support HIV prevention, treatment, and awareness.
answer_text: The high standard of applications for core grant has reinforced the valuable contribution that voluntary and community sector organisations make towards health and social care outcomes. The selection of the 25 highest scoring applications, from 259, to progress to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The Department of Health’s Sexual Health Action Plan includes a strategic objective to strive to eliminate HIV transmission and to support those living with HIV to live well. As part of the Plan, a number of activities are underway in HIV surveillance, such as analysis of late and incident HIV diagnosis and increasing the timeliness of availability of HIV surveillance data. Actions are also underway relating to public communication activity focused on HIV awareness, testing and stigma. Efforts have also been made to introduce the Fast Track Cities initiative to Northern Ireland and steering group has been established to support identification of priority actions to support this. Positive Life continues to be contracted by Public Health Agency Health Improvement to the annual value of £171,203 to deliver a range of services and health promotion initiatives that contribute towards tackling HIV transmission in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the decision not to award funding from Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26 to Positive Life NI, how his Department plans to achieve its commitment to ending HIV transmission in Northern Ireland by 2030.
answer_text: The high standard of applications for core grant has reinforced the valuable contribution that voluntary and community sector organisations make towards health and social care outcomes. The selection of the 25 highest scoring applications, from 259, to progress to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The Department of Health’s Sexual Health Action Plan includes a strategic objective to strive to eliminate HIV transmission and to support those living with HIV to live well. As part of the Plan, a number of activities are underway in HIV surveillance, such as analysis of late and incident HIV diagnosis and increasing the timeliness of availability of HIV surveillance data. Actions are also underway relating to public communication activity focused on HIV awareness, testing and stigma. Efforts have also been made to introduce the Fast Track Cities initiative to Northern Ireland and steering group has been established to support identification of priority actions to support this. Positive Life continues to be contracted by Public Health Agency Health Improvement to the annual value of £171,203 to deliver a range of services and health promotion initiatives that contribute towards tackling HIV transmission in NI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 13373/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13373/22-27 was answered on 22 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures being examined to improve driver compliance with yellow box markings.
answer_text: The responsibility for enforcement of yellow box junctions rests with the PSNI Road Policing, Traffic Management Unit, who can issue Fixed Penalty Notices for this offence. In the context of congestion in Belfast City Centre, my officials have had conversations with the police around compliance as part of the multi-agency working on this issue.However, my Department has implemented a number of measures to mitigate traffic congestion in Belfast city centre in recent months including refreshing yellow box markings at a number of key junctions and displaying roadside messaging on driver compliance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the projected costs of the redevelopment of the Casement Park stadium.
answer_text: It is not possible to be definitive about project costs at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 17386/22-27, whether he has requested any briefings or meetings on further fiscal devolution.
answer_text: ANSWERMy immediate focus is to progress the commitments in the Interim Fiscal Framework in relation to the Executive’s funding arrangements. These issues need to be progressed as a priority ahead of the next phase of the Spending Review in June to ensure that public services are properly funded. I will be raising this with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury when we meet at the Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) on 27 February. Nonetheless, it is also vital that we have the fiscal levers we need at our disposal going forward, and it was important that my predecessor, Dr Archibald MLA, secured a commitment to consider further fiscal powers as part of the Interim Fiscal Framework. Once work is sufficiently advanced in relation to our funding, I will bring my proposals to the Executive on the additional fiscal powers we might seek having considered the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18088/22-27, AQW 16105/22-27 and AQW 21318/22-27, when her Department’s response to the Inconsiderate Pavement Parking Findings Report will be published.
answer_text: As you are aware, my Department sought the public’s views on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and a Findings Report was published on 6 December 2023. Officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will issue when I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will review guidance provided to local councils and affected residents to ensure privacy and security concerns are considered when their home is listed.
answer_text: I am happy for my Department to consider any privacy concerns identified by the owners of a private dwelling when it is proposed for listing.  I am also content that my Department liaise with Councils around privacy concerns of owners when it consults with them (under Section 80(3) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011) on proposals to list private dwellings.  	Ultimately, it is a matter for each Council to determine how it treats such consultations under section 42 of the local Government Act (NI) 2014.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps her Department has taken to support the recent PSNI recruitment campaign to attract less represented communities to the PSNI.
answer_text: Recruitment decisions are properly for the Chief Constable to make, though my Department has a role in ensuring that the legislation is in place to support recruitment. The current legislative framework makes provision for targeted advertising in places likely to reach under-represented groups and provides for appropriate measures to ensure accountability in the recruitment process, in addition to the normal oversight arrangements operated by the Policing Board.  Throughout the most recent campaign and on an ongoing basis, I continue to actively promote a career in policing, including through social media platforms and at appropriate engagements and events, and have actively encouraged anyone thinking of joining the PSNI to apply regardless of their background. I have also encouraged other political leaders and elected representative to also demonstrate their support for recruitment campaigns including in under-represented groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the LGBTQI+ Strategy.
answer_text: I have decided to take a phased approach to the development of the Social Inclusion Strategies, with work currently underway at pace on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies.The timeframe for the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy will be confirmed soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any financial support available to pharmacists who are struggling to pay their registration fees to the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As part of the ongoing consultation on a possible increase in registrant fees, The Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland (PSNI) have proposed an option to pay by quarterly instalments using direct debit as a way of spreading the financial impact. Registrant pharmacists experiencing financial distress can in confidence discuss their individual circumstances with the Pharmacists Advice and Support Service (PASS) facilitated by the Pharmacy Forum NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the potential risk to public safety due to her Department’s decision to remove lighting on Newforge Lane.
answer_text: I am conscious that street lighting can contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women travelling at night.  However, the desire for lighting has to be balanced against environmental considerations, our desire to reduce the carbon footprint, electricity costs associated with our infrastructure, and limited budgets. Decisions on provision or removal of lighting are made taking account of these considerations.Newforge Lane is only partially adopted by my Department with maintenance and inspection undertaken on the first 162m of Newforge Lane, as well as a 180m section from house number 28 to number 47. The remaining sections of road are unadopted and do not fall under the responsibility of my department.  For this reason, and in line with existing policy, the lighting within the unadopted sections had reached the end of its serviceable life and was removed. This policy helps ensure replacement lighting is provided where it is most needed as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the LGBTQI+ Strategy.
answer_text: On 27 April 2021, Deirdre Hargey MLA, the former Minister for Communities, wrote to the Minister of Health, requesting nominations of officials to the Department for Communities’ (DfC) Gender Equality and LGBTQI+ Strategy Cross-Departmental Working Groups.On 6 May 2021, Minister Swann replied and nominated an official as the DoH representative in respect of the LGBTQI+ working group.  In August 2021, following engagement with the LGBTQI+ Co-Design Group, DfC drafted a position paper that was shared with working group members setting out the commitments sought by the Co-Design Group in respect of four draft outcomes in the LGBTQI+ Strategy. Following a period for review, DfC’s draft position paper was then shared with each of the nine departments for consideration prior to a second full day workshop in October attended by Co-Design Group members and departmental representatives.On 5 April 2022, my Department was represented at the Ministerial Steering Group meeting on Social Strategies, which included the LGBTQI+ Strategy.On 14 June 2024, DfC sought input regarding a range of questions included in a questionnaire from the Council of Europe’s Expert Committee on Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity and Expression and Sex Characteristics to combat discrimination on grounds of sexual orientation or gender identity. My Department provided the input requested.		 		Going forward, my Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with DfC in respect of the LGBTQI+ Strategy and its other strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any employees of the Northern Ireland Civil Service are currently on a career break lasting longer than (i) two years; (ii) five years; and (iii) ten years, broken down by those which are (a) paid; and (b) unpaid.
answer_text: ANSWERThe table below shows the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service employees currently on a career break for longer than (i) two years; (ii) five years; and (iii) ten years. None of the employees on a career break are paid. Career break lasting longer than:Number of NICS employees 2 years 1795 years 6310 years 26

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the LGBTQI+ Strategy.
answer_text: There has been no request for engagement to date from the Minister for Communities on the Sexual Orientation Strategy. The Minister for Communities is the lead Minister for the Sexual Orientation Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of the Northern Ireland Civil Service, calculated on a full-time equivalent basis, that have been on long-term sick leave in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The percentage of the Civil Service that has been on long term sick leave in the last 5 years is more accurately reported by headcount rather than full time equivalent (FTE), as there are varying work patterns within the Civil Service.  The FTE of individuals can be combined however the number of sickness absence days of individuals cannot.  Table 1 sets out the percentage of staff in the Civil Service (based on headcount) that has had one or more long term absence within the last five years and the total overall headcount of the Civil Service as at 1 April each year.  A long term absence is defined as one lasting 20 consecutive working days or longer.Table 1. Percentage of staff with one or more long-term absences within the last 5 years and headcount as at 1 April each year.YearPercentage of staff with one or more long-term absenceHeadcount 1 April2019/202013.322,8942020/202110.822,8952021/202212.723,2162022/202312.424,1262023/202414.024,407	Agency staff, staff on career breaks or on secondment outside the NICS are excluded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any proposals he or his predecessor (i) has brought forward; and (ii) plans to bring forward in support of a local mutual bank.
answer_text: My Department's priority in this shortened mandate is to strengthen the existing Credit Union movement rather than establishing a new mutual bank.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the projects in the South Belfast constituency in receipt of Communities in Transition funding.
answer_text: Communities in Transition (CIT) operates across eight designated areas. South Belfast is not one of the designated areas and no projects are currently being delivered in the South Belfast constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the LGBTQI+ Strategy.
answer_text: I have had no engagement with the Minister of Communities in relation to the LGBTQI+ Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any progress made in the delivery of the Belfast Streets Ahead programme and its outstanding phases of public realm improvement.
answer_text: My department continues to progress two phases under the Belfast Streets Ahead (BSA) Public Realm programme.  These are BSA Phase 3 in the north of the city centre and BSA Phase 5 in the south of the city centre.  These projects have, however, been significantly impacted by the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) plans for the next phase of the Belfast Rapid Transit (BRT) system and by the development of Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035. My officials continue to liaise with DfI officials to get a full understanding of the impacts of BRT and ETP on the Belfast Streets Ahead programme to identify areas for greater collaboration and to avoid potentially nugatory work and expenditure.While my department cannot progress the entire BSA Phase 5 project, at this time, an area in and around Blackstaff Square is being progressed as this area has been identified as not being significantly impacted by either BRT or ETP.  It is anticipated that that a planning application for Blackstaff Square and the surrounding streets will be submitted in the summer of 2025 with construction anticipated to start by Summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any proposals she or her predecessor (i) has brought forward; and (ii) plans to bring forward in support of a local mutual bank.
answer_text: My Department's priority in this shortened mandate is to strengthen the existing Credit Union movement rather than establishing a new mutual bank.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has funded any red squirrel monitoring and restoration projects in the greater Belfast area.
answer_text: My Department is not currently funding any red squirrel monitoring and restoration projects in the Greater Belfast area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the UK Treasury has offered any clarity on the origin of the Barnett consequentials which gave rise to the £17.1 million allocated on 6 February 2025.
answer_text: Following the conclusion of the Westminster Supplementary Estimates, Treasury officials advised that a more detailed analysis of the changes which gave rise to the Barnett consequentials provided at the Autumn Budget had resulted in an additional £17.1 million Resource DEL, and a reduction of £3.5 million Capital DEL, in 2024-25.  No further breakdown was provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she will be taking to progress the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The recommendations in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review provide an evidence-based framework to inform future investment in the railways across the island of Ireland. However, more work is needed to test the feasibility and affordability of the Review’s recommendations and to secure the necessary funding to take projects forward. Already, rail services on the island are being developed towards the Report’s vision. This includes an increase to an hourly train service between Dublin and Belfast, while procurement has also started on a new, faster, more sustainable “Enterprise” train fleet for the Dublin-Belfast route.The Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank Advisory Services to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations.With the assistance of EIB consultants, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail and NTA to consider the recommendations and identify those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035. Translink are currently undertaking a number of feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which include four funded from the Union Connectivity Development Fund: the reopening of Antrim-Lisburn (with a link to Belfast International Airport); Armagh-Portadown; Electrification of Belfast to the Border; and Portadown-Derry.These studies are due to report by spring/summer this year.  The Department will consider its prioritisation of projects once these feasibility studies are complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19516/22-27, when the additional consultation on the Knockbreda Road pedestrian crossing will start and finish.
answer_text: As Belfast City Council are the leading body on this consultation, I am unable to advise when the additional consultation will commence or complete.  However, I can advise that my officials will work collaboratively with their counterparts in the Council throughout the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether departmental five-year plans will be linked to budgets.
answer_text: Departments have been asked to produce five-year plans which include anticipated costs over that period.Alongside this DoF will produce a best estimate of Block Grant funding over the next five years. This will not be an indicative Budget but should aid understanding around any potential gaps between anticipated costs and estimated available funding. This work will inform the upcoming Budget process for 2026-29 and, through ongoing review, will continue to inform subsequent Budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the regional rates increase for 2025-26 will be presented to the Executive for discussion.
answer_text: The Executive has now formally agreed my proposals for the regional rate for 2025/26. This reflects the levels already published prior to Christmas in the draft Budget 2025/26.My Department will now make and lay the legislation for the Assembly to agree the required Statutory Rule at the start of March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will be published.
answer_text: Subject to agreement from my Executive colleagues, I intend to introduce an Employment Rights Bill in the Assembly by January 2026, or earlier if there is scope to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of meetings she and her predecessor have had in relation to securing pre-clearance for Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: I am planning to meet with the British Secretary of State for Transport in the near future to discuss shared interests in transport including aviation matters.  As civil aviation is a reserved matter, my officials have explored with the Department for Transport (DfT) the possibility of extending pre-clearance services to Belfast International Airport. DfT advised that the decision on whether to operate such facilities at airports overseas ultimately rests with the US authorities. DfT has committed to entering into exploratory talks with the United States Administration. My officials will do all they can to facilitate these discussions. My officials have also explored with Belfast International Airport number of issues, including the need to secure transatlantic flights which is a prequisite to potential support from the US Administration for pre-clearance, as part of its ongoing work with all three local airports in developing an Aviation Policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the legislative programme for 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s five-year spending plan, in accordance with the Budget Sustainability Plan.
answer_text: The member will be aware that as part of the agreement on the return of the Executive in 2024, DoF were required to publish and implement a plan for sustainable public finances and services and this plan was published in October 2024.My Department has been engaging with DoF to commence the development of longer-term financial sustainability plans which will cover 5 years and will help the Executive to plan over a longer time frame, even in the absence of multi-year budgets.Whilst DoF has not formally commissioned input yet, they have provided a suggested outline of the main headings they might expect to see in a five-year plan. They have also indicated that a likely timeframe might be that this would be produced by the Autumn, to inform the setting of the next budget in addition to longer term thinking.My department is currently assessing the suggested outline of the 5-year plan with a view to commissioning work internally to gather the inputs required.This work will incorporate my Three-Year Plan for the HSC which I published on 10th December 2024, and which outlines my plans for the remainder of this Assembly mandate. My key priorities for the next three years include delivering reform, tackling waiting lists, improving cancer services, addressing mental health and confronting persistent health inequalities. This will require investment in all parts of our system, including primary care, community care, social care and secondary care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Cost of Doing Business Study will include the cost of locally levied rates.
answer_text: The Cost of Doing Business research will cover a broad range of factors including property costs, which will take into account businesses’ annual rates liability. The terms of reference for the research is available on the department’s website - Cost of Doing Business Research - Terms of Reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has notified the Irish Government of the gold mine application at the Sperrin Mountains.
answer_text: Whilst my Department did carry out transboundary consultation specifically with Donegal County Council, officials issued a further transboundary consultation on 30 January 2025 to the Irish Government as the EEA State, which will allow the public in the south of Ireland opportunity to have their say and express their views on the project prior to the resumption of the public local inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any specific level of additional Barnett consequentials arising from the rise in employer National Insurance that the UK Treasury has indicated will be provided to the Executive.
answer_text: The Chancellor has said that support will be provided to departments and public sector employers for the additional cost due to increases to employers' National Insurance Contributions. The Executive will receive Barnett consequentials on any additional funding provided to Whitehall departments.As yet the Treasury has not confirmed the amount the Executive will receive. However, it is anticipated that the funding provided may fall well short of what is required. In an introductory meeting with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury I have highlighted the importance of the increased costs to the Executive being met in full and the need for the quantum of funding to be confirmed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will approve the installation of a pedestrian crossing on the Cairnshill Road, Castlereagh, at Finsbury Avenue and Cairnshill Crescent.
answer_text: Pedestrian crossing requests are considered where a need has been identified and are prioritised in line with criteria contained within my Department’s Policy.  This assessment takes into account the volume of vehicles and pedestrians using the road, as well as other factors including;the speed of traffic;the width of the road and the number of lanes to be crossed;the proximity to public amenities such as schools, libraries, health centres etc;the number of vulnerable and less mobile road users; andthe pedestrian collision history of the site.Officials carried out a survey on Cairnshill Road, in the vicinity of Finsbury Avenue, on 19 September 2024 and regrettably, results indicate that this location was not prioritised highly enough when compared to other proposed locations being considered for delivery within the limited budget available.The location will continue to be included on a list of possible sites to be considered when determining programmes for future years, subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has proposed any further gambling reform legislation for inclusion in the (i) Programme for Government; and (ii) legislative programme.
answer_text: I have not proposed any further gambling reform legislation for inclusion in either the Programme for Government or the executive legislative programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has made an assessment of the impact of wood burning in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has not assessed the impact of wood burning in Northern Ireland however the Air Pollutant Inventories for England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland 2005-2022 hold modelled data on domestic combustion in Northern Ireland. https://naei.energysecurity.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2024-10/DA_Air_Pollutant_Inventories_2005-2022.pdf
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the recommendations in the Asthma and Lung Northern Ireland report Domestic Burning In Northern Ireland Winter 2024-25.
answer_text: I welcome this report from Asthma and Lung UK and support the work they are doing to raise awareness of the issues around the burning of solid fuels. The Executive’s Energy Strategy, “The Path to Net Zero Energy,” sets out a pathway to decarbonising our economy and phasing out fossil fuels and aims to reduce carbon emissions and improve air quality to mitigate against the impacts on people’s health. I am committed to working with partners across government to deliver the Energy Strategy which includes the phasing out of coal and certain solid fuels for home heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any private or community organisations his Department commissions to provide services to those living with HIV+; (ii) the amount of annual funding each of these organisations receive; and (iii) any specific services they are commissioned to provide.
answer_text: (i) Positive Life is Northern Ireland’s only charity dedicated to supporting people living with HIV. (ii) The Public Health Agency commissions Positive Life to provide services for people living with HIV to a total value of £171,203.  Services are outlined below. (iii) The specific services provided are categorised as Tier 1, Tier 2 and Tier 3 services.The Tier 1 Service deliver planned individual and group interventions. The individual interventions programme provides an assessment and support-plan and has been delivered to 838 people affected by HIV in Northern Ireland. The group intervention programme which provides assessment and a residential programme has been delivered to 8 people affected by HIV in Northern Ireland.The Tier 2 Service is a programme of open access advice, information & support. 22 Therapeutic sessions have been delivered to 110 individuals, 12 Information sessions to 60 individuals as well as 20 Individual Outreach sessions The Tier 3 Service is a Telephone Helpline and Befriending Service. This provides a free phone confidential helpline and telephone support service to the general public and also provides regular communication support for people living with HIV in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the risk groups identified in the Public Health Agency HIV Surveillance in Northern Ireland 2019 report, to detail (i) the services provided to these groups; and (ii) the number of people that accessed these dedicated services, broken down by (a) gender; (b) sexual orientation; (c) ethnicity; and (d) age profile, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: HIV prevention interventions include education and awareness, promotion of risk reduction, HIV testing, access to Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) and access to HIV treatment for people diagnosed with HIV. Services are provided across the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, with additional services commissioned by the Public Health Agency.In recent years, HIV services have been further developed with investment to establish consultant-led teams in all five HSC Trusts. PrEP services have also been commissioned in recent years, aimed at reducing the number of new cases of HIV.Some elements of service provision, such as social work or clinical psychology support, are available regionally via the Belfast HSC Trust. The Belfast HSC Trust manages inpatient requirements for the region. The Public Health Agency HIV surveillance report, published in November 2024, sets out the latest data on people receiving HIV care in Northern Ireland by gender, age and ethnicity. This report can be accessed at https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2024-11/2024%20Northern%20Ireland%20HIV%20data%20%282023%20data%29.pdf The Public Health Agency also commissions a number of services which focus on provision of whole population and targeted awareness raising, as well as some services providing targeted HIV testing. HIV charity Positive Life has been commissioned to provide services for people living with HIV.  Over the past five years, these services have included planned individual interventions, group interventions, open programmes of information and support, and a telephone helpline and befriending service. The Rainbow Project has also been commissioned to provide a range of services for clients over the last 5 years. These services have included rapid testing for HIV and syphilis, support services tailored to the specific needs of LGBTQIA+ people across Northern Ireland, counselling sessions & interventions and awareness raising sessions for local communities to increase knowledge and skills.The Public Health Agency funds an outreach HIV clinic in the Belfast HSC Trust which provides specialist HIV care to individuals with a history of injecting drug use and/or those who are homeless. Injecting drug use has been recognised as an emergent risk factor for HIV infection in Northern Ireland in the 2022 and 2023 HIV reports.The Public Health Agency was unable to provide data on the number of service users over the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 18089/22-27, to detail (i) the proportion of the overall £314 million capital investment that remains to be allocated; and (ii) whether it will be ring-fenced for future budgets.
answer_text: The latest estimate (at December 2024) is that up to £312m capital investment is required to complete construction of the campus.  In March 2024 the Executive restated its commitment to the Strule Shared Education Campus pledging £150m of additional ring-fenced funding for the programme during the period 2024-25 to 2026-27. Of that £20m was allocated in the 2024-25 financial year.Budgets beyond the financial year 2024-25 have not yet been allocated and decisions on future years budget for all Departments will be subject to Executive review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will initiate an awareness raising campaign on the public health impacts of burning solid fuel and air pollution as recommended by the Domestic Burning in Northern Ireland Winter 2024-25 report from Asthma and Lung UK.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the impacts of burning solid fuel and air pollution on public health and how awareness raising can lead to positive behavioural change.  I will consider an awareness campaign on smoke control areas and the restrictions applicable therein, but a campaign will be dependent upon the resources available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a timeline for the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: The process of obtaining confirmation that the Dilapidation Bill is within the legislative competence of the Assembly is taking longer than had originally been anticipated.  The next stage will be to seek approval for the introduction of the Bill at the earliest available meeting of the Executive.   Once Executive approval is received, I will seek to have the Bill introduced, with the Speaker’s agreement, as soon as possible. Beyond that I am obviously unable to give definitive timescales for any subsequent stages of the Bill’s passage through the Assembly as this will be at the discretion of the Assembly Business Office.However, I would hope that Royal Assent could be achieved by late 2025 or early 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's work on a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme in Northern Ireland for parents who have suffered the loss of a child before 24 weeks gestation. In a joint announcement on 9 October 2024, the Finance Minister outlined her Department’s intention to bring forward legislation to enable a scheme to be developed in Northern Ireland.  My Department will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance on the necessary scoping work. I have also asked my officials to continue to engage with the Department of Health and Social Care so that all options for introducing a scheme can be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a timeline for the introduction of a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.Primary legislation is currently being drafted which will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. I hope to introduce the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill into the Assembly in the next number of weeks.We will continue to work with the Department of Health to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure that it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this work at pace. A more detailed schedule will be made available when this legislation has been considered by the Assembly, including the secondary legislation that will set out the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with Translink to (i) review the pricing structure of parking at stations that are frequently used as park and ride facilities; and (ii) reduce the cost of parking at train stations, such as Lanyon Place, that are used as park and ride facilities.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Car parks that are managed and operated by Translink and issues such as car parking costs, do not fall under the remit of the public service agreement my Department has with Translink. I can confirm that the previous parking rate available at Lanyon Place Station was subsidised to encourage use of the Enterprise service. The relocation of the service to Grand Central Station has resulted in the parking charge reverting to its original rate. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of traffic calming alterations for Orpen Park, Finaghy.
answer_text: The review of the proposed traffic calming measures at Orpen Park, Finaghy is ongoing and has taken longer than expected due to pressures on my limited staff resource. I have however asked officials, subject to other priority work, to bring this consultation to a conclusion within the next 6 months subject to available resources.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on plans to transfer his Department's land at Newforge to Newforge Sports Complex.
answer_text: My Department has now completed the initial phase of a Strategic Review of the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute’s Land and Property Holdings.  This has helped inform considerations around the lands at Newforge and discussions are ongoing between officials from my Department, the Newforge Community Development Trust and, indeed, other interested parties about the potential next steps for the DAERA lands at Newforge.  These discussions are being taken forward on a ‘without prejudice’ basis and any potential disposal or lease of the lands in the future will follow due process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Draft Budget 2025-26.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the provision that has been made to allow the free flowing of cyclists as a result of the upgrade of the A24 Saintfield Road and Knockbracken Road junction, Belfast.
answer_text: The road improvement scheme at Saintfield Road / Knockbracken Road is a developer led scheme and relates to planning conditions associated with the residential development of the Mealough Road Housing Zoning.As an integral part of the improvement scheme, a shared pedestrian / cycleway of 2.5m in width will be continuous on both sides of the Saintfield Road for the extents of the scheme.  There will be a 0.5m buffer between the shared use path and the carriageway in red surfacing material.Signalised toucan crossings will be in place to facilitate the safe crossing of side road approaches to the Saintfield Road.  In addition, a toucan crossing will be installed at the Knockbracken Healthcare Estate entrance to facilitate cycle crossing of the Saintfield Road.Advanced cycle stop lanes will be implemented on the Saintfield Road, providing a 5m stacking area (in green surfacing) for cyclists travelling along the road.The improvement scheme will remove discontinuities in cycle provision at the Knockbracken Road junction and will provide enhanced facilities to promote sustainable travel at this location.  The introduction of additional controlled crossing facilities will also provide road safety benefits for vulnerable road users.
