question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to review the eligibility for the half fare concessionary travel pass within the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The NI Concessionary Fares Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2023 and included an option which would widen the qualifying criteria for a SmartPass on the grounds of disability to align it more closely with other UK jurisdictions.While public support for this option was strong, (with 81% of respondents in favour of the change), due to the budgetary pressures on my department, I was not in a position to introduce the option this financial year. However, I have committed to reconsidering the qualifying criteria again in the future, if the financial position allows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline who is responsible for ensuring that teacher workload agreements are properly in place.
answer_text: The Teachers’ Workload Agreement, TNC 2024/2 has been agreed and was issued to all schools by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) in April 2024. It recognises the importance of the role of the professional Teacher in ensuring that the educational needs of young people are met as fully as possible.It is the responsibility of Boards of Governors to ensure that the workload of all Principals, Vice Principals and Teachers is managed fairly and consistently to ensure that all teaching professionals can discharge their roles and responsibilities effectively for the benefit of the teaching and learning of pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to list the Barnett consequentials arising from UK Government support for high street business and hospitality in relation to business rates in England.
answer_text: The Chancellor’s Autumn budget on 30 October 2022, included a reduction of £79.66 million in 2025-26 in relation to business rates.  This includes the impact of the reduction in the support provided to retail, hospitality and leisure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how much his Department spent on his visit to the United States in October.
answer_text: I attended a series of meetings and events in the US and Canada during a visit between 10-18 October 2024.	Flights	£25,639	Accommodation	£13,170	Other travel and subsistence costs	£1,658	TOTAL COSTS for 6 people	£40,467		Some additional small amounts of travel and subsistence may remain to be claimed by officials in accordance with the NICS Travel and Subsistence Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the efficacy of teacher workload agreements.
answer_text: The teachers' Workload Agreement TNC 2024/2 was jointly developed and agreed by both the teaching trade unions (NITC) and Management Side of TNC.The central purpose of the agreement is to enable Principals, Vice Principals and Teachers to achieve the following objectives:a. to maintain and enhance the quality of teaching and learning; b. to preserve, in so far as this is possible, the flexibility of the contractual framework and the professional autonomy of staff; c. to ensure that members of staff are able to observe their contractual requirements, pursue their careers and undertake their day-to-day duties without unfair and unreasonable requirements being placed on them in relation to those duties and with sufficient time to enjoy a reasonable work/life balance; d. to protect and enhance the professional relationships within a school; e. to ensure that Boards of Governors as employers are aware of and in a position to meet their legal obligations with respect to employment matters and to operate within the spirit of the legislation governing Working Time and Health and Safety.The agreement clarifies the legislative terms and conditions of teaching staff including working hours, teaching time, cover arrangements and supervision.The guidance outlined in the agreement has been developed to provide a framework for Principals and Boards of Governors to ensure the allocation and distribution of duties to all Principals, Vice Principals and Teachers is managed in a fair and reasonable manner that enables staff to observe their contractual requirements with sufficient time to enjoy a reasonable work/life balance.I am satisfied that the Workload Agreement TNC 2024/2 is effective in providing clarification, advice and guidance on teachers' terms and conditions to Boards of Governors, Principals and Teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will implement an action plan or provide sign language interpreters on standby, in case of emergencies where deaf people require urgent medical assistance.
answer_text: The Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) was introduced on 1st April 2023 and offers a range of in-person and remote sign language interpreting services for health and social care appointments.  The service is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI) and is delivered through one central booking service.  SPPG have set up an Advisory Board to help advise and shape the RCSS for people who are deaf and hard of hearing in Northern Ireland.HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland worked collaboratively on the development of their new five-year Disability Action Plan and Equality Action Plan (2024-2029).  The plans outline actions and initiatives to address section 75 inequalities, improve equality of opportunity, promote positive attitudes for disabled people and improve their participation in public life.  The Actions in the Plan have been shaped by an audit of inequalities, engagement with experts by experience and key stakeholders and formal consultation.A specific Action is included in the 2024 – 2029 Plan in relation to deaf and hard of hearing service users.  This commits all Trusts to continue the roll out of the Health and Social Care Regional Communication Support Service for People who are d/Deaf, d/Deafblind and Hard of Hearing and improve access to Sign Video within hospitals using computers and phones accessing the Trust Wi-Fi.SLI interpreters in NI work as freelancers, meaning they cannot control when they choose to work, which includes Out of Hours (OOH).  Those who have expressed they would be happy to be contacted by OOH are not obliged to take calls or answer text messages when an in hours or OOH booking is requested.It is apparent that the number of interpreters is insufficient to meet current and future demands in NI.  SLI are working to increase the number of interpreters in NI.   DfC are currently investing in an Interpreter Training Programme delivered by Foyle Deaf Association which provides for the training and registering of six British Sign Language and four Irish Sign Language interpreters.  Additionally, the Department is investing in a further increase in the numbers of British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language interpreters through funding to Queen’s University Centre for Translation and Interpreting to deliver an MA in Interpreting for up to 20 Deaf and hearing students.  This two-year course launched in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the evidence on which he based his decision to allow taxis to use bus lanes.
answer_text: The disruption to traffic flow in Belfast city centre is well documented and to alleviate some of it, and to support the taxi industry, I asked my officials to bring forward proposals which will permit Class A and Class C taxis to use certain bus lanes in the vicinity of the works. Work on the legislation is ongoing and

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 16957/22-27, to detail the amount of funding for the Strule Shared Education Campus that remains to be allocated.
answer_text: The overall estimated Department of Education (DE) cost of the Strule Campus is £375m.The latest estimate is that up to £316m capital and £8m revenue investment is required to be allocated to complete construction of the campus and fund support and evaluation costs for 10 years after campus opening.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 16956/22-27, for his assessment of the impact the changes to the Central Applications Office points system for points awarded to A-levels will have on northern undergraduate entry levels to colleges and universities in the Republic of Ireland in the September 2024 intake.
answer_text: It is my understanding that the proposed changes to improve the points system to better align the equivalence of the A-Level grades with Leaving Certificate points awarded in the South of Ireland, announced in December 2023 by the then Minister Simon Harris, are not due to take effect fully until 2025. These changes, therefore, will not have had any significant impact on the September 2024 intake.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will publish detailed proposals for the devolution of new tax powers to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the response issued for AQW 17386/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has sought any (i) meetings; and (ii) briefings in relation to opportunities for food producers to expand sales in the EU under the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: My department is committed to maximising the benefits of our high quality local produce to consumers both here and across the globe. As an example of this commitment, I am looking look forward to soon publishing the Executive approved NI Food Strategy Framework.  		Although, I have not sought any specific meetings or briefings in relation to opportunities for food producers to expand sales in the EU under the Windsor Framework I regularly advocate the benefits of dual market access when I can and am fully committed to supporting our food producers as appropriate.  I recognise delivery of and compliance with Windsor Framework requirements are key to securing dual market access and continued access to EU markets.		I have continually espoused this message during all meetings with my Welsh and Scottish colleagues, UK and Irish Government and industry reps. My officials have worked positively with Defra, DAFM and other UK jurisdictions in many areas of food policy such as review of marketing standards, food security and both EU and UK GI schemes.		I am also advocating for a SPS/veterinary agreement, thereby providing the platform for continuity and further supporting this vital market.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the report of the Fiscal Commission will be brought to the Executive for discussion.
answer_text: My department has prioritised those elements of the interim Fiscal Framework which need to be progressed in the near-term. These include: publishing a sustainability plan; establishing an interim Public Sector Transformation Board to invite and assess proposals for the £235m ring-fenced transformation programme; and developing an evidence base in relation to the relative need factor used in our funding arrangements.Once these elements are sufficiently advanced, I will then bring my proposals to the Executive on the additional fiscal powers we might seek to negotiate with the British Government as part of a wider Fiscal Framework. These proposals will be informed by both the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission, and the results of the public consultation already undertaken by my department in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any new entrance or egress on protected routes in the last five years.
answer_text: Accesses onto protected routes are a granted by the planning authority through the planning permission process.  The Department’s policy is in PPS 3 AMP 4    https://library2.nics.gov.uk/pdf/drd/2014/0342.pdfWhilst the Department holds the information requested, it is not in a readily accessible format as the planning portal is not searchable by protected route. To accurately provide an answer to the member’s question, officials would need to examine each planning application individually for the requested 5 year period. This information could only be obtained at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce congestion at Finaghy crossroads.
answer_text: I can advise that the current traffic signal sequencing at Finaghy crossroads is complex, with up to 6 traffic movements and 4 pedestrian movements that need to be controlled efficiently but more importantly, safely, within the limited road space available.During peak periods the traffic lanes leading to and from this junction run at full capacity and with limited road space there is only one traffic lane on the legs exiting the junction. Due to the bus stop on the Upper Lisburn Road country bound side of the junction, traffic on occasions tails back to the lights causing congestion.Given the limited road space, the current traffic signalling arrangement and road marking layout at this junction provides the best possible balance between the competing demands of through and turning traffic. There are no plans to make any changes at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total cost of the Fiscal Commission.
answer_text: The total resource cost of the Fiscal Commission, including the renumeration of members, the cost of secretariat staff seconded from NICS, and external research, was £544,917. There was no capital cost in this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department is taking to protect the rights of private renters.
answer_text: My Department is progressing a programme of Private Rented Sector (PRS) reform.  A key component of this work is achieving a balance between the rights and responsibilities of landlords and tenants.   Recent changes to private tenancies law that have increased the rights and protections for tenants include:Requirements for certain information to be provided on commencing and during a tenancy;A limit on deposits that can be requested and held;The requirement for receipts for cash payments; and Measures to increase fire safety.Ongoing work that will further enhance these rights and protections, includes the development of regulations on electrical safety and to allow the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods.  It is anticipated that both these regulations will be in place in the coming months.		I am currently considering what further actions I can take over the reminder of the mandate that will further improve the safety, security and standards of the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reviews that have taken place of school pitches in response to reports of the presence of toxic microplastics in the recent installation of rubber crumb artificial grass pitches in schools.
answer_text: The Department is following the current regulations and guidance from the Sports Councils (Sport England, Sport Wales, Sportscotland and Sport NI) with regard to addressing the environmental impact of rubber infill in 3g pitches. Viable alternatives to the rubber infill used and improved recycling opportunities and processes are being considered and research is underway by the Sports Councils and The Sport and Play Construction Association (SAPCA). The Department will take advice from its technical advisers in Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) and from industry experts in agreeing suitable alternatives for use on the schools’ estate. In the interim, contractors providing synthetic pitches on the schools’ estate are advised to follow best practice guidance on how to minimise infill dispersion into the environment during construction, use, maintenance and disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure those who have been privately diagnosed with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder have timely access to medication through the health service.
answer_text: Service users accepted onto an ADHD waiting list with a private ADHD diagnosis are placed on a waiting list in chronological order of their referral, in line with Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP) guidelines. Having a private diagnosis will not change their position on the waiting list for a consultant appointment.HSCB wrote to private health providers and GP’s in June 2021 of their duty to inform patients that there may be ongoing costs associated with their private health care treatment until such time as they are seen by a HSC clinician.When patients with private assessments reach the top of the waiting list, Trusts will evaluate the private assessment process against compliance with National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. If the diagnostic assessment complies with NICE, the diagnosis will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided by HSC. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines, a HSC clinician will complete the diagnosis. Drugs prescribed to treat ADHD are controlled and it is advised that they are prescribed initially by a Consultant. With a private ADHD diagnosis anyone requesting a prescription through the HSC, will have to be physically assessed by the HSC prescribing clinician to assess their medical need.  Drug treatment for children, young people and adults with ADHD should always form part of a comprehensive treatment plan that includes psychological, behavioural and educational advice and interventions (or occupational needs in the case of adults). If prescribing under shared care agreements it should be clear where responsibility for prescribing lies.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average time spent on a waiting list for adults who have been privately diagnosed with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder who wish to access medication through the health service.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care TrustThe specifics of this information would not be available within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust waiting list information. Northern Health and Social Care TrustNorthern Health and Social Care Trust do not hold this information. South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustAs the Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) Service has not been formally commissioned, waiting times may be in excess of several years.  The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is not able to given an accurate or estimated average length of time spent on a waiting list for adults who have been privately diagnosed with ADHD who wish to access medication through the health service.  The Trust currently is only in a position to continue medication and review patients who have an active prescription.Southern Health and Social Care TrustThere is no commissioned adult ADHD diagnostic service in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, therefore, the Trust does not retain a waiting list.Western Health and Social Care TrustDiagnostic assessment and treatment for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder is not a funded service within the Western Health and Social Care Trust (Trust) Adult Mental Health Services.  Therefore, referrals for adults are not accepted within the Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to (i) phase out the use of microplastics in artificial grass pitches by 2030, in line with EU chemical REACH legislation; and (ii) ensure that the financial burden of this change in legislation does not fall on grassroots sports organisations.
answer_text: My Department and Sport NI, an Arm’s Length Body of the Department, are still assessing the impact of the EU chemical REACH legislation on Northern Ireland.  This legislation will ban the sale of intentionally added microplastics into the European market, including rubber infill for synthetic surfaces such as the 3G pitches which have been installed across Northern Ireland.  The European Commission has confirmed a transition period before the new restriction becomes effective in October 2031.My officials advise at this stage, there is no immediate requirement to remove existing rubber crumb infill from artificial grass pitches, however it is expected that it will not be possible to source new rubber crumb to maintain existing artificial grass pitches from October 2031.Research confirms that the typical lifestyle of a properly maintained 3G pitch is 10 years, therefore it may be possible to maintain any existing pitches for its expected lifespan. I also understand research continues in the sports sector to identify satisfactory substitute materials.Regarding the financial impact of this legislation on the sector, it is normal practice that for any 3G pitch funded by my Department or Sport NI, that the asset owner considers the lifecycle and maintenance costs for the project as part of its initial Business Case assessment stage.  I have asked my officials along with Sport NI, to continue to monitor developments regarding the REACH legislation and any equivalent in the United Kingdom; future developments of new style artificial grass pitches; and the financial impact these developments may have on sporting organisations across Northern Ireland. Sport NI’s Chief Executive has confirmed that they have provided information on this matter to the sports sector via a publication of its ‘Synthetic Turf Pitches and Microplastic Pollution’ paper, which is available on its website at https://www.sportni.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Microplastics-Information-Paper-November-2023.pdf.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will bring forward a motion for a democratic consent vote under the provisions of Article 18 of the Protocol on Ireland in the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement.
answer_text: Any decision to jointly table a motion for a democratic consent vote is for the First Minister and deputy First Minister to consider, following the initiation of the democratic consent process by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11225/22-27, to detail the number of Executive meetings where he has raised the budget for the Arts.
answer_text: I have raised funding for the Arts directly in bilaterals and in writing with the Finance Minister.My position on the budget for the arts is on the record.  I continue to make the case for increased funding for this important sector at every available opportunity.I will also be sending a paper to Executive colleagues shortly on the social, Economic and educational benefits of the Arts and the cross departmental benefits that will arise from increased investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions his Department is taking to encourage (i) students from Northern Ireland to apply for undergraduate study in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) students from the Republic of Ireland Ireland to apply for undergraduate study in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am committed to removing barriers to people from here who want to study in the South and vice versa.  Officials are considering options for addressing these barriers and I am seeking a meeting with my counterpart in the South, Minister Patrick O’Donovan, so that we can confirm actions that can be taken, separately and collectively, to open up greater opportunities for our young people to study throughout Ireland.  It is already clear that there is a desire for better information for prospective students about their options and that is an issue on which officials are currently working.  Starting this academic year my department has made available postgraduate tuition fee loans of up to £6,500 for eligible postgraduate students from here studying at an approved institution in the South. I also intend to introduce similar loans for part-time undergraduate students from here who wish to study in the South. Both of these financial packages will help address some of the financial barriers to cross border student mobility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an up to date full cost estimate for the construction of the Strule Shared Education Campus.
answer_text: The latest total overall Department of Education cost of the Strule Campus is £375m. This is the investment required to complete the construction of the campus and fund support and evaluation costs for 10 years after campus opening.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish an updated Nutrients Action Programme.
answer_text: In June this year my Department published an Implementation Report for the Nutrients Action Programme covering the period 2020 to 2023.  Officials met with key stakeholders to present the findings from this report and to outline the review of the Nutrients Action Programme which is currently being progressed.My Officials are scheduled to brief the AERA Committee in November on the outcome of the review and proposals for the updated Action Programme. A further stakeholder meeting will also be held in November.A public consultation on proposals for the updated Action Programme will be launched in December.  In parallel, there will also be a consultation on the associated Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulation Assessment.Following the consultation and consideration of the responses, I envisage that the updated NAP Regulations will be introduced before the end of June next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will publish a report on the Accessible Transport Strategy 2025.
answer_text: The Department for Regional Development consulted on the Accessible Transport Strategy 2025 in late 2015 and published a summary of responses to the consultation in 2016. Actions to support an inclusive and accessible transport network were subsequently included in the draft Programme for Government Outcomes and Delivery Plan which was consulted on in 2016. Following this, the focus has been on ensuring that inclusive transport is embedded within my Department’s policies and infrastructure, rather than bringing forward a standalone Accessibility Strategy. This approach helps embed the culture of inclusivity within the Department’s work.  In line with this approach, the Department draws on the advice of the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Access Committee to guide the design and development of new transport infrastructure and policies.  The development of a new Transport Strategy 2035, which my officials are currently taking forward, will provide further opportunities to consider and improve the accessibility of the wider transport system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the positive impacts recorded by his Department in relation to the Employment and Skills Initiative, Art Work.
answer_text: This initiative has contributed to addressing labour market barriers faced by individuals and organisations in the Arts Sector in light of the impact of the pandemic. 70 new employment opportunities have been created through the Employment and Skills Initiative’s Art Work scheme, supporting the role that Arts sector organisations play in educating, inspiring, educating, and invigorating communities across Northern Ireland.		Feedback from the Department’s delivery partner, FutureScreens NI, has identified positive benefits to participants in terms of enhancing their job prospects and skill sets. It has also provided scope for some organisations to expand their activities, reach and their capacity post Covid.		The lessons learned from the Employment and Skills Initiative will inform the ongoing support my Department provides for the sectors involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected financial saving for not renewing the Employment and Skills Initiative, Art Work.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 16111/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of meetings she has had in relation to Civil Service reform.
answer_text: Civil Service reform by its nature involves a wide range of components, including but not limited to embracing technology, reform of our workforce/estate and modernisation of our finance and HR services through Integr8. I have had briefings and updates about a wide range of enablers relating to Civil Service reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why the Executive Sub-Committee on Reform Following the RHI Inquiry has not met since 2020.
answer_text: Under its terms of reference, the Sub-Committee was to meet after the publication of the Inquiry Report in order to produce an action plan for implementation of recommendations flowing out of the RHI Inquiry report; and to agree a final report to be submitted to the Executive for publication.At its third and last meeting on 16 December 2020, it completed its preparation of the action plan.  Its final report was agreed by the Executive in October 2021.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they secured any promises of investment from the International Investment Summit on 14 October 2024.
answer_text: The International Investment Summit provided us with an opportunity to showcase our economy, our people, and our approach to partnering with investors.We connected directly with a wide range of current and prospective investors at the Summit.  During our bi-lateral meetings with companies, we emphasised the advantages of locating here and the various supports available to investment partners.  We are optimistic that these constructive and positive engagements, along with our commitment to growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy, will encourage future investment right across this region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14103/22-27, why her Department did not seek advice from relevant Executive Office officials when deciding if the June Monitoring Round was in line with pre-election guidance.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided for AQW 13831/22-27. Having considered the guidance provided by the Executive Office further advice was not considered necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there will be an October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: Yes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the omission of the mention of the Advocate General for Northern Ireland from Article 12 of the Criminal Attempts and Conspiracy (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 has prevented any prosecution or in any other way impeded justice.
answer_text: The omission of the mention of the Advocate General for Northern Ireland from Article 12 of the Criminal Attempts and Conspiracy (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 has not prevented any prosecution or impeded justice in any way.  To date, this legislative anomaly has been dealt with through engagement between the Advocate General’s Office and the Public Prosecution Service.   The Office of the Advocate General has provided consent to prosecution for the relevant offences at the Public Prosecution Service’s request.  Provisions in the new Justice Bill will make a technical change to correct this legislative anomaly which was never intended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether an instance of domestic abuse can be recorded as such when it involves (i) a perpetrator under the age of 18; and (ii) a victim under the age of 18.
answer_text: Regardless of the age of the victim or perpetrator, if the incident or crime meets the definition of domestic abuse it will be recorded as domestic motivated. The PSNI has adopted the definition of domestic abuse as outlined in the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031:“threatening, controlling, coercive behaviour, violence or abuse (psychological, virtual, physical, verbal, sexual, financial or emotional) inflicted on anyone (irrespective of age, ethnicity, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation or any form of disability) by a current or former intimate partner or family member.”  While not all domestic abuse incidents will result in the recording of a crime, all crime will be recorded as having a domestic abuse motivation where it meets the relevant definition provided above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any recent discussions she has had with officials in relation to the need for a review of the regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer given to AQW 15532/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 7057/22-27, and in light of the recommendation from the Council of Europe Committee of Experts on compliance with the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages that it is essential that Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition is required to be a speaker of Ulster Scots, whether they can confirm this will be a requirement of the Commissioners post.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.As previously advised, we have instructed our officials to prepare additional advice to enable the competitions to be launched. The eligibility criteria and person specification for each post will be detailed in the competition documentation. The competitions will be run in line with the Commissioner for Public Appointments guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the operation of Section 9(3) of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to communicate public messages around ongoing traffic disruption in Belfast.
answer_text: Traffic congestion in Belfast occurring in the mornings and during the evening rush hour is as a result of a £340 million investment in the new Grand Central station, and part of it is as a result of the £3.2 million that is being spent on upgrading the Sydenham bypass. This is a welcome and necessary investment in our infrastructure for the economic wellbeing of us all.Your rural colleagues are looking on in envy at Belfast's receipt of £3.2 million for road improvement and a £340 million bus and rail station. Your rural colleagues are asking, "What are you complaining about?".  However, I accept that there has been traffic congestion in Belfast. We are reviewing that. If lessons need to be learned, they will be learned. We are resurfacing a major arterial route into Belfast and the reason that we are resurfacing it is that it is such a high-volume part of the road network. I suspect that most Members travel on it daily. It needs to be upgraded and resurfaced — that is why we are doing it.My Department has been issuing public messages through a number of channels to ensure the public are kept fully informed about traffic conditions in the Greater Belfast area.The Traffic Information and Control Centre continually monitors traffic flows using a network of over 200 CCTV cameras.  Information on any incidents, roadworks or abnormal traffic conditions is uploaded to the TrafficWatchNI website and to their X (twitter) account which has almost 85,000 followers.Extensive electronic message signs, both permanent and temporary, are also used to warn the public of upcoming road works and potential delays on the network.My Department has issued press releases and social media posts through the DfI Twitter and Facebook accounts and worked closely with the media to communicate these messages to the public.My Department has also been working closely with Translink and their contractor to ensure all information relating to roadworks associated the construction of the Belfast Grand Central Station is communicated to a wide audience using press releases, media briefings, social media and local community focus groups.I also informed the Assembly, through the Speaker’s office, of the likelihood of increased traffic congestion in April 2024 due to works associated with Belfast Grand Central Station.My officials and I have also taken the opportunity during media interviews to keep the public informed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the timeline for the publication of an updated Together: Building a United Community strategy.
answer_text: We refer the member to the answer given in AQW 16299/22-27.

answer_text: I am due to meet with Sir Patrick Vallance, Minister for Science, Innovation and Technology and will raise this matter with him.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of meetings that took place in relation to the Budget Sustainability Plan; and (ii) whether it was discussed at the Executive.
answer_text: Officials engaged extensively with their Treasury counterparts and Departments on the development of the Budget Sustainability Plan.  The Plan was also raised at Ministerial bilateral meetings and as part of the engagement with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and Chancellor. The Finance Committee took evidence from officials on four occasions across three weeks on the budget Sustainability Plan.  Yes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of his comments in the media on 26 September 2024 in relation to the arts sector, and that it was not just for extra grants or add on or something that's fluffy, but a real investment in our people, which will have a positive economic return, how he came to the decision not to renew the Employment and Skills Initiative, Art Work.
answer_text: The Employment and Skills Initiative was one of a range of incentives supported by my Department under Covid Recovery arrangements.  Funded from the 21/22 budget, the Art Work programme received c£4.6m over a fixed three year period to address labour market barriers faced by individuals and organisations arising from the impact of the pandemic on the arts and creative economy.No decision was made in relation to renewing the scheme, as this Covid recovery related initiative was a timebound intervention and will end in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many organisations he has declined to meet since taking office.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on traffic calming measures at Orpen Park, Finaghy.
answer_text: The review of the proposed traffic calming measures at Orpen Park, Finaghy is ongoing and has taken longer than expected due to pressures on my staff resources. I have however asked officials, subject to other priority work, to bring this consultation to a conclusion as soon as possible.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of public money his Department has spent on exploring traffic calming arrangements for Orpen Park, Finaghy, inclusive of (i) staff hours; (ii) consulting; and (iii) stakeholder engagement, since the 2019-20 financial year.
answer_text: My officials have advised that development work has been carried out internally within my Department and as such, staff hours / stakeholder engagement are not recorded against specific schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the £45 million announced to support Local Economic Partnerships is new money.
answer_text: The dedicated funding to support the Local Economic Partnerships, as part of the Sub-Regional Economic Plan, will be financed from within the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected financial saving for not renewing the Employment and Skills Initiative, Art Work.
answer_text: The Employment and Skills Initiative was one of a range of incentives supported by my Department under Covid Recovery arrangements.  Funded from the 21/22 budget, the Art Work programme received c£4.6m over a fixed three year period to address labour market barriers faced by individuals and organisations arising from the impact of the pandemic on the arts and creative economy.No financial savings will be achieved, as this Covid recovery related initiative was a timebound intervention and will end in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any investments and financial holdings held by the Northern Ireland Civil Service Pension Schemes in Israel, including (i) actively managed equities; (ii) actively managed fixed income including any government or corporate bonds; (iii) passively managed equities; (iv) passively managed fixed incoming including any government or corporate bonds; and (v) any other investments including real estate investment trusts, direct funding provided to commercial lenders or any other financial investment or product.
answer_text: The main NICS Pension Scheme is an unfunded defined benefit scheme and operates on what is known as a 'pay-as-you- go' basis. This means that no investment fund is built up or held. Pensions are paid from AME, funded by payroll contributions from employees and employers, plus additional funding from the Exchequer, as required. Therefore, the NICS pension scheme has no income, holdings or investments of the types specified.All new NICS employees are auto- enrolled in the main defined benefit scheme.  Should a member wish to opt out of the scheme the NICS also provides access to two alternative workplace pension defined contribution arrangements:The Partnership pension accountA Stakeholder Pension accountScottish Widows and Standard Life are the current independent pension providers for the NICS defined contribution arrangements. Both are regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority. In the Partnership arrangement only, the individual’s employer is required to make an age related contribution to the chosen provider on their behalf. Money invested in either of the available defined contribution options is not under the control of the Civil Service and it does it have any related income, holdings or investments of the types specified. The most recent superannuation accounts for the NICS pension schemes are published at: Superannuation and Other Allowances Resource Accounts 2023-24 (finance-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will publish the Budget Sustainability Plan.
answer_text: The Budget Sustainability Plan was published on 03 October 2024.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/budget-sustainability-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether holding a driving licence is a requirement for practising midwifery.
answer_text: Rotational or community midwifery roles do require a driving licence to meet the full needs of the post.  However, this requirement will be waived for any applicant whose disability prohibits driving but has access to a form of transport approved by the Trust, which permits them to carry out the duties of the post.Any post-specific flexibilities or exceptions will be stated in recruitment advertisements and job specifications issued by Trusts.

answer_text: To date, there have been no applications made under Section 9 (3) of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action he is taking to help those in further education who have (i) dyslexia; and (ii) dyscalculia.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is committed to removing barriers to further education for students diagnosed with learning difficulties and/or disabilities, including those with dyslexia and dyscalculia. Under the Special Educational Needs and Disability (NI) Order 2005 (SENDO), further education colleges are required to make reasonable adjustments for students with learning difficulties and/or disabilities, so that they can access provision. The Department assists further education colleges in fulfilling this obligation by providing funding to support students with learning difficulties and/or disabilities through the Additional Support Fund. The Additional Support Fund provides £4.5 million funding to colleges annually to assist in the provision of additional support to enable students to complete a course of study and/or progress to further study, training, employment or to independent living.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he proposed the inclusion of a Green New Deal in the draft Programme for Government.
answer_text: Further to AQW 11659/22-27 I intend to seek Executive approval of the draft Green Growth strategy shortly. Following adoption of the Strategy, I would then be keen to explore with Executive Ministers the potential to make the case for a Green New Deal for Northern Ireland. This of course would be cross cutting and would require an agreed position.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support being offered to Lough View Integrated Primary and Nursery School following recent security alerts.
answer_text: As a result of several security incidents over recent years at Henry Jones Playing Fields, a project was delivered to upgrade the CCTV system at Lough View Integrated Primary and Nursery School. This scheme completed on 2 August 2024 and the system is operational.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has sought any briefing on potential legislative interventions in relation to financial services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As the Member is aware, this is a Reserved matter. I receive briefings on a range of matters, including in relation to the operation of financial services locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has responsibility for consumer protection, including in relation to the sale of concert tickets.
answer_text: My Department's Trading Standards Service (TSS) is responsible for enforcing over 160 pieces of consumer protection legislation, covering a wide range of sectors and practices. TSS helps to promote and maintain a fair trading environment, protect consumers and enable businesses to thrive.Additionally, the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland (CCNI), an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, has a statutory remit to promote and safeguard the interests of consumers and has specific functions in relation to energy, water, transport, food and postal services.The main consumer protection legislation relevant to the sale of concert tickets is the Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 (CPRs). The CPRs aim to protect consumers from unfair commercial practices such as misleading claims and other harmful online selling practices by sellers or resellers of tickets for events. The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) is currently investigating concerns regarding the sale of Oasis concert tickets by Ticketmaster, including how so-called ‘dynamic pricing’ may have been used. Ticketmaster: consumer protection case - GOV.UK (www.gov.uk)Trading Standards and CCNI engage with the CMA on a regular basis and can act on the findings of any investigations as appropriate.If consumers have any specific concerns or questions about concert tickets, they should contact Consumerline on Freephone 0300 123 62 62 or by email to Consumerline@economy-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress to deliver the short-term schemes 2022-25, which are due to have been completed under the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022-2031.
answer_text: A number of schemes on the Belfast Cycling Network (BCN) have been completed since its publication in March 2022. Sections of the Forth Meadow Community Greenway and a section within Phase 1 of the Beechill Greenway have been delivered in partnership with the Belfast City Council. My Department has also completed an innovative, environmentally friendly, street lighting scheme along the urban section of the Comber Greenway. Officials have advised that the Lagmore Avenue BCN scheme and West Belfast Greenway Phase 1a are programmed to commence construction this financial year. Statutory processes are underway or are being prepared for the Montgomery Road, Sydenham Greenway Phase 1, and Island Street schemes.  Design work is ongoing on the majority of the other short-term schemes and my officials are aiming to progress as many of these as possible in the time period up to the end of 2025, subject to staff resource, budget availability, and the successful completion of statutory processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of spend on recently-announced road improvement projects, to include detail on cycling lanes and any other cycling infrastructure budgeted within these improvement works.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are primarily delivered to improve the condition and integrity of the road network and provide benefits to all road users, including cyclists, using the new surface. Where cycle lanes exist within the extent of a resurfacing scheme, a new surface course will normally be provided, on a like for like basis, as part of the scheme. Officials within the Department also engage internally when developing their programmes and will collaborate to deliver resurfacing alongside improvements to active travel infrastructure where there is merit in doing so.In the region of £200,000 will be spent on cycling facilities on the recently announced Sydenham By-pass resurfacing scheme.None of the other road improvement projects, as defined in your question’s submission, have any existing or proposed cycling infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce barriers and waiting lists for those awaiting a (i) dyslexia; and (ii) dyscalculia assessment.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce barriers and waiting lists for those awaiting a (i) dyslexia; and (ii) dyscalculia assessment.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) do not routinely screen for dyslexia or dyscalculia. A school’s Special Educational Needs Co-Ordinator (SENCO) should be familiar with a wide range of assessment tools which assist them in identifying individual children’s learning strengths and weaknesses, including a measure of severity of symptoms of literacy or numeracy difficulties including dyslexia and dyscalculia. Where specific learning difficulties in literacy or numeracy have been identified within an EA psychology assessment, the Educational Psychologist will recommend to the school a range of strategies and resources after considering those previously used by the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he or his officials have met with Baroness Foster in her role at Intertrade UK.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he is actively seeking to introduce United States of America immigration pre-clearance; and (ii) whether it is an agreed Executive policy.
answer_text: (i) As civil aviation is a reserved matter, my officials have explored with the Department for Transport (DfT) the possibility of extending pre-clearance services to Belfast International Airport. They advised that the decision on whether to operate such facilities at airports overseas ultimately rests with the US authorities.The British government has committed to entering into exploratory talks with the United States Administration. My officials will do all they can to facilitate these discussions.(ii) In the draft Programme for Government a commitment is given that the Executive will work with the British Government to support pre-clearance between the United States and Belfast International Airport, promoting international investment and innovation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the delay in the full opening of Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: It was planned to have an operational opening of the station in early Autumn 2024, and Translink are still on course for this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQT 532/22-27, to detail (i) how the Education Authority has failed controlled schools; and (ii) the evidence that supports this view.
answer_text: In my recent statement to the Assembly, I reflected that the current system was failing controlled schools, due to its construct, as per the findings of the Independent Review. This Review highlighted that:“The managing authority role has always been a challenge because the EA also provides a wide range of services to all other schools. This results in complicated systems for school management, which are, in particular, suboptimal for the Controlled sector. This has been compounded by the terms of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which imposes on the EA the duty to support integrated education”.“Furthermore, the Integrated Education Act 2022 makes it difficult for the EA to properly represent the interests of Controlled schools in the area planning process, given its new obligation to support Integrated schooling”.“Principals in the Controlled sector have suggested that the EA is stretched too thin and is conflicted by servicing all school types, leaving Controlled schools relatively unsupported. At the very least this would indicate that sectors should be supported with greater consistency and equity”.“Our consultations with senior school staff, particularly in the Controlled sector produced a consistent pattern of criticism of the EA. The strong message from school leaders is that they want customer-focused, responsive and value-for-money services to meet the needs of their school and their learners. This is not their current experience”.“Principals offered severe criticism of services provided in relation to minor works, maintenance and procurement as well as support services for learners with SEN. They have stated that the EA is not sufficiently customer focused or, perhaps, it is not entirely clear who its customers are. There is widespread frustration because of a perceived lack of responsiveness…It is essential that schools be kept aware of appropriate named contacts and that, where appropriate, this be done on a geographical basis”.The Review also referenced the recent landscape review of the EA, noting this “produced similar findings and highlighted many of the same failings. The landscape review report made 13 recommendations on the themes of future form, function and financing of the EA; improvements required to support organisational effectiveness; and governance, accountability and relationships. The report indicated that matters have improved over recent years but that many aspects of the operation of the EA remain profoundly unsatisfactory”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any other justice department in the UK or Ireland, either sovereign or devolved, does not have responsibility for any part of civil law reform.
answer_text: It is not my Department’s responsibility to conduct an analysis of the remits of other justice departments and it therefore does not hold this information.

The interim Board has not provided any further detail or assessment of the proposals received at this stage.
