question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps he will take to reduce congestion on Ravenhill Road, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the traffic congestion on the Ravenhill Road, Belfast during peak traffic flows similar to many roads within the Belfast area.A key aspect of my Department’s transport policy is to encourage use of sustainable transport modes as an alternative to the private car by providing improved facilities for walking, cycling and public transport.My Department is currently developing the detailed design for improved cycle facilities and active travel infrastructure along the Ravenhill Road, from the junction with Ormeau Embankment to the roundabout at Rosetta. The proposed cycle scheme is included within the short-term schemes within the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022-31. The provision of this proposed active travel infrastructure will encourage more people to walk, wheel and cycle for shorter, everyday journeys instead of using the car.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of (i) the decision to make Belfast Grand Central Station a silent station; and (ii) the impact this decision will have on those who are registered blind.
answer_text: There are several reasons for Belfast Grand Central Station being a quiet station*. On a practical level there are hundreds of services departing the station daily and to have an announcement for each would just create a cacophony of constant noise that would be impossible to decipher.  Translink engaged with relevant stakeholders such as RNIB, GuideDogsNI and IMTAC from the outset of this project and made them aware of this approach. It was noted that so many service announcements can be counterproductive and confusing for not only the blind and visually impaired communities but also distressing for those with sensory issues.The station is designed to be a welcoming, relaxing space for everyone and also aims to be a place for people to meet and have conversations.  Translink continues to work with key stakeholders will be reactive to accessible aspects of the station based on user feedback. *The station will not be fully silent. Announcements will be made by exception (e.g. service changes).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Defective Premises Bill will receive Royal Assent.
answer_text: The Act received Royal Assent on 20th September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of his Department’s action plan, whether the (i) Wild Atlantic Way; (ii) Ancient East; and (iii) Hidden Heartlands brands will be extended into the North by January 2025.
answer_text: Extending the three experience brands, developed by Fáilte Ireland, into the North is one of my key priorities to help deliver regionally balanced economic growth for the tourism sector.Following my engagement with Minister Martin from the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media, a new All Island Strategic Tourism Group has been established. This group will take forward consideration of the extension of Fáilte Ireland’s regional branding, and other key priority areas of co-ordination for the tourism sector. Its membership comprises officials from my Department, the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media and the Chief Executive Officers of Tourism Ireland, Tourism Northern Ireland and Fáilte Ireland. The group met for the first time on 4 September 2024 and there has been ongoing dialogue on this particular issue. I have asked the Strategic Tourism Group to work at pace to ensure implementation at the earliest possible time.  There has already been significant progress as part of a €7.6m Shared Island Wild Atlantic Way / Causeway Coastal Route Tourism Brand Collaboration Project and which is a joint initiative in partnership with Tourism NI, Fáilte Ireland and Tourism Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered reviewing the eligibility criteria for school transport to (i) remove the requirement to apply to schools in order of nearest suitable school; (ii) build in financial assistance for families on low incomes; and (iii) update the distance measurement to safe travel on foot.
answer_text: (i) (ii) A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. It is important to note that the transport policy supports other policies within the Department, such as parental preference and Special Educational Needs (SEN). Work is progressing on an end-to-end review of SEN and work is also underway on an action plan arising from the EA Landscape Review, to include detailed consideration of the operational delivery of services such as school transport. I have also recently received advice on the Independent Review of Education from officials and I will announce a response to the recommendations in the coming weeks. Any review of the Home to School Transport Policy should complement and build on the outcomes from these workstreams and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24. Any proposals for policy change would need to be carefully scoped out and costed within the context of a detailed policy review and would likely require changes to legislation. (iii) The home to school transport policy clearly states that the term “walking distance” should not be interpreted as an injunction upon parents that their child must walk to school, rather it is used to determine whether responsibility for assisting a pupil with transport to and from school lies with the parent or with the EA. The qualifying distances of 2 miles for pupils registered at a primary school and 3 miles for any other child are laid out in the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.  The EA must measure the distance from home to school by the nearest available route, which is the route along which the child could walk to school with reasonable safety when accompanied by an adult. Where parents are concerned that the route is not safe, they may make a transport appeal to the EA on that basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has given consideration to expanding the eligibility criteria for accessible parking bay applications for those under-65 to include anyone who holds a blue badge and receives the higher rate mobility component of Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: A Blue Badge holder under 65 years of age with any form of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) award for the mobility component, either the standard rate (8-11 points), or the enhanced rate (12 points or more), is already eligible to apply for an accessible parking bay, so the change you suggest to the criteria is not needed.Information on the accessible parking bay application process including the eligibility criteria is on NIDirect and can be viewed using this link: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/accessible-parking-bays

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has sought any oral or written briefings on the development of (i) the site known as Tribeca; and (ii) Crumlin Road Courthouse.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps his Department is taking to address the skills shortage in trades, particularly car mechanics.
answer_text: Our skills ecosystem offers many opportunities for people to undertake training in trades including car mechanic skills.  A range of trades and in particular, motor vehicle pathways are available through Apprenticeships at Levels 2 and 3 and Traineeships at Level 2.  Colleges also deliver a range of programmes at levels 4 and 5 in car mechanics and related subjects. The Department established a Motor Vehicle Sectoral Partnership in 2019 to engage with industry to help ensure our apprenticeships and vocational education pathways align with industry needs.  Colleges have also invested significantly in resourcing the curriculum for trades and motor vehicle with up-to-date equipment and learning facilities. The Skills Focus programme provides employers with the opportunity to upskill employees in trades. Last year there were 322 enrolments onto the programme, for accredited qualifications in the automotive industry.  The Skill Up programme which is funded by my Department supports training in several related courses including Electric Vehicles and welding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether an Apprenticeship Action Plan, promised by August 2024, has been published.
answer_text: My ambition is to expand the pipeline of future talent for business, as well as skills and employment opportunities for citizens through delivery of a three-year Apprenticeship Action Plan.  Delivery of early actions commenced during the summer, this includes a new Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund. Engagement has been underway for a number of months with a range of stakeholders through the development phase, with introductory stakeholder events currently underway. In parallel, officials have commenced the development of an Apprenticeship Action plan to set out our work to grow and enhance our apprenticeship provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the proportion of Northern Ireland Investment Fund projects that have, either in whole or part, included hotel developments.
answer_text: The Fund has supported 14 projects to date, of which 6 are hotels or aparthotels.  The total value of loans committed to these projects is £62,060,000, which represents 36.66% of the £169,283,000 in loans committed to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of meetings she has had with groups representing Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic communities since July 2024.
answer_text: The anti-migrant sentiment we have witnessed in Northern Ireland in recent months and the overtly anti-Islamic element of the targeted attacks on members of minority ethnic communities, their homes and businesses is of particular concern. In this context, engagement with representatives of Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic communities has become particularly important and I have welcomed recent opportunities to meet with representatives of a range of organisations. I have met with the following organisations in recent months: representatives from the African and Caribbean Support Organisation Northern Ireland (ACSONI); Belfast Islamic Centre; the National Black Police Association (NBPA) and Ethnic Minority Police Association (EMPA). Officials also met with the Mid and East Antrim Inter Ethnic Forum.These meetings provided valuable opportunities to listen to the concerns of individuals and organisations, to offer reassurance and reaffirm the Department’s commitment to creating communities where each person feels safe and valued.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any estimate that has been made of the additionality, on any economic metric, created by Northern Ireland Investment Fund projects since 2020.
answer_text: Since its initial launch in 2017, the Investment Fund has provided close to £170m in loans and leveraged a further £170m in private sector investment, which has resulted in over 5,000 jobs being supported, the construction of 1,100 additional hotel beds, as well as progressing over 100,000 sq m of employment space.While the level of additionality associated with projects supported by the Investment Fund is not recorded it is clear, given the Fund supports proposals that would not otherwise have been financed, then these projects would not have proceeded or would not have proceeded in the same time period or to the same scale had they not been supported by the Fund.The Fund shows how the public and private sector can work together to support schemes that make a real difference to communities and people’s lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the impact on Lough Neagh and other waterways, whether he has made any direct representations or proposals to the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to investment in wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: The funding of Northern Ireland Water is a matter for the Minister for Infrastructure. DAERA and DfI work closely together and DfI alongside NI Water, were key stakeholders in the development of the DAERA Lough Neagh Action Plan. Investment need for the delivery on NI Water wastewater assets across Northern Ireland, which includes those assets around Lough Neagh, has already been identified, as part of the price control process. This is subject to budget availability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any new legislative or regulatory actions, not including scoping work or consultation, proposed in the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
answer_text: You will be aware there are actions in the Lough Neagh Action Plan that will potentially require the introduction of new legislation or regulatory actions. It would not be appropriate at this time to pre-empt the detail. My department will seek appropriate advice to determine details of new legislative or regulatory actions required as delivery of the Lough Neagh Action Plan progresses. Any new legislative or regulatory actions will be advised to the Assembly through standing Assembly processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to the recommendations outlined in the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges the recommendations outlined within the report and how these can help to deliver better outcomes for those living with blood cancer. However, my Department was not consulted or involved in the development of this particular publication.My Department has undertaken service reviews across the core haematology cancer pathways (Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia) in Northern Ireland. The haematology pathway reviews have identified a range of recommendations that will help transform the future delivery of haematology services. Work is ongoing to progress the implementation of the haematology review recommendations and is dependent on the complexity and scale of the review recommendations, available resources and other existing commissioning priorities.I remain fully committed to the implementation of the NI Cancer Strategy and to working collaboratively with all stakeholders to transform our cancer services that will deliver better outcomes for all of those impacted by a cancer diagnosis in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will publish the findings of the report into the trialled residents' parking scheme.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and I have had the opportunity to consider the draft review report.  I plan to issue the final review report soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department intends to continue the Neurology Deceased Patients Review until they have reviewed the records of every former patient of Dr Michael Watt who was treated by him in the final decade of his practice and is now deceased.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the efforts his Department has made to contact the next of kin of deceased patients of Dr Michael Watt to alert them about Phase Three of the Neurology Deceased Patients Review; and (ii) whether they can request their loved one be included in this phase.
answer_text: On 3 July 2024, I announced the commencement a further phase of the Neurology Deceased Patient Review (Phase Three). Phase Three comprises 18 families which have approached the RQIA and confirmed that they wish to proceed with an Expert Review in relation to a deceased family member. The primary purpose of this review is to provide the opportunity to impart answers, if possible, to the families in respect of the quality of care and treatment provided to their deceased relatives.Further to this, a six-month period with a close point of 31 December 2024, has been provided for any additional families who are interested in having their deceased family member’s clinical records reviewed to contact the RQIA to explore involvement in the process.The RQIA will engage with any affected family who makes contact by 31 December 2024. Following this process, the Department of Health and the RQIA will clarify next steps for the Neurology Deceased Patients’ Review.Consideration of further phases will be guided by a number of factors including the learnings from the work completed to date (including family expectations and experiences, ethical considerations and systems learning), the time requirements for future work, and the resource requirements (including staff and budget requirements).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the Budget Sustainability Plan, due to be published in August 2024, will be published.
answer_text: Work by my officials has continued over the summer with the Budget Sustainability Plan now close to being concluded.While it was originally due for publication by the end of August, the pre-election period limited Treasury’s ability to engage with us. I therefore agreed a short extension to the deadline with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to the end of September 2024.As the Budget Sustainability Plan is a cross-cutting publication, this extension to the deadline was crucial to ensure that the Executive is given the time to review and agree the Budget Sustainability Plan before publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether phase three of the Neurology Deceased Patient Review will be the final phase of the review.
answer_text: On 3 July 2024, I announced the commencement a further phase of the Neurology Deceased Patient Review (Phase Three). Phase Three comprises 18 families which have approached the RQIA and confirmed that they wish to proceed with an Expert Review in relation to a deceased family member. The primary purpose of this review is to provide the opportunity to impart answers, if possible, to the families in respect of the quality of care and treatment provided to their deceased relatives.Further to this, a six-month period with a close point of 31 December 2024, has been provided for any additional families who are interested in having their deceased family member’s clinical records reviewed to contact the RQIA to explore involvement in the process.The RQIA will engage with any affected family who makes contact by 31 December 2024. Following this process, the Department of Health and the RQIA will clarify next steps for the Neurology Deceased Patients’ Review.Consideration of further phases will be guided by a number of factors including the learnings from the work completed to date (including family expectations and experiences, ethical considerations and systems learning), the time requirements for future work, and the resource requirements (including staff and budget requirements).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the advice he received; and (ii) the groups and individuals he consulted with, before taking the decision to ban puberty blockers.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider increasing the funding to Shopmobility Northern Ireland to meet their needs.
answer_text: I have made the decision to provide funding to Shopmobility Northern Ireland for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24 following consideration of the responses to the Budget Equality Impact Assessment.  I have approved an increase in funding for the front line Shopmobility Schemes based on total levels awarded for 2024/25.My department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and the level of funding this year has led to a situation where even maintaining existing funding is challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Shopmobility Northern Ireland assists those travelling for medical appointments, what consideration he has given to providing funding to allow them to continue this service.
answer_text: Core Funding for the Shopmobility schemes is provided by The Department of Infrastructure under Transport Programme for People with Disabilities (TPPD).  The Department of Health funds a number of schemes through Shopmobility Belfast, with BHSCT for the Royal Victoria Hospital and SEHCST for the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald which assist those travelling for medical appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will announce his next steps in relation to the social inclusion strategies.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current provision for adoptees to access healthcare records pertaining to their birth parents and any medical conditions that might impact them.
answer_text: When considering adoption for a child, an adoption agency is required by law to obtain and keep a record of information about the child’s health. It is also required to arrange any physical/medical examinations and screening for the child and—as far as reasonably practicable (dependent on parental engagement and consent) - the child’s birth parents, as may be recommended by the agency’s medical adviser. As far as it is practicable, a birth family health history should also be obtained, including details of any serious physical or mental illness and any inherited and congenital disease. This information is required to be placed in the child’s case record and preserved for at least 75 years. The adoption agency has discretion to disclose information to adopted adults seeking access to their information.  The release of information will take into account the need to balance the welfare and interests of the adopted person with those of any third parties whose information may be held.Any medical information held in adoption records may be limited to information which was known at the time of adoption. When a child is placed for adoption, he or she is issued with a new Health and Care number. Processes are in place to advise relevant health care settings of the child’s new name and health and care number, and to arrange for the transfer of relevant medical records. Through the ongoing development and implementation of a digital health and care record system, the Department is working to ensure that a continuous record of care both pre- and post-adoption is maintained, while at the same time ensuring that all necessary steps are taken to respect the privacy of the adopted person and their family.To assist those who have been adopted to access their adoption record, including any information it contains relating to the health records of birth parents, my Department issued ‘Access to Birth Records and Adoption Agency Case Records for Adults Impacted by Adoption to Health and Social Care Trusts and Adoption Agencies – Practice Guidance’ on 29 November 2023, for immediate implementation.  This guidance was developed by experts by experience, including adoptees and birth mothers, working alongside experienced adoption organisations. It provides clear processes and best practice guidelines which aim to ensure that people affected by adoption have access to as much information about themselves and their families as possible; and that access is provided in a fair, balanced and transparent way. My Department also engaged with adoption organisations throughout the passage of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which introduces a new legal framework for disclosure of information. When implemented, it will provide for a new and more consistent approach to access to information held in adoption agency records and by the Registrar General about adoptions which take place after the Act comes into operation. 		For adoptions, which took place before t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to address addiction care.
answer_text: The Department of Health developed a Strategy for Substance Use in 2021 ‘Making Life Better, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’. This Strategy outlines a number of recommendations required to support the population with the challenges presented by the use of drugs and alcohol.One of the recommendations within the Strategy is that ‘the PHA and HSCB (now Strategic Planning and Performance Group SPPG) will produce, across all tiers of service a new outcome focussed strategic plan, to replace the Alcohol and Drugs Commissioning Framework’.This Plan has now been developed as led by both the SPPG and PHA. The Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan for Substance Use 2024-2028 has been developed following considerable involvement with stakeholders including people with lived and living experience, the community and voluntary sector, HSC Trusts and agencies within education, housing and justice.The Plan has been subject to a 12-week public consultation period and outlines a number of key strategic priority areas and associated actions for the short, medium and longer term over a four-year period. The 8 key strategic priorities identified within the Plan will, when implemented, reform our current service provision to ensure that people impacted by the use of substances receive the right support, at the right time, in the right place by the most appropriate person and service.Officials are currently working with key stakeholders on the implementation of the priorities identified in the Plan which will be actioned over a 4-year period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14155/22-27, to detail (i) the dates when Land and Property Services received the 23 ratepayer applications for charitable exemption; (ii) the dates of the property inspection undertaken by the District Valuer for each property; and (iii) the effective decision date for each application, quoting the related Property ID and the Unique Property Reference Number for each property.
To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14155/22-27, for a breakdown of the 45 properties referred to, including the Property ID and the Unique Property Reference Number for each property.
To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14154/22-27, why the date of 1 January 2019 was chosen.
answer_text: Land & Property Services carried out non-domestic revaluation exercises in 2020 and 2023 the outcome of which reflected the wider macro-economic market changes on Net Annual Values (NAVs) in the Valuation List since 2019, particularly due to the impact of Covid.It was considered reasonable in such circumstances to reflect the Valuation List history over a five-year period from 1 January 2019 to present day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12016/22-27, for an update on the Strategic Review of the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute’s land and property holdings.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of AFBI Land and Property Holdings is nearing conclusion. Consideration is now being given to how the Recommendations emerging from the Review can be progressed. This includes ongoing considerations around the disposal of land at the Newforge Lane site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce legislation to address the rise in drink spiking.
answer_text: Spiking in any form is an abhorrent act and is to be condemned in the strongest possible terms, I encourage anyone affected to report the matter to the police at the earliest opportunity. 		There are currently laws in place to deal with this type of behaviour. Depending on the circumstances, spiking can be dealt with as an offence under the Offences against the Person Act 1861 (the 1861 Act). In spiking incidents where sexual assault is a factor, the Sexual Offences (NI) Order 2008, which makes it an offence to cause a person to engage in any type of sexual activity without consent, may be applicable.  Depending on the circumstances of the case, offenders can be liable to a maximum of life imprisonment.I am however cognisant of the discussions surrounding the UK Governments plans to amend the law around spiking. The last Government had intended to update the provisions within the 1861 Act to recast the current offences and provide clarity, without any doubt, that spiking is illegal and it had been my intention to seek a legislative consent motion to extend these changes to Northern Ireland. However, this proposal fell with the change of Government.  My officials remain engaged with UK officials on this issue and on any alternative proposal being developed on this issue, which will help inform the approach taken here in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to reduce the age at which bowel screening is offered.
answer_text: As you may be aware, Action 7 of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. Currently, in Northern Ireland, men and women aged 60-74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for screening. Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range and further reduce the sensitivity level is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity and any treatments that may be required, are aligned. As a first step, the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in April 2023, reduced the screening FIT threshold from 150 micrograms Hb/g faeces to 120 micrograms Hb/g faeces. Collaborative work has been ongoing between the Public Health Agency (PHA) and Queen’s University Belfast on the development of a bowel cancer screening model (taking into account both age extension and further reduction in FIT threshold). This model will contribute to informing the future approach, including how these are best phased into the programme to maximise benefits and outcomes and to provide more detailed cost information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact on economic performance of preferential access under the NI Protocol, compared to that of GB.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken analysis, informed by regulatory indices developed by the UK Trade Policy Observatory, to better understand the opportunities that our unique dual market access provides for particular types of products and sectors. This was published on 21 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether all assets qualifying for rates relief in the Tribeca site boundary are being occupied by the appropriate qualifying entities.
answer_text: Occupiers of properties that are granted Charitable Exemption, Sport & Recreation relief, Industrial Derating, Non-domestic Vacant Relief and Freight Transport relief, are awarded the support after a process of application, assessment, and decision by the District Valuer on the basis of the facts and evidence in each case.  		Since 01/01/2019 the District Valuer has considered 23 ratepayer applications for Charitable Exemption and a further 22 properties where there has been a change of occupation on an Exempt property within the boundaries of the Tribeca site. 		In total LPS has reviewed the rateable distinguishments on 45 properties, of which 20 have met the threshold for Exemption, and 25 have not.		LPS is currently reviewing procedures to ensure occupier changes on property benefiting from such reliefs are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether Land and Property Services have carried out the required regular inspections of each asset in the Tribeca site boundary to ensure the relief being granted is appropriate, qualifying and continuing since its initial grant.
answer_text: Occupiers of properties that are granted Charitable Exemption, Sport & Recreation relief, Industrial Derating, Non-domestic Vacant Relief and Freight Transport relief, are awarded the support after a process of application, assessment, and decision by the District Valuer on the basis of the facts and evidence in each case.  	LPS Revenue & Benefits will inform the District Valuer when a ratepayer change has taken place that may affect the relief awarded, and the District Valuer will review the use, occupation and entitlement to relief on the property. Rates will be payable from the date of removal of the entitlement to the relief. 	Since 01/01/2019 the District Valuer has considered 23 ratepayer applications for Charitable Exemption on property within the boundaries of the Tribeca site. The District Valuer has concluded that Exemption from rates was warranted in 15 cases, based on the two key factors of use and occupation of the property at the date of each application. In 8 cases the threshold for Exemption as set out under Article 41 of the Rates Order was not met, and Exemption from rates was not approved.Since 01/01/2019 LPS has reviewed the Exemption of a further 22 properties within the Tribeca area where a change of occupation has occurred. This has resulted in Exemption being removed from 17 properties, with the remaining 5 still considered to be eligible. 	In total since 01/01/2019 LPS has reviewed 45 properties, of which 20 have met the threshold for Exemption, and 25 have not.		LPS is currently reviewing procedures to ensure changes of occupier on property benefiting from such reliefs are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13831/22-27, whether advice was sought from the Head of the Civil Service, or her office, in relation to the impact of pre-election period guidance on the timing of the June monitoring round.
answer_text: Both I, and my officials, considered the guidance that the Executive Office issued for Civil Servants and Special Advisors on conduct during the pre-election period. No further advice was sought.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail why her departmental officials attend the UK Strategic Lawsuits Against Public Participation taskforce despite policy responsibility lying with another department.
answer_text: Legislative efforts in the UK Parliament to address Strategic Lawsuits Against Public Participation (SLAPPs) have been concentrated in the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023 ("the 2023 Act"), and the Strategic Litigation Against Public Participation Bill.Sections 194 and 195 of the 2023 Act, which extend to England and Wales only, set out a statutory definition of a SLAPP and provide for a power to make rules of court to allow claims to be struck out at an early stage and protect the defendant from paying costs. Due to the scope of the 2023 Act, the measures only apply to claims relating to economic crime. The Strategic Litigation Against Public Participation Bill, which fell at dissolution in May 2024, would have largely replicated these provisions, but apply them to claims relating to any issue. The Department of Justice is responsible for policy and legislation relating to the civil courts in Northern Ireland, so considering the approach adopted by the UK Parliament, the Department retains a significant interest in the work of the SLAPPs Taskforce. The Department of Finance is responsible for civil law reform, including in relation to defamation. However, not all SLAPPs relate to issues within the Department of Finance's remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value of fines levied under Article 17(2) of the Clean Air (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Article 17(2) of the Clean Air (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 is enforced by district councils in Northern Ireland.  Accordingly, my Department does not hold this information but I understand that it is held by and can be requested from Environmental Health departments within each local council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will introduce a workers' rights bill.
answer_text: It is my intention to launch an extensive public consultation on 1 July.  Beyond the closing of that consultation, I will need to review the responses from stakeholders and form conclusions on the way forward.  Policy decisions will need to be agreed by the Executive and the drafting of an Employment Rights Bill will take some time.  Given the likely scale of the task I estimate that the Bill will be introduced to the Assembly by January 2026.  If there is scope to introduce the bill at an earlier juncture, I will seek to do so.  Also, there may be changes I can make sooner via secondary legislation, following the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to review the (i) operation; and (ii) effectiveness of the Clean Air (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.
answer_text: Whilst there are no plans at present for my Department to review either the operation or the effectiveness of the Clean Air (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, I am content to engage with the member as required on any changes he feels would be merited.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether advice was sought from the Head of the Civil Service, or her office, in relation to the impact of the pre-election period on the timing of the June monitoring round.
answer_text: Both I, and my officials, have considered the guidance that the Executive Office issued for Civil Servants and Special Advisors on conduct during the pre-election period. The Department of Finance view is that the June Monitoring round is normal and routine business of the Executive, and it is therefore appropriate to proceed with the June Monitoring round during the pre-election period. I have written to the First Minister and deputy First Minister and to Executive colleagues to express this view. Indeed, Minister Long and Minister O’Dowd have also written highlighting the importance of agreeing June Monitoring as soon as possible. There is a twofold risk of not confirming the June Monitoring position as soon as possible. Departments may delay taking decisions in the hope of receiving an additional allocation – this delay could increase the risk of overspends or lead to greater adverse impacts than would have been the case had decisions been taken earlier. Conversely, departments may proceed with actions which will not be required if an additional allocation is confirmed. It is therefore important that the outcome of the June Monitoring round is agreed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to introduce air filters for classrooms.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 6296/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 23 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made an assessment of the Pharmacy First service and its potential application in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am happy to inform the member that the Pharmacy First service for Everyday Health Conditions has been operating in Northern Ireland since March 2022, replacing the previous Minor Ailments Service.  Referral into the Pharmacy First service includes referral from GP practices, the community pharmacist or self-referral by the patient. Pharmacy First for everyday health conditions facilitates the assessment, and treatment if appropriate, of 13 common conditions such as acne, mouth ulcers and verrucae. All community pharmacies are contracted to provide this service. In addition, the expansion of the service to a number of additional conditions has been explored as follows:The Pharmacy First Service for Emergency Hormonal Contraception (EHC) was introduced in July 2022. The service ensures that women and young people aged 13 years and over have timely access to sexual health service and free EHC (including the provision of bridging contraception) when clinically indicated. The service is currently provided by 392 community pharmacies. From July 2022 to March 23 11,829 women and young people have benefited from the service.  The Pharmacy First Pilot Service for Sore Throat in patients aged 5 years and over ran between November 2023 and March 2024 as part of the winter preparedness plan in 42 community pharmacies in NI. There were 6768 sore throat consultations under this pilot scheme. An evaluation of the pilot scheme is currently being undertaken by the Medicines Optimisation Innovation Centre (MOIC) with the evaluation report expected mid-summer 2024. Any decision to continue the sore throat service into 2024/25 or to roll it out more widely will be subject to the outcome of the evaluation by MOIC and will be subject to funding being available.The Pharmacy First Service for Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs) in women aged 16-64 years was rolled-out in Northern Ireland in November 2023 following a successful evaluation of the pilot scheme undertaken by MOIC. SPPG has written to community pharmacy contractors to confirm that funding for this service will continue while the budget for 2024/25 is awaited. Currently, 456 community pharmacies provide the UTI service and, since the launch, over 5,500 patients have benefited from the service.The Department of Health launched a Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy in May of this year; it describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030. One of these priorities is to expand the range of clinical services available through community pharmacies to improve access to patient care pathways for the diagnosis, treatment and prevention of illness. This will include the Pharmacy First Service, however, further development of this service in Northern Ireland is dependent on having an electronic platform and the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she has received detailed legal advice on the High Court judgment on the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) when she will update the Assembly on her plans.
answer_text: I have only, very recently, received written legal opinion on the High Court judgment relating to the anonymity of suspects provision of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.These are highly significant and complex matters which require careful consideration before a way forward can be determined. I am currently considering the detail of the advice provided.  I would plan to update the Assembly on my decision as early as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when her Department will publish proposals for changes in our domestic and business rates system based on the consultation that took place between November 2023 and February 2024.
answer_text: I wanted to give Committee sight of the factual report summarising the consultation responses received in relation to the Rating Revenue Raising Measures well in advance of publication.  So, it has been provided to you as Chair of the Finance Committee. My officials also provided extensive briefing to the Committee on the responses received in closed session on 12 June. As officials are currently in a pre-election period, it is not possible for them to publicly comment on policy or publish the consultation report until after the election.Any future changes to the rating system will be made between now and the end of the Assembly mandate. They will be informed by the evidence gathered as part of the consultation process that ended in February this year.My proposals for changes to the rating system will be based on the widest evidence base available. This includes my extensive engagement with stakeholders since taking up post, the Executive’s wider policy priorities, my desire to grow the tax base, and my Department’s wider work on Budget sustainability. I am developing short, medium and long term policy proposals for the Executive that will work for our own devolved tax system. Although rates are a devolved matter, I will also be taking account of any change in position on business rates by the new government formed at Westminster, including any potential Barnett consequentials that may relate to that position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has raised the issue of the funding of Casement Park in any meeting with the Chief Secretary to HM Treasury.
answer_text: Casement Park is an Executive flagship project and as such a priority for funding by the Executive. While the Department for Communities (DfC) is the lead department for the development of Casement Park, and therefore is in the lead in engagement with the British government in relation to it, I have written to the Communities Minister offering any support my department can provide.I have continually pressed the Chief Secretary to the Treasury for the additional funding for public services and infrastructure, and discussed Casement Park and Sub Regional stadia with Hilary Benn, the then Shadow Secretary of State, at a meeting on 21 May 2024.I have also raised Casement Park with the new Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Darren Jones MP, restating my view that the British Government should make good its commitment and confirm its contribution immediately, pointing out that any further delay would represent a huge lost opportunity to garner the very significant sporting, economic and social benefits that the Euro competition would generate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the external groups he has met since coming into office in relation to the (i) anti-poverty strategy; (ii) disability strategy; (iii) gender strategy; and (iv) sexual orientation strategy, as set out in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: While I have not yet met external groups to discuss the development of the Social Inclusion Strategies, I acknowledge the input from stakeholders and the contribution they have made to the work to date. I will announce the next steps to be taken in relation to the Social Inclusion Strategies shortly. This phase of development will include engagement and consultation both by myself and my officials.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when they will (i) publish the responses to the public consultation on a review of the Race Relations (Northern Ireland)) Order 1997; and (ii) bring forward policy proposals.
answer_text: A review of the current Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 has been conducted as part of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015 - 2025, including a 12-week public consultation on possible changes which concluded in June 2023. (i) A report on the responses and feedback from the consultation is currently under consideration. (ii) We aim to bring updated Race Relations legislation before the Assembly in the 2025-2026 Session and intend to publish the report in advance of that.  The report will bring forward identified policy changes and move us a step closer to providing the best possible legal protection against racism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has asked his officials to review the policy on removing flags from departmental property.
answer_text: Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. Until such times as a comprehensive approach can be developed, my Department will continue to apply its current processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of binding nature restoration targets in Northern Ireland which at least meet targets set in the EU Nature Restoration Law.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing a draft Nature Recovery Strategy, based on the 23 targets of the UN’s Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF).  All four countries of the UK have given a commitment to review their respective biodiversity strategies to embrace the GBF collectively.As the EU is a fellow party of the UN Convention on Biological Diversity, the new EU Nature Restoration Law is also based on the obligations contained in the GBF, therefore there is no divergence in what the UK, NI and EU are aiming to achieve.Target 2 of the GBF requires countries to “… ensure that by 2030 at least 30% of areas of degraded terrestrial, inland water, coastal and marine ecosystems are under effective restoration, in order to enhance biodiversity …”  A number of NI policies currently under development will contribute towards achieving this nature restoration target, including the Peatland Strategy, Ammonia Strategy and Blue Carbon Action Plan. In tandem, the draft Environment Strategy contains many actions associated with large scale restoration works. In addition, the draft Nature Recovery Strategy contains objectives designed to halt the loss of biodiversity that may require new legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they raised the issue of the funding of Casement Park in their meeting with the Prime Minister on 5 February 2024.
answer_text: The discussion covered a range of areas including financial sustainability, investment, transformation, and public sector pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: Since the public consultation closed in September last year work has been ongoing to consider the outcomes of the consultation and finalise the Review. I expect to receive the final Review for my approval in the coming weeks, after which, the Review will be shared with my Executive colleagues.My officials are currently engaging with their colleagues in the Department of Transport regarding the processes and timeframes for agreement of the Review by the Irish Government and to agree a date for its publication. It is anticipated the final Review will be published in the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the progression of legislation to create legally binding lasting power of attorney.
answer_text: Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides for the creation of lasting powers of attorney (LPAs). Whilst commencement of these provisions is for the Department of Health, the Department of Justice has a significant interest in the operational implications. The Department of Finance also retains an interest in the interaction of LPAs with enduring powers of attorney. Departments are jointly involved in making implementation plans to allow for commencement of the uncommenced provisions of the 2016 Act. Progress, however, is likely to remain slow given the severe financial pressures facing the Executive at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the issues that are delaying (i) the closure of Muckamore Abbey Hospital; and (ii) the relocation of residents.
answer_text: The proposed closure date of June 2024 for Muckamore Abbey Hospital was set subject to the successful resettlement to community placements of the patients remaining in the hospital, and agreeing appropriate alternative assessment and treatment options.Work to expedite resettlement arrangements for the remaining patients is being led by the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, chaired by Dr Patricia Donnelly.  	However, a number of factors have impacted on the delivery of the planned resettlements within the timeframes originally envisaged, including the availability of suitable accommodation in the community and difficulties in recruiting the required appropriately skilled staff.  Work is continuing with providers to address these issues and enable the resettlements to take place as soon as practicable.Following an assessment from the Oversight Board on progress towards resettling the remaining patients, I announced on 5 June a short extension to the timeframe for the closure of the hospital.  As we drew closer to the proposed closure date of June 2024, I was aware that the lack of clarity about the immediate future of the hospital was causing anxiety for the remaining patients and their families about the future arrangements for their care, and the uncertainty was also impacting on hospital staff.The safety and well-being of the patients remaining in the hospital is of paramount importance to me, and I am determined that each patient is afforded the necessary time to enable them to successfully transition to their new homes.  The resettlement process, particularly for those patients who have spent a considerable part of their lives in Muckamore, can be complex and any attempt to rush this work risks the breakdown of individual community placements.  I know such breakdowns can be a difficult and traumatic experience for patients and their families.It was for this reason that I decided setting a further deadline at this time for the hospital’s closure is not helpful, though I have been very clear that this does not mean there is any change to the policy position, which remains that no-one should be required to live in a hospital.Planning is continuing at pace through the work of the Oversight Board in relation to establishing resettlement timelines for those patients who are ready for discharge from the hospital, and the work being progressed by the Belfast Trust to close the hospital is continuing. As resettlements from the hospital progress, the staffing position at Muckamore is being kept under ongoing review.  As patient numbers reduce, my focus is on ensuring that services at the hospital can continue to be provided safely, and it will be important to make sure that any contingency arrangements that may be necessary to facilitate this are in place.  The primary purpose of any such arrangements will be to ensure the safety and well-being of the remaining patients.  These contingency arrangements are considered at meetings of the Regional Res

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the combined stormwater overflows at Knock River and Mill Road West will be upgraded.
answer_text: The management of the public sewerage network is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:Work has commenced to upgrade the storm overflow at the Knock River, known as the Knockbracken storm overflow, with an anticipated completion date of late spring 2026.The upgrade of the Mill Road West storm overflow is currently included within NI Water’s Price Control 21 (2021-2027) investment programme.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they raised issues in relation to the funding of Casement Park at the first meeting of the East-West Council.
answer_text: Casement Park continues to be an Executive flagship project, as reflected in the PfG.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions his Department has taken to facilitate (i) the relocation of residents; and (ii) the closure of Muckamore Abbey Hospital.
answer_text: The proposed closure date of June 2024 for Muckamore Abbey Hospital was set subject to the successful resettlement to community placements of the patients remaining in the hospital, and agreeing appropriate alternative assessment and treatment options.Work to expedite resettlement arrangements for the remaining patients is being led by the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, chaired by Dr Patricia Donnelly.  	However, a number of factors have impacted on the delivery of the planned resettlements within the timeframes originally envisaged, including the availability of suitable accommodation in the community and difficulties in recruiting the required appropriately skilled staff.  Work is continuing with providers to address these issues and enable the resettlements to take place as soon as practicable.Following an assessment from the Oversight Board on progress towards resettling the remaining patients, I announced on 5 June a short extension to the timeframe for the closure of the hospital.  As we drew closer to the proposed closure date of June 2024, I was aware that the lack of clarity about the immediate future of the hospital was causing anxiety for the remaining patients and their families about the future arrangements for their care, and the uncertainty was also impacting on hospital staff.The safety and well-being of the patients remaining in the hospital is of paramount importance to me, and I am determined that each patient is afforded the necessary time to enable them to successfully transition to their new homes.  The resettlement process, particularly for those patients who have spent a considerable part of their lives in Muckamore, can be complex and any attempt to rush this work risks the breakdown of individual community placements.  I know such breakdowns can be a difficult and traumatic experience for patients and their families.It was for this reason that I decided setting a further deadline at this time for the hospital’s closure is not helpful, though I have been very clear that this does not mean there is any change to the policy position, which remains that no-one should be required to live in a hospital.Planning is continuing at pace through the work of the Oversight Board in relation to establishing resettlement timelines for those patients who are ready for discharge from the hospital, and the work being progressed by the Belfast Trust to close the hospital is continuing. As resettlements from the hospital progress, the staffing position at Muckamore is being kept under ongoing review.  As patient numbers reduce, my focus is on ensuring that services at the hospital can continue to be provided safely, and it will be important to make sure that any contingency arrangements that may be necessary to facilitate this are in place.  The primary purpose of any such arrangements will be to ensure the safety and well-being of the remaining patients.  These contingency arrangements are considered at meetings of the Regional Res

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will (i) publish his review of the Cross-Compliance Penalty Regime; and (ii) bring forward proposals for change.
answer_text: My Department undertook a Consultation on Future Agriculture Policy Proposals for Northern Ireland in late 2021 and early 2022. This consultation included proposals for the replacement of the current Cross-Compliance regime with a new set of Farm Sustainability Standards. I will make decisions on the new Farm Sustainability Standards and a new penalty regime later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why the membership of the interim Public Sector Transformation Board was not communicated to the Assembly via a Written Ministerial Statement, similar to her Written Ministerial Statement on the interim Fiscal Framework on 21 May 2024.
answer_text: Establishing a Public Sector Transformation Board is a requirement of the Financial Package. My department moved at pace to put an interim Board in place to invite and assess transformation proposals so that vital funding could begin to be deployed this year. Once the membership of the interim Board had been confirmed, it held its first meeting within days on Tuesday 21 May, and by Friday 31 May, it had agreed and issued its call for proposals to departments.I was scheduled to answer oral Assembly questions on Tuesday 4 June, and as a question on transformation had been tabled, I used that as an opportunity to verbally update Members in the Assembly on the significant progress that had recently been made on transformation.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the review of procedures for sponsoring groups visiting Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: An internal audit of event management in Parliament Buildings was completed by the Assembly Commission’s internal auditors in February 2024. The internal audit made several recommendations to make the process for booking an event more concise and easier to follow. The new ‘Sponsored Events in Parliament Buildings Policy’ sets out the procedures for sponsoring group visits to Parliament Buildings and specifies when cross-designation support for Member-sponsored events is required. The requirement for Member-sponsored events to have cross-designation support was agreed by a previous Assembly Commission in the early 2000’s after a number of issues arose from plans for events in Parliament Buildings that were viewed by others as being contentious. To mitigate this issue, the requirement for cross-designation sponsorship of events in Parliament Buildings was introduced.The new Policy has been published on the Members’ Portal and on the Assembly’s website at https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/policies/events-in-parliament-buildings-and-its-grounds-policy/.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the (i) British government; and (ii) Irish government in relation to securing funding to construct Casement Park.
answer_text: Casement Park continues as an Executive flagship project, as reflected in the PfG.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to address the issue of pavement parking.
answer_text: The recent introduction of the Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 allows the Department to enforce against vehicles found parked on footways alongside bus lanes, bus stops/stands, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory ‘School Keep Clear’ markings.  The legislation adds to list of specific areas where footway parking is prohibited, and I believe it is a positive step forward in deterring inconsiderate pavement parking. The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’ and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.  Where we do not have the powers to act, the police can enforce against vehicles found to be obstructing a footway.On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking. This can be accessed using this link;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/inconsiderate-pavement-parking-findings-report I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have had the opportunity to consider the Findings Report.
