answer_text: The table below sets out the number local undergraduate students enrolled at higher education institutions in the south from 2017/18 to 2021/22 (the latest year for which data is available). 									YearEnrolments2017/181,0752018/191,1402019/201,2852020/211,2552021/221,250

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the guidance his Department has issued to post-primary schools about the use of smart phones (a) on school grounds; and (b) in classrooms; and (ii) any plans to add to this guidance.
answer_text: The current guidance to schools on the effective educational uses of mobile digital devices, including smart phones is set out in DE Circular 2016/26 (supported by the principles relative to online safety issued under DE Circular 2016/27).  Officials are working with the Education Authority (EA) to update this guidance through engagement with stakeholders.Up to date online safety advice is provided in the Safeguarding guidance to schools and via the Safer Schools App available to all schools.Guidance relating to behaviour requires that schools have a policy in place which promotes positive behaviour among pupils in order to safeguard their welfare and facilitate their educational progress at school.  Most schools will therefore set out limitations on the use of personal mobile phones through their behaviour policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps being taken to ensure that adequate (i) nursery; and (ii) primary one places are made available to meet demand in South Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.  Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting. Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 65 funded pre-school places still available in the South Belfast Parliamentary Constituency area at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process.The EA PEG continue to monitor pre-school provision in all areas including South Belfast.In respect of primary admissions, my department works with schools to create additional capacity in sectors and areas where oversubscription arises.  At the conclusion of the primary admissions process the EA has advised that one child resident in South Belfast was still to secure a place with 237 places still available.Children with a statement of specialist educational needs (SEN) follow a different process than the general admissions process, one which seeks to secure an appropriate placement which meets their individual needs.  There is significant demand for specialist education places particularly for pre-school and primary one children, and the Belfast area is an area of pressure.  The Education Authority, supported by my Department, is working at pace to create additional capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient appropriate provision for all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has commissioned any policy work on the regulation of flags.
answer_text: I understand your question to relate to the unauthorised display of flags in public spaces and on street furniture and am answering on that basis.  I have not commissioned any specific policy work in my Department on the regulation of such displays as the Department of Justice is not responsible for flag regulation.  The Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition (FICT), established by The Executive Office, made a number of recommendations in relation to the flying of flags in Northern Ireland. I previously wrote to the then First and deputy First Minsters jointly with the former Infrastructure Minister, whose department has significant responsibilities in this space, to ask for a discussion regarding the recommendations and to press for the publication of the FICT report with an Action Plan, and I supported this approach at Executive. My officials are also working on Judge Marrinan’s Review of Hate Crime legislation in Northern Ireland. Whilst it does not specifically address the issue of the regulation of flags in public spaces, recommendation 15 in the Review states that consideration should be given to implementing a statutory duty to remove hate expression from public space and refers to a number of relevant public authorities, including Councils, the Department for Infrastructure and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, that should take all reasonable steps to remove hate expression from their own property and, where it engages their functions, broader public space. Whilst the flying of a flag is not on all occasions, in and of itself, an expression of hate, in the context of Northern Ireland, flags can and are used on occasion to intimidate and incite. Policy development work is ongoing in relation to this recommendation, though much of this would fall to the other named departments to deliver. Given the potential overlap with the FICT report, I also wrote to the First and deputy First Ministers on the need for an agreed approach to addressing these cross-cutting issues at Executive.I remain eager to work with Executive colleagues to develop an Action Plan to progress recommendations from the FICT Commission’s Report and will continue to pursue a viable way forward that addresses this issue.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when a Programme for Government will be presented to the Assembly.
To ask the Minister of Education whether the decision to reallocate £150 million from integrated schools was agreed by the Executive.
answer_text: The Fresh Start Agreement set out a commitment by the UK Government to release up to £500m of new capital funding over ten years to support shared and integrated education and shared housing.  It was never solely for integrated education. Ring-fenced Fresh Start funding for shared and integrated education was removed by the UK Government. The Executive played no part in this decision and there was no consultation with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether an internal or external recruitment campaign was conducted for the appointment of a new Permanent Secretary in her Department.
answer_text: I had no role in the appointment of the Permanent Secretary to the Department of Justice.  The appointment process is a matter for the Head of the Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a bid was made to the Minister of Finance for increased funding for Arts in the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: Yes a bid was made by my Department to the Minister of Finance for increased funding for the Arts in the Budget 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10338/22-27, to detail the cost to the Executive of vacant property relief for (i) more than one year; (ii) more than five years; (iii) more than ten years; and (iv) more than 20 years.
answer_text: At 31st March 2024, the total cost to the Executive of vacant property relief awarded for the 2023/24 rating year for, 	(i) 	more than one year is estimated to be £15.46 million. 	Of this:	(ii) 	£8.32 million related to occupancies which had been availing of the relief for more than five years; 	And of this:	(iii) 	£3.26 million related to occupancies which had been availing of the relief for more than 10 years.	This includes long term vacant units paying non-domestic vacant rating at a liability of 50% of the occupied rate, plus properties excluded from rates due to their status (for example listed buildings, properties owned by insolvent companies etc).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 9203/22-27, how many meetings between parties entitled to form an Executive and the Head of the Civil Service took place between May 2022 and the restoration of the Assembly on 3 February 2024.
answer_text: We are advised by the Head of the Northern Ireland Civil Service that a total of 100 meetings took place. These meetings took a variety of forms that included collective meetings where all parties entitled to form and Executive were present, and individual bilateral meetings with party leaders and/or representatives.

A bill for rates was issued on 6th May 2024 in line with Land & Property Services billing processes for payment backdated to 1 April 2023, which is the start date of the current Valuation List.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rateable value for the car park in North Street, Belfast.
answer_text: The Net Annual Value (NAV) of the carpark valued at N38 North Street, Belfast, Antrim in the 2023 Valuation List is £45,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline indicative dates for the introduction of legislation to amend The Limitation (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.
answer_text: It is my intention, subject to the scheduling of Assembly business, to introduce the Defective Premises Bill to the Assembly on 20th May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the traffic calming alterations most recently consulted on for Orpen Park, Finaghy.
answer_text: The review on the proposed traffic calming alterations is still ongoing and has taken longer than expected due to the staff resources currently available. I have however asked officials, subject to other priority work, to bring this consultation to a conclusion as soon as possible.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 7057/22-27; (ii) AQW 9203/22-27; and (iii) AQW 9531/22-27.
answer_text: (i) AQW 7057/22-27 was answered on 26 April 2024.(ii) AQW 9203/22-27 was answered on 30 April 2024.(iii) AQW 9531/22-27 was answered on 20 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that intensive poultry production has on rivers.
answer_text: Intensive poultry production has the potential to have a negative impact on rivers if the poultry litter and manure produced is not utilised appropriately.  Poultry litter and manure are effective organic fertiliser for growing crops. However, if they are applied at levels which exceed crop nutrient requirements or in unsuitable soil and weather conditions, it can cause nutrient pollution in rivers.In Northern Ireland, significant work has been carried out to develop alternative sustainable outlets for utilising poultry litter and reduce the quantities which are applied to agricultural land. The government initiative on the ‘Sustainable Utilisation of Poultry Litter’ in 2012 ultimately resulted in the establishment of the Stream Bioenergy anaerobic digestion plant at Tully near Ballymena. The plant currently processes 40,000t of poultry litter per year to produce renewable electricity to power some 6000 homes and 3 types of processed fertilisers. This enables export of excess nutrients and reduces nutrient loadings on local agricultural land. The plant is in the process of being expanded to a capacity that will be able to utilise all the poultry litter produced in NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what the term reshuffle means in relation to permanent secretary roles.
answer_text: AnswerThe Head of the Civil Service (HOCS) is responsible for the line management of Permanent Secretaries. When a Permanent Secretary post becomes vacant, HOCS fills this vacancy in accordance with the relevant NICS policies. This can include a reshuffle i.e. moving an existing Permanent Secretary into the vacant role and filling the resultant vacancy through recruitment, (the Senior Civil Service operates as a corporate resource and can be moved to different posts according to business need). Any such managed move is determined by a range of factors including experience, skills set, departmental agenda, etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what additional rail provision, including services and signage, will be provided at (i) Finaghy train station; and (ii) Balmoral train station to support a successful 2028 UEFA European Football Championship and other events at Casement Park.
answer_text: Public transport services, including signage, are frequently enhanced to facilitate spectators attending various major events in Belfast. The platforms at Balmoral and Finaghy train stations, which are a short distance to Casement Park, have previously been extended to facilitate six train car length platforms and therefore to cater for higher passenger demand. In addition, Casement Park is also currently served by Glider and Metro services operating a combined seven-minute frequency at peak and eight minutes off peak.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will ask his officials to facilitate the relocation of Trademarket to Bankmore Square, Belfast.
answer_text: DfI has a licence agreement in place with Belfast Central Business District Ltd for the Bankmore Square area, which ends on 31 March 2025. My Department is therefore unable to enter into a separate agreement with any other party to licence this area at this time.My Department is willing to engage with Trademarket and other relevant bodies should they identify other potentially available sites that could accommodate Trademarket.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he submitted a paper to the Executive to recommend the removal of ring-fenced funding from ten integrated schools.
answer_text: Ring-fenced Fresh Start funding was removed by the UK Government.  My Department was informed of the removal of ring-fenced funding by the Department of Finance.  This was not a decision made by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale for the decision not to undertake an internal recruitment trawl for the position of Chief Executive of the Education Authority.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department was in breach of Clause 64(1) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 from 1 April 2024.
answer_text: Section 64 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 states that the Minister of Finance shall, before the beginning of the financial year, lay before the Assembly a draft budget, that has been agreed by the Executive Committee. However, Section 64 (1A) states that at least 14 days before laying a draft budget for a financial year, the Minister of Finance must lay before the Assembly a statement specifying the amount of UK funding for that year notified to the Minister of Finance by the Secretary of State.I received confirmation of the funding available from the Secretary of State on 20 March, meaning that Section 64 (1A) in itself prevented me from laying a Budget before the start of the financial year.The Executive has agreed a short delay in agreeing a Budget for 2024-25 until the end of April to allow full consideration of the Budget it is agreeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of properties availing of vacant property relief for (i) more than one year; (ii) more than five years; and (iii) more than ten years.
answer_text: At 31st March 2024 the number of occupancies availing of vacant property relief for:more than one year was 13,036.  Of this:8,942 more than five years; and of this:5,614 more than 10 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Phase 2 of the Belfast Rapid Transit will extend the Glider service to Carryduff.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when negotiations on new a fiscal framework will begin.
answer_text: At my meeting with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 28 February, we agreed to our respective officials commencing engagement on a new Fiscal Framework. That engagement has now begun.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to including St. Anne's Primary School on Upper Lisburn Road, Belfast, in the current review for further part-time 20 mph speed limit zones.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they were informed of the decision to remove funding from integrated schools before 21 February 2024.
answer_text: We were not informed before 21 February 2024 of the decision to remove funding from integrated schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her consent or agreement was sought for the secondment of her Department's Permanent Secretary to the role of Chief Executive of the Education Authority.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 9951/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to review the Licensing of Pavement Cafés Act (Northern Ireland) 2014, with the specific intent to amend Section 1, Clause 4 to extend the number of minutes required for the removal of temporary furniture.
answer_text: There are no plans to review the Licensing of Pavement Cafés Act (Northern Ireland) 2014, with the specific intent to amend the legislation to extend the number of minutes required for the removal of temporary furniture.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has made an assessment of the economic cost of the new Electronic Travel Authorisation on the tourism sector.
answer_text: I am concerned about the impact of the introduction of the British Government’s Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme on our tourism sector. I have met with the NI Tourism Alliance to discuss this vital issue and to hear at first hand the industry’s concerns. Tourism is performing extremely well with the number of external overnight visitors quadrupling between 1998 and 2019. Seamless all-island travel is key to this growth. In 2019, international visitors (who will require an ETA when introduced) contributed £220 million to the local economy and it is this expenditure that could be impacted by the introduction of the ETA scheme.  I have written to Tom Pursglove MP, the Minister for Legal Migration and the Border at the British Home Office to seek an urgent meeting to discuss solutions that would protect our tourism industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department holds data on the number of long haul flight routes operating out of Northern Ireland airports.
answer_text: I understand that chartered flights to Orlando, Florida operate on a seasonal basis from Belfast International Airport. No other long haul flights currently operate from our airports, and therefore no data on the number of long haul flight routes is held the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost of the Air Passenger Duty block grant adjustment since the policy was introduced.
answer_text: Following the decision to devolve and reduce Air Passenger Duty (APD) on direct long-haul flights in 2012, the Executive had to meet the ongoing financial consequences of devolving long haul APD as a consequence of state aid rules. This was, and continues to apply, via a Block Grant Adjustment to reflect the tax receipts forgone by the British Treasury at the point of devolving the duty. At the time of devolution, it was agreed that the cost to our block grant to abolish long haul APD would be indexed to the growth in long haul passenger numbers across Britain and the North. There is no change in the Treasury’s position that we must continue to bear this cost. The table below sets out the Block Grant Adjustment associated with the reduction of direct long-haul Air Passenger Duty.  	Year	Block Grant Adjustment	2024/25	£2.445m	2023/24	£1.826m	2022/23	£1.210m	2021/22	£0.659m	2020/21	£2.343m	2019/20	£2.343m	2018/19	£2.283m	2017/18	£2.224m	2016/17	£2.166m	2015/16	£2.139m	2014/15	£2.139m	2013/14	£2.045m	2012/13	£2.011mSource: HMT’s Block Grant Transparency publication for costs in 2016/17 to 2024/25. Costs for earlier years provided by HMT directly to DoF. This cost represents the reality of tax devolution. We bear the financial consequences of devolving a tax, and the risk that revenue, and the activity that generates it, can either rise, or fall – which has happened in this case. This is consistent with the approach to tax devolution in Scotland and Wales.

answer_text: The number of children under the age of eighteen years who have had corrective surgery for cleft palate in each of the last six financial years is broken down by Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) in the table overleaf.Cleft lip and cleft palate are often treated at specialist NHS cleft centres and therefore may not be seen in every HSCT. Caution is therefore advised when using this data as an indicator of performance across HSCTs.Table 1. Number of children1 who have undergone corrective surgery for cleft palate2, per HSCT, from 2018/19 to 2023/24PHSCT2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24PBelfast393336292927South Eastern<5<50000Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.1Figures represent individuals under 18. Where an individual was admitted more than once for corrective surgery with cleft palate, they were counted in the HSCT of their first admission that year. Figures across years cannot be summed as an individual may have undergone surgery in more than one year. 2Corrective surgery for cleft palate was identified using the following OPCS-4 codes in any of the operation fields of the Hospital Inpatient System: F29.1, F29.2, F29.8, or F29.9, combined with any of the following ICD-10 codes in any of the diagnosis fields: Q35.1, Q35.3, Q35.5, Q35.7, Q35.9, Q37.0, Q37.1, Q37.2, Q37.3, Q37.4, Q37.5, Q37.8, or Q37.9.3Figures between 1 and 4 have been suppressed to prevent patient disclosure.PData for 2023/24 is only provisional at this time. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the service provision for dealing with cleft palate in children; (ii) the current number of specialised staff, broken down by grade, in dealing with cleft palate; and (iii) the budget allocation for this.
answer_text: Around 40 babies a year are born in NI with Cleft Lip and/or Palate (CLP).  CLP is an anomaly resulting from failure of fusion of embryological parts forming the lip, nose and palate, caused by genetic or environmental factors.  Surgery to repair the CLP usually takes place within the first year of life with subsequent treatments provided due to growth/physical development.(i) The Cleft Lip and Palate service in Northern Ireland is provided between RBHSC and South Eastern Trust. The service is multidisciplinary involving plastic surgery, dentistry, specialist nursing, psychology and speech and language therapy.  Clinical interfaces with orthodontics, audiology, genetics and ENT services are also supported consistent with the clinical needs of each patient.All children diagnosed with a CLP anomaly cleft must be seen within 24 hours by a nominated member of the Cleft team. A formal pathway supported by the multidisciplinary team provides care throughout the patient journey.  (ii) The current number of specialised staff funded to provide treatment includes: 2 Consultant Plastic SurgeonsConsultant Dental SurgeonSpecialist DentistSpeech and Language Therapist – Band 8b2 Speech and Language Therapists – Band 7Specialist Nurse – Band 8aSpecialist Nurse – Band 7 Psychologist – Band 8a Cleft Co-ordinator – Band 5Admin Support - Band 4(iii)	The 2023/24 budget level is approximately £660k which includes funding for a network arrangement providing access to specialist from an expert centre based in GB.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following comments by Junior Minister Reilly on 8 April 2024 during ministerial questions, whether they will publish the equality impact assessment conducted on the decision to remove ring-fenced Fresh Start funding from integrated schools.
answer_text: Funding for integrated schools is a policy matter for the Education Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children waiting for surgery for cleft palate in excess of (a) three months; (b) six months; (c) nine months; and (d) twelve months; and (ii) his plans to reduce the waiting list for this treatment.
answer_text: Cleft Lip and/or Palate (CLP) is a congenital anomaly resulting from failure of fusion of embryological parts forming the lip, nose and palate.Treatment involves an initial primary procedure and subsequent secondary/cosmetic procedures.There are 10 patients currently waiting for a primary procedure and 31 patients waiting for secondary/cosmetic procedures.Of the patients waiting for primary procedures, 90% are waiting less than 6 months.  Belfast Trust have stated the total number of children waiting for surgery for cleft palate in excess of Three months is 30Six months is 24Nine months is 18 Twelve months is 12Plans to reduce waiting times are in progress following the appointment of a second surgeon in February 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many local breweries have applied for a local producer’s licence as provided for in Clause 10 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: A total of four applications have been made for a local producer’s licence from breweries as provided for in the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she was first consulted on the secondment of her Department's Permanent Secretary to the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Head of the Civil Service informed me of the secondment to the Education Authority and did not seek my consent or agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process for the recruitment of the Chief Executive Officer of the Education Authority, including whether there was an internal trawl for the position.
answer_text: The decision to fill the forthcoming vacancy for the role of Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the Education Authority (EA) by process of secondment was agreed by the EA Board on receipt of a proposal I made. The education system and consequently the EA face unprecedented challenges; therefore, I concluded that someone with the seniority, experience and skill of a Permanent Secretary was required to instil confidence in stakeholders and the wider public. The proposal was made with the approval of the Head of the Civil Service and the NICS Board. There was no internal trawl for the position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will carry out an assessment on the cost effectiveness of road surfacing with concrete as opposed to tarmac.
answer_text: Generally concrete surfacing is only used on rigid pavement construction, that is when all pavement layers are concrete. There are very few roads in the North of this type, hence it is not considered a suitable option for maintenance resurfacing on the existing road network.  With respect to new roads, rigid pavements are an available option under the current standards, however, they are seldom used on new major roads projects across the UK or Ireland.Common issues with the use of concrete road surfacing include safety concerns due to lower surface friction when compared to asphalt surfacing; environmental concerns including noise and embodied carbon; high initial costs; excessive maintenance costs and high replacement costs at end of life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what percentage of individuals going through the Limited Capability for Work Related Activity or Limited Capability for Work assessment are waiting more than 14 weeks for an appointment date with Medical Support Services where a paper decision is not possible.
answer_text: On 25 March 2024, 4.7% of claimants have been waiting more than 14 weeks for an appointment, while 7.8% of claimants with an appointment have been waiting more than 14 weeks for an assessment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they were consulted prior to the appointment of the Chief Executive Officer of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority appointed the Chief Executive with the approval of the Minister of Education.The Education Minister did not consult us in relation to this appointment as there was no requirement to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there was an external advertisement to recruit the new Chief Executive Officer of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The post was not externally advertised but rather it will be filled as a result of a three-year secondment which I agreed with the Board of the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current complement; and (ii) the planned complement after 30 June 2024 of (a) specialist assessment beds; and (b) hospital liaison nurses, for those with a learning disability in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Departmental officials and individual Trusts meet every two weeks to plan and review progress on the individual resettlement plans for patients in Muckamore hospital. Currently, there are 24 patients with a Learning Disability who are in the process of resettlement, but who are at this point receiving care in inpatient settings.   The Department has established a Task & Finish Group to finalise a Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) as part of a wider exercise to improve outcomes for children and adults with Learning Disability and establish a new model of care for people with Learning Disability. Throughout 2023, there has been a significant work on this revised model of service, which includes provision for specialist in-patient and assessment and treatment beds. To ensure there is adequate and appropriate alternative service provision in place after Muckamore closes, my Department is working with Trusts to deliver specialist Learning Disability Assessment and Treatment beds, as well as enhanced community preventative services.  Pending the outworking of this, officials have also recently written to Trusts to advise of an interim measure which will effectively regionalise the current Learning Disability specialist bed provision, and are working with Trusts to agree a process to operationalise this.Currently, there are 10 dedicated Learning Disability beds in SHSCT, 7 in WHSCT and 3 beds in NHSCT.  People with mild to moderate Learning Disability who have mental health needs, may also access core adult mental health psychiatry inpatient beds, depending on an assessment of their individual needs.  SEHSCT and BHSCT have recently submitted business cases to my Department for additional inpatient provision. These are intended to help address the growing pressures for access to in-patient, assessment and treatment beds. A costed implementation plan will be developed following agreement on the future model and completion of a finance review.In BHSCT there is one Learning Disability Liaison Nurse who covers acute and in-patient hospital facilities. They assist with communication and provision of advice to non-specialist medical and nursing colleagues when they are providing treatment for patients with complex intellectual needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has taken responsibility for adjusting the Limitation (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 in order to increase the time period for owners of defective properties to pursue civil claims.
answer_text: My Department is currently exploring how it might most quickly bring forward legislation to amend the Limitation (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 at the earliest opportunity, pending agreement of the Executive and the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he intends to commence the powers to set Corporation Tax funding as provided for in the Corporation Tax (Northern Ireland) Act 2015.
answer_text: My department does not have the authority to commence the powers to set the rate of Corporation Tax, as provided for in the Corporation Tax (Northern Ireland) Act 2015. The responsibility for this rests with the British Treasury.Rather, I intend bringing a paper to the Executive in due course to seek to agree our approach to tax devolution more broadly. However, I am clear that the devolution of Corporation Tax could only occur if it were affordable, and worthwhile if it ensures that ordinary citizens benefit through the creation of sustainable, well-paying jobs and if it furthers economic development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is responsible for enacting the legislation outlined in recommendation three of the Operation Kenova Interim Report.
answer_text: Recommendation three of the Operation Kenova Interim Report proposes enactment of legislation for both procedural time limits and judicial case management to handle cases passing from a new legacy structure to the criminal justice system.  This recommendation falls to my Department to consider. Both procedural time limits and judicial case management have been considered previously and were not prioritised at that time, with other initiatives deemed to have greater potential for reducing delay. It is worth noting that Sir John Gillen, in his report on law and procedures in serious sexual offences, found that statutory time limits and case management in other jurisdictions had a limited influence on reducing delays or the length of the criminal justice process.  In addition, it was noted that in England and Wales requests for extensions are common, increasing bureaucracy and pressure on courts, exacerbating delay and creating increased pressure on already limited resources. I will want to take time to consider this recommendation and determine next steps.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many formal meetings have taken place on a draft Programme for Government since May 2022.
answer_text: This Executive was formed on the 3 February 2024 and, since then, we have formally discussed the production of a Programme for Government on four occasions. A significant amount of work has already been carried out and this is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date on which he was first notified, whether verbally or in writing, of the UK Government decision to remove the ringfence from shared and integrated education Fresh Start funding.
answer_text: ANSWERI was informed by the Minister of Finance on 13 February 2024 of the decision by the UK Government to remove the ringfence from £150 million of previously earmarked Fresh Start capital funding and make it available to the restored Northern Ireland Executive as resource funding for wider public sector transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the date on which she was first notified, whether verbally or in writing, of the UK Government decision to remove the ringfence from shared and integrated education Fresh Start funding.
answer_text: Formal communication of the financial package came through a letter from the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 13 February 2024. This set out the high-level detail of the Executive Restoration Package which included £708 million from new and existing funding streams. It did not include a breakdown of those funding streams, which I subsequently requested. This clarity was provided through further correspondence from Treasury, at official level, on 1 March 2024 which specifically detailed the packages that were being un-ringfenced, including the Fresh Start Agreement package. I notified Executive colleagues on 5 March 2024.However, my officials have advised that NIO provided them with an initial indication of the funding streams under consideration for inclusion in the financial package in late December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will bring forward legislation to regulate the transfer of common parts during the sale of apartment blocks.
answer_text: I will engage with officials within the Department to assess how this issue can be addressed. I note that the Law Commission indicated that specific reform relating to the transfer of common parts would be complex and may be better considered in the wider context of statutory management schemes.  Future work will be dependent on competing priorities and available resources.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline when the official Opposition will be resourced in line with the Report on the outcome of the Independent Review of the Adequacy and Effectiveness of the Statement of Entitlements for an Official Opposition at the Northern Ireland Assembly.

To ask the Minister of Justice what conversations she has had with Executive colleagues in relation to the implementation of the recommendations from the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition Report.
answer_text: Since restoration of the Executive and Assembly, I have not had any conversations with current Executive colleagues in relation to implementation of recommendations in the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition Report.I do, however, intend to do so in the context of progressing implementation of Judge Marrinan’s Independent Review of Hate Crime legislation in Northern Ireland, which makes a number of related recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what actions her Department will take to ensure the (i) prevention; and (i) removal of posters which are deemed as hate speech by the PSNI.
answer_text: Stirring up hatred (the use of threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour or display of written material against a protected group) is an offence under the Public Order (Northern Ireland) Order 1987. Decisions on determining if the content of these posters are stirring up hatred or a hate crime or incident are operational matters for the PSNI.Officials in my department have been engaged in a Community Safety Response Group (CSRG) convened in November 2023 in response to growing anti-migrant sentiment in a number of areas across Northern Ireland, expressed on occasion through the erection of posters. The CSRG is a cross-Departmental / multi-agency group with representation that includes: The Executive Office; Department for Infrastructure; PSNI; Northern Ireland Housing Executive; and Councils. It has a Gold, Silver, and Bronze Command structure. The Response Group enables agencies to share information and to co-ordinate actions aimed at securing the removal of hate expressions, including those involving the erection of posters.The Department of Justice does not have any powers to remove material from public space.  Under Section 32 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000 and common law, the PSNI have fundamental duties to take steps to bring offenders to justice and prevent the commission of criminal offences.  There are also a number of specific powers vested in other public authorities. For example, district councils in Northern Ireland have powers to remove or obliterate graffiti detrimental to the amenity of any land in its district, or any placard or poster in its district that does not have planning permission.  Powers are also vested in the Department for Infrastructure under planning legislation to remove items and recover the cost of doing so for any unauthorised materials on lampposts or other street furniture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department is considering to address the impediments for shared care between private and NHS doctors, currently being faced by service users and GPs.
answer_text: Department of Health’s policy in relation to pre and post treatment care for patients who wish to avail of private health care.Where a patient seeks pre and/or post treatment care specific to their private treatment, they will not be entitled to such support from primary or secondary care services in Northern Ireland. Therefore, it will be important for the individual to ensure that all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider.In line with normal practice, patients will continue to be able to access emergency care where necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to address the level of NHS dentistry services in the BT7 and BT6 areas of Belfast.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  I have now considered proposals for a pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24 that is in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report. My intentions were communicated to the British Dental Association on 5 March 2024 and the Department is reviewing their response in advance of implementation. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have developed investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and these are under active consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any planned resurfacing works on Lisburn Road, Belfast, in particular between Cranmore Gardens and Lisburn Road police station.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his considerations to an uplift in funding for Sure Start services.
answer_text: Stabilisation of Sure Start after a period of budget uncertainty will be an important first step in the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy. My Department has been engaging closely with the Department of Health (DoH) which administers the Sure Start programme, to identify and quantify factors relevant to stabilisation, including uplift costs for 2024-25.  I will be asking the Executive for the funds required to meet these costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide additional funding to the Housing Executive to allow for the recommencement of discretionary Home Improvement Grants.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has responsibility for administering a range of grants including four discretionary Home Improvement Grants. Renovation Grant, Replacement Grant, Home Repair Assistance Grant and Common Parts Grant. Due to budget pressures these discretionary grants are currently only made available in exceptional circumstances. Substantial budget pressures are impacting across all Departments, and I am not currently in a position to provide additional funding for these grants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy; including a timescale for sending the strategy out for public consultation.
answer_text: Work on the development of a Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy is underway within my department.  This is a significant piece of work and over coming months my officials will be engaging across Executive departments; with local government and with other stakeholders in a process that will culminate in the production of a draft strategy that will be opened for public consultation.Given the significance of these sectors to a wide range of policy areas, to the economy and to the quality of life of all people in Northern Ireland, I intend that the consultation period should be timed to encourage responses from as broad a range of interests as possible.  I will make an announcement on the timing and form of the consultation process in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department is undertaking to reduce waiting times for health service autism assessments for (i) children; and (ii) adults.
answer_text: My Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation. The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. Over the last six months, my staff have held meetings with all Trusts to chart the existing referral and assessment routes for paediatric ASD. After completing this mapping exercise, officials are collaborating with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Community Child Health to establish a standardized pathway across the region that aligns with NICE guidelines. The development of this pathway will incorporate best practices, emerging evidence, and the understanding that effective workforce planning is essential in meeting the demands.The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan (2023-2025) includes an action to consult on, and finalise, the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The strategy and the Framework embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of referral and diagnostic pathway. A Review of Adult Autism provision across the region has been undertaken. One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment. Department of Health officials are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Autism services under review, recognising that the demand for assessment and diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability and Ministerial priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will commit to increasing the spend per head on the arts sector, to bring it into line with the level of expenditure on the arts per head in other nearby jurisdictions.
answer_text: Decisions on future funding for the arts, as with all the other calls for investment, will be considered in the light of decisions on the budget. The Budget for 2024-25 has not yet been settled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Limitation Order, whether her Department will align it with the 30 day period in England and Wales.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to amend the Limitation (NI) Order 1989 in order to align it with the 30 year period in England and Wales.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of Fujitsu contracts with the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERThe eTendersNI procurement portal records data on the value of contracts awarded. The system has been in operation since April 2015 and the data records that Fujitsu has been awarded three contracts for Northern Ireland Civil Service departments which are current, with a total contract award value of £263 million. This would not include contracts awarded to Fujitsu by wider public sector bodies (e.g. Education Authority, Libraries NI etc).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether a Programme for Government will be aligned to a multi-year budget.
To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Horizon scandal, to detail any meetings he has had in relation to Fujitsu contracts in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: My officials meet with Fujitsu regularly. I have not had any meetings in relation to Fujitsu contracts in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will ensure that ten percent of his transport budget in 2024-2025 will be on active travel, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The implications of Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 are currently being considered by my Department.Regardless of how the requirement is interpreted it will need significantly more capital investment in active travel in the future. Evidence from elsewhere on these islands tells us that ramping up an ambitious programme of investment of this nature takes years. I am pleased to say that my officials have taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Act to increase the future delivery capacity that would allow us to strive to meet our ambitions for active travel.As the Member is also aware, the budgets for next year have not yet been set and we are continuing to work closely with the Department of Finance. It would be for the Executive to decide final budget allocations. I will consider funding for active travel in the context of my Department’s overall budget, taking into consideration factors such as other competing priorities, legislative requirements and deliver capacity. I am enthusiastic about the positive change that active travel would make to the lives of people who want to have greater choice in how they travel, particularly for shorter everyday journeys, or in combination with using public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the Post Office Horizon scandal, what due diligence and guarantees his Department has taken to ensure that Fujitsu is able to deliver on their contract with the Education Authority.
answer_text: The new Strategic Partner and School Management System (SPSMS) contract will provide an end-to-end fully integrated and inter-operable IT and School Management service that places learners at the heart of the solution by bringing together best of breed applications to support teaching and learning, while allowing for flexibility and personalisation according to learners’ needs.The coming year will see a transition of all services from the current provider, Capita, to the new Strategic Partner, Fujitsu. The Education Authority (EA) is a designated Centre of Procurement Expertise and is directly responsible for the procurement of goods and services on behalf of the entire school estate. The EA advise that it conducted a procurement for the replacement of its current service provision in strict accordance with the Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and The Public Contracts Regulations (2015). Fujitsu was awarded the contract on the 21 December 2023. According to the EA the robustness of the procurement process and detailed evaluation has provided it with assurance that the awarded service provider can and will deliver all required services.  The EA maintains rigorous management processes to ensure the effective delivery of services through external providers in accordance with its contractual terms.The Department has worked and continues to work closely with the programme team responsible for this delivery, to provide ongoing assurance and departmental oversight. Furthermore, in preparation and planning for the new service, several working groups have been established with representation from departmental policy leads to support delivery and implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will provide funding to Belfast City Council to see further alleyway gates installed in areas experiencing anti-social behaviour and criminality.
answer_text: My Department has no specific funding provision for alleyway gates and given the ongoing priorities within the remit of the Department of Justice, and the extraordinary constraints on our budget that position is unlikely to change. Primary responsibility for alleygating lies with local councils, with support from the Departments for Infrastructure and Communities, who may in some cases have landowner responsibility.My Department fully recognises the negative impact which anti-social behaviour can have on communities and in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides annual funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level.  PCSPs work in partnership with the local community, designated partners, statutory bodies and the voluntary sector to reduce the impact of, actual and perceived ASB in our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will publish the findings of the recent Residents' Parking Scheme pilot that took place at Rugby Road, Belfast.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the report will be published when I have considered the review findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions he has had with the Education Authority in relation to the approval of a £485 million contract to Fujitsu to deliver a school management system.
answer_text: The contract with Fujitsu was awarded prior to my appointment as Minister of Education, therefore no discussions have taken place with the Education Authority (EA) and myself in relation to this contract award. The EA advised that it conducted a robust procurement for the replacement of its current service provision in strict accordance with the Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and The Public Contracts Regulations (2015).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what communication his Department has received from the Department of Work and Pensions in relation to jobcentre referrals to food banks.
answer_text: My Department has not received any communication from the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to jobcentre referrals to food banks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will ensure funding is provided to progress works for provision of a soft play area for Botanic Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has not yet received confirmation of its capital budget for 2024/25, I am therefore not currently in a position to confirm the availability of any potential funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, with reference to appointments under the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022, (i) whether the Irish Language Commissioner will be required to be a speaker of Irish; and (ii) whether the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition will be required to be a speaker of Ulster Scots.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 amends the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and provides the statutory basis for the establishment of 3 new bodies, namely: the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression; the Irish Language Commissioner; and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition. TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of this Act for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies. This will be in-line with ourdecisions. Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment has been made regarding the need for a pedestrian crossing on Upper Malone Road, at the section of Old Forge Manor and Old Coach Lane.
answer_text: Details of the planned controlled pedestrian crossing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the Upper Malone Road area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  Following a number of requests for a crossing at this location, the most recent pedestrian and vehicle survey was carried out on 13 and 15 June 2023.  The results of this survey fell well below that required for further assessment of a controlled crossing and as such the Department has no plans to introduce a pedestrian crossing facility at this location.I can however advise that central hatching road markings and a pedestrian island have previously been provided on the country side carriageway of Finaghy Road South in order to improve pedestrian facilities in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of a deposit return scheme for drink containers.
answer_text: My department has been developing options for a Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) for drinks containers.		I am keen to achieve a scheme which is consistent with the UK-wide approach taken for many years on packaging and also takes into account Deposit Return Scheme in Ireland which started on 1 February 2024. 		Such a scheme can significantly reduce litter, increase recycling and quality of recycled material to promote a circular economy for single use drinks containers. European countries have achieved collection and recycling rates of up to 98% for plastic drinks bottles. 		Following publication of the UK Government response to the last formal consultation in January 2023, officials across the four administrations have undertaken extensive engagement with relevant stakeholders across the UK, including NI stakeholders that operate on an all-island basis; Irish government officials; and the Irish DRS operator.		Together with the other UK nations, I aim to share the outcome of this engagement in a joint policy statement in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what options his Department is exploring in relation to a potential ban of disposable vapes.
answer_text: I am aware of the growing scale of the environmental challenges arising from disposable vapes, given their hard to recycle components and the propensity for them to be littered. As well as health policy proposals to tackle vapes, the recent UK-wide Health-led consultation ‘Creating a smoke free generation’ published at the end of last year, also contained environmental proposals to restrict the sale and supply of disposable vapes, due to their environmental impact. Following the publication of the joint Government response to the consultation in January this is year, I am considering my position in relation to disposable vapes, including a potential ban, and will set out the next steps I propose to take in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what percentage of leisure centres have free period products available for visitors and staff.
answer_text: The provision of services in leisure centres is an operational matter for District Councils and any request for information of this nature should be directed to the relevant Council or Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve flood defences in areas that were affected by flooding in Autumn 2023.
answer_text: The rainfall in Autumn 2023 resulted in many watercourses in the South and East of the province rising to record levels resulting in widespread flooding across this region. Existing flood defences provided by my Department worked effectively in protecting hundreds of properties in Newry, Newcastle and other towns across the North.My Department is undertaking a number of flood studies including Newry, Portadown and Downpatrick to examine the flooding mechanisms, identify and assess any potential solutions, and then determine if any solution is viable to alleviate future flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s preparations to prevent and manage a potential spread of measles.
answer_text: Measles is a highly contagious, serious viral infection with complications including meningitis and hearing loss. It is the most infectious of all diseases transmitted through the respiratory route. Measles is a notifiable disease under the Public Health Act (Northern Ireland) 1967 and should be reported by clinicians to the Public Health Agency (PHA) when a case is suspected based on clinical presentation.The most effective measure to stop measles cases emerging, and to contain wider spread of imported cases, is to ensure that 95% of the population is vaccinated with two doses of the Measles, Mumps and Rubella (MMR) vaccine. Due to falling uptake of the MMR vaccine over several years, an MMR vaccine catchup campaign has been launched locally. Individuals up to 25 years of age, who have had less than two doses of a measles containing vaccine, are being contacted and invited to receive the MMR vaccine via GP surgeries and Trust vaccination clinics. Those aged 26 years of age and over who remain unvaccinated or partially unvaccinated can request the vaccine from their GP practice. Over 32,000 letters have now been issued to children and young people who have been identified as missing either one or both MMR vaccination. The letters include information about where to find details of local vaccine clinics. The letters also have a telephone number and PHA have set up a helpline which is running from 5th February – 31st March 2024.  This helpline is providing further advice and information about the vaccination and where to get vaccinated. The PHA is leading the overall multi-agency response to manage measles cases in Northern Ireland. A PHA chaired multi stakeholder working group has been established to coordinate readiness plans from the public health perspective and to ensure readiness in terms of the outbreak management response, laboratory testing arrangements and infection prevention control arrangements.A communication entitled ‘Preventing a Measles Resurgence in Northern Ireland’ was circulated by the Director of Public Health in PHA to Trust Medical Directors, Directors of Nursing, Primary Care GPs, Community Pharmacists and Out of Hours GPs on 26 January 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on negotiations with the UK Government on the fiscal framework and revenue raising in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will allocate a budget to implement the recommendations of the Independent Review of Education.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the instruction they have given officials to develop revenue raising proposals as part of a sustainability plan as outlined in their letter to the UK Government on 5 February 2024.
answer_text: We have given no instruction of that nature to our officials.It should be noted that this would primarily be the responsibility of the Finance Minister to bring along such relevant paper to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is exploring a ban on the possession and use of zombie knives.
answer_text: I am clear that zombie knives have no place in Northern Ireland.  My officials are taking forward work to ensure that the appropriate provisions under the Offensive Weapons Act 2019 are in place to prevent the manufacture, sale or hire, importation, and possession in private of zombie knives.  However, I am aware that in England and Wales, there have been concerns that the existing provision does not go far enough and that the Home Office is seeking to address this.  My officials are engaging with the Home Office with a view to considering whether additional measures are required in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's work in relation to the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: Since the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) was announced in 2021, my officials have been engaging closely with colleagues in the Department of Transport, to progress the Review.  The draft AISRR report was released for the purposes of Strategic Environmental Assessment from 25 July to 29 September 2023 and recent engagement has focussed on analysing the responses to the consultation and working to finalise the draft Report. Over the coming months, this engagement will continue at ministerial and official level as both Departments work to address the issues raised through the consultation and present the final report for agreement by the governments in both jurisdictions. Once the Review is published, joint efforts will turn to prioritising the recommendations within the Review, developing an implementation plan, and looking to explore avenues of funding to progress the interventions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the public consultation on the provision of free period products.
answer_text: A public consultation was held for 12 weeks from June to Sept 2023. Over 1600 responses were received. The responses broadly confirmed support for free provision and have informed our approach to the arrangements for making period products freely available. A report summarising the findings is currently being finalised with the aim that it will be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to reduce waiting times for access to community dental services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: To address the issue of access to all Trust Health and Social Care Services across Northern Ireland subsequent to the Covid-19 Pandemic, in mid-2022 the Department of Health (DoH) established the Performance and Transformation Executive Board (PTEB) and introduced the HSC Service Delivery Plan (SDP).Trust performance data are collated and analysed by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) against the agreed SDP targets and reported to PTEB on a quarterly basis with monthly highlight reports provided to the Senior Leadership Team of DoH for the intervening months. The SDP process requires SPPG to identify and address any areas where performance is more than 5% below target. As part of this approach SPPG meet regularly with the CDS Clinical Directors of all Trusts to monitor progress and identify potential remedial actions to improve future performance/ access.The CDS in the SEHSCT performed strongly in year one of the SDP with increased access to dental treatment under general anaesthetic and increased numbers of new and review CDS contacts in general. However, its ability to meet the projected targets in 2023-24 has been temporarily reduced by the roll out of the Encompass programme as staff time and resources have had to be diverted to this priority work area. Encompass should in the long term allow the HSC to operate as one integrated system by delivering a new digital care record for every citizen, acting as the basis for our digital transformation and changing the way that care is delivered across NI.Ongoing industrial action has also reduced access to CDS care. Furthermore, demographic changes mean that the cohort of patients cared for by the CDS (patients with complex needs often as a result of disabilities) has increased leading to a demand/ capacity mismatch which adversely affects access.SPPG has established a working group looking at solutions to the backlog of CDS patients that need to be treated under general anaesthetic which built up during pandemic. SPPG is also working with CDS teams in all Trusts to ensure that theatre utilisation is optimised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will undertake a review of traffic management at Stranmillis roundabout, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that the Stranmillis Roundabout, like many junctions throughout the Greater Belfast area, caters for a high number of vehicles, particularly at peak traffic times.  Previous observations carried out during such periods have indicated that, whilst the junction is busy, traffic passed through the junction in a slow and controlled manner.  Outside of these times traffic flows in the area are generally lower and there are no specific issues for drivers using the roundabout.An overall assessment of the Stranmillis Roundabout suggests that the present junction layout meets the traffic management and pedestrian requirements at this location and copes well with the conflicting demands of traffic in the area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a Programme for Government will be presented to the Assembly.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: I am considering the next steps to be taken in the development of an Executive Sexual Orientation Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: We are committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls as soon as possible. This is a cross Executive Strategy which has been co-designed with a wide range of partners. Our officials are liaising with Departmental colleagues and members of the Co-design group regarding the outcome of the public consultation, with a view to submitting the draft Strategic Framework to the Executive in coming weeks. Preparatory work to enable effective delivery is already underway, and a draft two-year Implementation Plan is being developed for consideration by Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action he has planned to address the problem of graffiti on Finaghy railway bridge.
answer_text: Having visited Finaghy Road North Bridge in person and engaged with local MLAs, I fully appreciate the significant concerns for local community that this issue presents.  Graffiti can have a very negative impact on communities and so my Department prioritises its removal from road infrastructure where it presents a risk to road safety or where it is offensive, racist or sectarian in nature. Following a request last year, my Department repainted part of the barrier on Finaghy Road North Bridge to remove some offensive graffiti. I am also aware that previous studies into potential solutions have been completed and while the options identified may not fully address the issue of graffiti at this location, my Department remains committed to working with the local community and elected representatives to determine how these may be delivered in the context of current challenging financial environment.In the meantime, my Department will continue to take appropriate action to remove further graffiti on the Finaghy Road North Bridge as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment he has made of the available funding for the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route.
answer_text: The estimated cost of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route is currently in the region of £145m and will be subject to review once the final outline business case is complete.  £35m of funding has been secured through Belfast Region City Deal, however the £20m of New Deal funding that had been identified for the project has been paused, As such a considerable shortfall of £110m remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the proposed pilot scheme for low carbon heat technologies, such as heat pumps; and (ii) whether he is considering introducing a heat pump grant, similar to those available in England and Wales.
answer_text: In relation to the proposed pilot scheme as referenced in the Energy Strategy, ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’, the department are supporting delivery partners to further promote low carbon heating technologies through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme. This support will help consumers transition away from fossil fuels to renewable heating technologies and stimulate growth within the sector.	My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department will provide the NI Fiscal Council the power to compel department to respond to concerns arising from their findings.
answer_text: The Fiscal Council was established on a non-statutory basis by the previous Finance Minister Conor Murphy MLA to bring greater transparency and independent scrutiny to the current and future state of our public finances. My Department is working on a new Fiscal Council Bill to put the Council on a statutory footing and I will seek to progress this as soon as possible. It is my intention that this Bill will be developed in line with the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development principles for Fiscal Organisations, providing a sound legislative framework to underpin its work and safeguard its independence. I look forward to the Finance Committee’s positive contribution to that as the Bill is taken through the required legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will reverse funding cuts to the Arts Council of Northern Ireland, announced by the Permanent Secretary in 2023.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the arts to people and to communities. The 2024-25 funding for the Arts Council of Northern Ireland will form part of the budget decisions to be made by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on whether there are plans to reopen the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement will she and her Department have with the Department of Justice, Ireland, on how to address hate crime on an all-Ireland basis.
answer_text: In my introductory call with Justice Minister McEntee last week we discussed the issue of hate crime and agreed the value of creating opportunities to work jointly together to tackle the broader issue of hate crime in the two jurisdictions. In Northern Ireland, recommendations from Judge Marrinan’s Review of Hate Crime Legislation Report provide the main framework for the development of policy and legislative proposals on hate crime.  As part of this work, my officials engage as necessary with other jurisdictions on hate crime issues, including with Department of Justice Ireland officials. This engagement provides the opportunity to share information, understand and monitor progress on respective hate crime policy development and legislative approaches to ensure a robust response to hate crime in the two jurisdictions. At an operational level, whilst an independent matter for the Chief Constable, I am aware that the PSNI and An Garda Síochána work together to develop operational awareness and share learning and information on cross-border Hate Crime practice and interventions.
