This forms part of the Inter-Governmental Agreement Positive Action Group work plan. They have established a joint senior team to oversee and drive co-ordinated activity on combatting operational challenges north and south, including hate crime. This will also be a consideration in the current review of the Cross Border Strategy between PSNI and An Garda Siochána.I welcome the opportunity for officials and criminal justice partners to continue to engage across the jurisdictions to share knowledge and expertise on hate crime issues as our respective Bills develop.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department's policy for keeping records of road closure applications submitted by private event organisers.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department will review the eligibility criteria for free school meals, in response to the rising cost of living.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.  I will be considering the next steps for this review in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department plans to publish the recommendations of the review into road closure legislation.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline plans to support social work students, particularly with fuel costs while they are on placement.
answer_text: My Department provides a Social Work Student Incentive Scheme for all NI Degree in Social Work students who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study.  In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. The DOH Student Incentive Scheme is in addition to any student finance support, which can be applied for separately.  I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking clinical courses due to the cost of living crisis. Unfortunately, due to budgetary constraints, my Department is unable to provide more assistance to students than it does currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) how the number of self-referrals to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, allowed for each council area, is calculated; and (ii) what steps are being taken to advertise and signpost claimants to the self-referral option.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive administers the Affordable Warmth Scheme on behalf of the Department for Communities.  The Scheme aims to address the effects of fuel poverty and energy inefficiency within privately owned properties. On 1 September 2023 the Affordable Warmth Scheme moved from a Council referral model to an application based (self-referral) scheme through the Housing Executive.  All enquiries for the Affordable Warmth Scheme are initially assessed by telephone through the Housing Executive’s NI Energy Advice Service.  To make an application to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, customers can contact the NI Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk The Housing Executive’s freephone NI Energy Advice Service actively promotes energy efficiency advice and various grants and services across Northern Ireland and across all tenures including social housing and low-income households.  The service also provides information on renewable energy advice, energy saving tips, how to switch energy provider, debt assistance, fuel poverty advice signposting and how to benefit from oil-buying savings.  The advice service is promoted in partnership with a variety of stakeholders, including community groups, political representatives, schools and local Councils, as well as various media channels and through events such as Love Your Home and Balmoral Show Exhibitions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions his Departmentis taking to ensure safe and sustainable staffing for maternity services, in particular bereavement care, through a workforce plan and sufficient long-term funding.
answer_text: Effective workforce planning is a key theme in the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People (May 2018) and its Second Action Plan (2022-23 to 2024-25) (June 2022).The Second Action Plan contains a range of actions that address attraction, recruitment and training of staff, as well as the identification of our required workforce skills and supply over a five to ten year horizon through a rolling programme of strategic workforce reviews.  These reviews, which are led by my Department in partnership with stakeholders, will increasingly focus on planning by programme of care.  Aligned to the health and social care Transformation Programme, they will aim to address the multidisciplinary and interprofessional aspects of service delivery and training.The outputs of these service level workforce reviews provide a regional view of future workforce need and skills mix within a health and social care wide optimum workforce model – our strategic workforce plan.Regarding maternity services, my Department is planning their transformation to a Continuity of Midwifery Carer (CoMC) model.  Implementing this model in our maternity services will be crucial to ensuring that women, babies and families have the best and safest midwifery care possible.  Following implementation, we intend that continuity of midwifery carer will be available for all women, including those women who are pregnant following a previous pregnancy loss or the death of a baby.  Significant investment in staff and resources will be required, including the appointment of a Regional CoMC Midwife Consultant and a CoMC Implementation Lead Midwife in each Health and Social Care Trust to allow for coordination.In the meantime, bereavement midwives will continue helping women and their partners/families through the bereavement journey, including emotional support after discharge.  In addition to these specialist roles, many midwifery staff across HSC Trusts have received additional training sessions on bereavement care.  The aim is to ensure that midwives are available to offer support to women who have had a bereavement.My Department’s Chief Nursing Officer has also commissioned Phase 11 of the Delivering Care Policy Framework, to determine a safe and effective staffing model for the midwifery workforce in Northern Ireland.  This work is progressing within the Public Health Agency, and we are endeavouring to propose a workforce model and requirements for midwifery by the end of 2022. The Executive agreed on 14 January 2020 to provide additional funding of £5-10m in 2020/21, increasing thereafter to £60m over 5 years, to deal with safe staffing concerns, including the full implementation of Delivering Care; although the Department recognises that there may be a need for additional funding within that timeframe.This work complements my undertaking in the 2020 Framework Agreement to develop and introduce robust Safe Staffing legislation with the agreement o

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with representatives of the arts sector to discuss the economic contribution of arts and the impact that the economic crisis will have on the sector.
answer_text: All external meetings I have attended up to the end of June 2022 are published publicly on www.opendatani.gov.uk. From June 2022 to October 2022, I have met with two representatives of the arts sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what efforts have been made to adopt more alleyways and entries, particularly in Belfast, to reduce the cleanliness and public health risks presented by unadopted entries.
answer_text: It is my Department’s policy only to adopt alleyways and pathways that are considered essential for safe vehicular and pedestrian access and where the area is not already served by a good network of public footways.  I fully support local community efforts to improve the appearance of back entries and other similar spaces within their areas, to enhance community pride, reduce anti-social behaviour and improve the general area. Consequently, the Department launched a new alleyway transformation initiative and wrote to all Councils inviting them to scope the opportunities that exist for interventions of this nature, and to apply to my Department for funding. In January 2021, funding was made available to Councils for the ‘Greening of Alleyways’.  This funding could be used either directly by the Council, or be provided to communities to carry out work to improve back entries, alleyways and similar open spaces, irrespective of ownership or adoption by my Department. This funding is available irrespective of whether the site has been adopted by my Department. Officials have engaged with Belfast City Council to develop proposals however no funding has been allocated to date.  Officials have also had discussions regarding their alleyways programme and the possibility of the Department lending support (by way of funding on a without prejudice basis) to this programme.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has received a response from the UK Chancellor to her request to uplift the rate of the Tax-Free Childcare scheme from 20 to 30 per cent.
answer_text: There has been no response from the UK Chancellor to the previous Education Minister’s request that the Government contribution to Tax-Free Childcare be increased from 20% to 30%.  This is an issue I intend to explore further with the UK Government in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many candidates applied to be appointed as the independent reviewer of the licensing system.
answer_text: This question has been answered in AQW 3298/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what progress his Department has made on the delivery of the Carryduff Greenway.
answer_text: As the member will be aware, the development of greenways is the responsibility of local Councils rather than my Department.  However I am keen to support the development of greenways and have invited councils to submit applications for grant in support of greenways and active travel generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to outline the Department’s reasons for appointing the University of Stirling as independent reviewer of licensing, including the surrender principle; and (ii) what assessment was made of the University's ability to act independently.
answer_text: The procurement process to appoint the University of Stirling to conduct the review of the licensing system was managed by officials from the Department of Finance’s Construction and Procurement Delivery on behalf of the Department, in line with Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended) and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy. The University of Stirling met the award criteria.The University of Stirling will manage a multidisciplinary team which includes international experts with more than 20 years’ experience in research on alcohol licensing, evaluation of alcohol policy and the impacts of policy on diverse health, social and economic outcomes. The experts have been recruited from the University of Stirling, the University of Edinburgh and the University of Sheffield.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has plans to promote the availability of Erasmus places for NI students via ROI institutions.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department have in place to retain taxi drivers.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North. This role requires balancing the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   My Department remains committed to working within this remit to play its part, alongside those in the taxi industry, to address the range of issues the industry faces, including undertaking a consultation on taxi fares which closed on 7 October 2022. You may wish to note that the Department has no direct role in the recruitment or retention policies that taxi operators have in place. The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation and there are many factors that contribute to the current shortage of taxi drivers.  As we recover from the Covid pandemic potential drivers will compare the industry to other driving jobs, including those that do not require them to be responsible for managing running costs, or that involve unsociable hours. All of these factors influence the attractiveness of the industry, both to existing licenced taxi drivers that are choosing not to drive for the industry at present, as well as potential new recruits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department holds or gathers data on the number of Northern Ireland students accessing the Erasmus programme via ROI institutions.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with trade unions, in the last three months, to discuss the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has met with mortgage providers to ascertain the impact of increased interest rates in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 4078/17-22 on the same matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department is considering to support taxi drivers in light of increased fuel costs and the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North. This role requires balancing the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   My Department remains committed to working within this remit to play its part, alongside those in the taxi industry, to address the range of issues the industry faces, including the high cost of fuel.You are aware of the recent public consultation on increasing the maximum fare structure. The consultation was open for eight weeks and closed on the 7 October 2022.  My officials are now preparing a synopsis of the consultation responses and a paper on the way forward for my consideration. It is only after this process has concluded that my decision on the outcome of the review can be implemented along with any necessary amendments to the Taxis (Taximeters, Devices and Maximum Fares) Regulations (NI) 2015. This review is to help balance the needs of drivers, operators and taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with business representative groups, in the last three months, to discuss cost pressures facing business.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with energy suppliers, in the last three months, to discuss the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her officials have made an assessment of the impact on the housing market as a result of increased mortgage interest rates, including on Housing Executive demand, the private rented sector and homelessness levels.
answer_text: i) Overall demand for social housing can be assessed through applications to the social housing waiting list. The most up to date details on waiting list figures can be accessed at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics ii) In relation to the private rented sector, we are seeing a growth in demand for rental properties from those households now less able to access the owner occupier sector. This may be a factor in an increase in rent. Increasing mortgage interest rates may also contribute to rents increasing, as landlords pass on the increased cost of mortgages to tenants.  Other factors, such as changes in how tax applies to mortgage costs may also be a factor.iii) The Department’s Housing Bulletin provides statistics on households presenting as homeless due to mortgage default. The number of households presenting as homeless due to mortgage default, for the Quarter ending December 2023, was 17. The most up to date statistics can be accessed at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics My Department also actively monitors Northern Ireland specific data regarding mortgage arrears, Court actions for possession/ repossession and works closely with Housing Rights to identify trends. An increase in activity has been noted, but this needs to be considered in the context of a gradual return to more usual levels of business, following low levels during the pandemic.Information on possessions (regarding applications to the court and order made by the court) can be accessed at: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/nicts-mortgages-action-possessionRepossessions refer to the court order being enforced by the Enforcement of Judgments Office (NICtS) - this data can be accessed at:  https://data.nisra.gov.uk/table/POSSCOMPLGDAs part of the continual review of social security policy, my Department is in the process of reviewing Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI). The review will consider if the scheme provides adequate support and flexibility for those who require it.   The review will also identify if changes to the scheme may contribute to a more effective delivery of financial support to those who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3355/22-27, for a breakdown of the £15.4 million spend.
answer_text: The total cost to design and construct Bellarena Passing Loop was £15.4m and is broken down as follows:Translink CostsProfessional FeesCivils Design & BuildSignalling & Telecoms Design & BuildLands CostsTranslink / Client supplied MaterialsTranslink Bus SubstitutionTotal Cost£148,458£608,940£2,938,249£10,440,251£324,216£741,284£200,837£15,402,239

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has met with mortgage providers to ascertain the impact of increased interest rates in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recently wrote to the local banks and UK Finance, the representative body for the banking and finance industry including building societies, to ask for their assessment of how the higher interest rates expected following the British Government’s recent fiscal event are affecting the mortgage market and borrowers here in the North. I also stressed how important it is that they do all that they can to support homeowners through this challenging time by providing a range of forbearance options to help all borrowers, households and businesses, facing financial difficulty. I also urged them to ensure that support and advice is being provided to borrowers and made it clear that all steps possible should be taken to avoid possession of properties and business failures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking to expand the take up for Support for Mortgage Interest payments, in light of the increase in interest rates.
answer_text: My Department aims to ensure that all individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. My Department’s Make the Call Service offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for entitlement to social security benefits, additional supports and/or services.  The Department’s Make the Call advertising campaign is running across TV, radio, digital media and newspapers recognising the impacts on cost of living and highlighting the Make the Call service; the campaign began on 21 September and will run until 9 October.Departmental staff are not qualified to provide financial advice to customers, however, if further advice is requested, staff will signpost the customer to a range of support organisations including the Money Advice Service and Advice NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her officials have made an estimate of the impact on the housing market as a result of the increase in mortgage interest rates, including on (i) Housing Executive demand; (ii) the private rented sector; and (iii) homelessness levels.
answer_text: i) Overall demand for social housing can be assessed through applications to the social housing waiting list. The most up to date details on waiting list figures can be accessed at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics ii) In relation to the private rented sector, we are seeing a growth in demand for rental properties from those households now less able to access the owner occupier sector. This may be a factor in an increase in rent. Increasing mortgage interest rates may also contribute to rents increasing, as landlords pass on the increased cost of mortgages to tenants.  Other factors, such as changes in how tax applies to mortgage costs may also be a factor.iii) The Department’s Housing Bulletin provides statistics on households presenting as homeless due to mortgage default. The number of households presenting as homeless due to mortgage default, for the Quarter ending December 2023, was 17. The most up to date statistics can be accessed at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics My Department also actively monitors Northern Ireland specific data regarding mortgage arrears, Court actions for possession/ repossession and works closely with Housing Rights to identify trends. An increase in activity has been noted, but this needs to be considered in the context of a gradual return to more usual levels of business, following low levels during the pandemic.Information on possessions (regarding applications to the court and order made by the court) can be accessed at: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/nicts-mortgages-action-possessionRepossessions refer to the court order being enforced by the Enforcement of Judgments Office (NICtS) - this data can be accessed at:  https://data.nisra.gov.uk/table/POSSCOMPLGDAs part of the continual review of social security policy, my Department is in the process of reviewing Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI). The review will consider if the scheme provides adequate support and flexibility for those who require it.   The review will also identify if changes to the scheme may contribute to a more effective delivery of financial support to those who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what the average waiting time is for children to receive an autism diagnosis; and (ii) what his Department is doing to improve autism spectrum diagnosis waiting times.
answer_text: (i) As all Trusts do not record date of diagnosis on systems as a point in time it is not possible to give an average time to diagnosis. Trusts provide intervention post diagnosis and children and young people are not discharged at point of diagnosis. (ii) I would direct you to responses provided in AQW 1376/22 -27 and AQW 1222/22 -27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the (i) number of school-age asylum seeker children currently waiting for a school place; (ii) age profile of these children; and (iii) number of children waiting who have not yet been assigned to a key worker.
answer_text: This information is not held by my Department.  While assistance is provided by the Education Authority (EA) to asylum seekers, asylum seeking children are not assigned key workers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the appropriateness of hotel contingency accommodation that is used to house asylum seekers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters here.  The Executive Office holds strategic responsibility both for refugees and asylum seekers, and for coordinating the regional response with the Home Office.  I have written to the Executive Office to reiterate the importance of resolving these issues with the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the total cost of Bellarena Train Station.
answer_text: The new halt and passing loop at Bellarena was opened in November 2016 at a cost of £15.4m. This included costs for the purchase of the required land, new track, signalling and telecoms systems. In addition a new level crossing was constructed along with two new platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the provision of social services for asylum seekers in NI.
answer_text: Children under the age of eighteen years who are asylum seekers in Northern Ireland and children of asylum seeking parents who are ‘children in need’ are entitled to the full provision of a range of personal social services under the provisions of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995. The Order does not make any distinction between indigenous and non-indigenous children.Unaccompanied Asylum seeking children are also entitled to the support of an Independent Guardian, a separate support from that provided by HSCT statutory Children’s Services teams.For adults, a person who is not ordinarily resident is a visitor and access to healthcare in Northern Ireland is provided under the Health and Personal Social Services Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (“the Regulations”).  The Regulations provide that some categories of visitor are exempt from charge.  The Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland 2015 (PNOR Regulations) entitles persons to Health care but NOT to social care services.HSC Trusts are required to provide assistance that is necessary to prevent a breach of human rights or EU Treaty rights. HSC Trusts must conduct a human rights needs assessment in order to ascertain whether there is a human rights breach. If the Trust identifies a human rights breach, it must provide the necessary services to meet that need(s). The assistance, if assessed as necessary is provided under Article 15 of the Health & Personal Social Services 1972 Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on design for the recladding of the Finaghy bridge; and (ii) what actions his Department intends to take to help to improve its appearance.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my recent response to AQW 2871/22-27 on this subject.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has considered reviewing the eligibility criteria for free school meals in response to the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department commenced a review of free school meals and uniform grants in late 2021. The review is considering a number of options for who may be able to access free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for some/all year groups and changes to the current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit. It is also actively considering the option for guidance on the costs of school uniforms to be on a statutory basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the childcare strategy.
answer_text: I made a Written Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 providing an update on the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and planned actions for the next few months.   You can view the statement at the following link https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-update-executive-childcare-strategy.Affordability of childcare for families is a growing concern and is a key factor being considered in the development of the Strategy.  The current Review of Childcare Services will assist in determining the extent of this problem and inform potential actions. In the interim, I am keen to ensure that families are aware of the existing financial support available with the cost of childcare.  My officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Health and HMRC on a promotional campaign to ensure that parents are aware and accessing all available financial support that they may be entitled to.  I have also written to the UK Chancellor asking him to consider an uplift from 20% to 30% to the rate of the UK Government’s contribution, as part of the Tax-Free Childcare Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what steps her Department is taking to assist parents with rising childcare costs.
answer_text: I made a Written Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 providing an update on the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and planned actions for the next few months.   You can view the statement at the following link https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-update-executive-childcare-strategy.Affordability of childcare for families is a growing concern and is a key factor being considered in the development of the Strategy.  The current Review of Childcare Services will assist in determining the extent of this problem and inform potential actions. In the interim, I am keen to ensure that families are aware of the existing financial support available with the cost of childcare.  My officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Health and HMRC on a promotional campaign to ensure that parents are aware and accessing all available financial support that they may be entitled to.  I have also written to the UK Chancellor asking him to consider an uplift from 20% to 30% to the rate of the UK Government’s contribution, as part of the Tax-Free Childcare Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when structural works will begin on the proposed Traffic Calming Scheme at Orpen Park, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that, in response to objections raised during the consultation process, my officials are reviewing the most recent design for traffic calming measures in Orpen Park.Officials advise that a further consultation will be required before a scheme is progressed. Subject to satisfactory completion of the legislative process, which will reflect the out workings of the consultation exercise, it would be anticipated that the earliest construction date would be spring 2023.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many legacy benefit claimants have experienced managed migration to Universal Credit; and how many will undergo managed migration by 31 March 2023.
answer_text: The final stage of Universal Credit (UC) implementation, known as Move to UC, involves the managed migration of those who remain in receipt of the legacy benefits being replaced by Universal Credit. A start date for Move to UC has not yet been decided therefore no one has undergone managed migration here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on her Department's plan to enforce managed migration for legacy benefit claimants to Universal Credit.
answer_text: The final stage of Universal Credit (UC) implementation, known as Move to UC, involves the managed migration of those who remain in receipt of the legacy benefits being replaced by Universal Credit. A start date for Move to UC here has not yet been decided and planning is underway.  People claiming one of the benefits/credits being replaced by Universal Credit do not need to do anything unless their circumstances change as they will be contacted when it is time for them to move to Universal Credit.Anyone thinking about making a claim to Universal Credit can seek independent advice from the Advice NI Helpline on 0800 915 4604 and use benefit calculators on nidirect to check their entitlement.My Department’s Make the Call service can also support people with a free and confidential needs assessment, by calling 0800 232 1271; texting ADVICE to 079 8440 5248; visiting nidirect.gov.uk/makethecall (external link opens in a new window / tab) or emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how future decisions around a new model of hyperacute stroke care will take account of, and impact, other actions detailed in the Stroke Action Plan, such as the targeted workforce review and plan.
answer_text: My Department recognises the crucial role of the stroke workforce in delivering improved outcomes for stroke survivors. The workforce review will consider the respective roles of each profession within the stroke workforce, the number of each required and the training and development needed to deliver an effective stroke service.The workforce review will encompass both the hospital-based and community-based stroke workforce and, consequently, will be informed by analysis to be undertaken on Hyperacute Stroke Care which will identify the workforce requirements for that model of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the targeted stroke workforce review, that is to be undertaken as part of the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, will include consideration of the skills and training needs of any future stroke workforce.
answer_text: My Department recognises the crucial role of the stroke workforce in delivering improved outcomes for stroke survivors. The workforce review will consider the respective roles of each profession within the stroke workforce, the number of each required and the training and development needed to deliver an effective stroke service.The workforce review will encompass both the hospital-based and community-based stroke workforce and, consequently, will be informed by analysis to be undertaken on Hyperacute Stroke Care which will identify the workforce requirements for that model of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the status of the Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 5 public realm scheme; and (ii) whether further pedestrianisation of any streets involved in the scheme is being considered.
answer_text: My Department is progressing the Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 5 project, which is currently at RIBA Stage 2 - Development of Concept Design.  These design proposals are at an early stage and will be subject to consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will ensure that all new buses acquired by Translink will be low floor vehicles to allow easier accessibility for wheelchair users.
answer_text: The accessibility and inclusivity of our public transport network is vitally important for me and I am fully committed to providing inclusive and accessible Public Transport and facilities for all customers. I am pleased to advise that Translink has agreed specifications for bus designs going forward into future procurement with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC). All vehicles used on local stage carriage services will be low floor. Translink is currently evaluating a low floor single deck Goldliner and would hope to develop an agreed specification on this vehicle type with its stakeholders including IMTAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to (i) the development of a standard platform height for all stations on the NI Railways network; (ii) the adoption of a procurement standard for all new trains to have a low-floor with extendable gap fillers to match the platform height; and (iii) funding a rolling programme of platform improvements.
answer_text: The design and construction of railway platforms is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question in relation to platform heights to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.ukAs per (i), the procurement standards of new trains is an operational matter for Translink and you should direct your question to the organisation. Translink is currently carrying out a rail halt accessibility review of each of its rail halts and stations against current legislations and standards including the Department for Transport’s “Design standards for Accessible Railway Stations”. This review will provide a list of proposals and costs for implementation. Once complete, any recommendations from the review will be subject to an approved business case and will require funding secured to progress. My Departmental officials will engage with Translink when this business case is produced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the provision of sexual health services; and (ii) whether there are plans to ensure a full return to pre-pandemic levels of these services.
answer_text: (i) Despite staffing pressures each of the 5 NI HSC Trusts are endeavouring to provide the full range of sexual health services in addition to having to cope with the Monkeypox outbreak.  The introduction of virtual clinics and the availability of STI online testing has greatly enhanced the level of activity that can be delivered across all of Northern Ireland (ii) The Department of Health and HSC Trusts are working closely together to ensure a full return to pre-pandemic levels of these services.  This position has been greatly enhanced by the confirmation of an additional recurring investment of £1.9m which will maintain a PrEP clinic in each Trust area as well to maintain the provision of an online STI service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has given consideration to restricting development along former rail alignments; and (ii) whether any routes are being considered for development as public transport or active travel corridors.
answer_text: With the Climate Act now in place the importance of an accessible rail network comes into new focus and I believe that will include the need for new rail links across the island with new rail halts to ensure as many people as possible can easily access a rail network that meets their everyday work and leisure needs and supports the local economy.All councils are in the process of preparing new Local Development Plans (LDPs) for their areas and in doing so they must take account of the provisions of the he Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS). The SPPS states that, “LDPs should identify and safeguard disused transport routes such as former railway lines and canals where there is a reasonable prospect of re-use for future transport purposes.  Where this is not the case, consideration should be given as to whether protection should be afforded through the Plan for alternative purposes such as a recreational, nature conservation or tourism related use.” The Department continues to work with councils to ensure that, where appropriate, these important assets are protected. As you may be aware, work is ongoing to take a strategic look at our rail network to determine how best it can deliver for our citizens in the future. One element of this work is the All-Island Strategic Rail Review. The Review is being undertaken to allow us to consider our rail network across this island with a view to how it can be improved for everyone. It will examine the potential for rail connections across the island including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed.My Department is also currently developing the new Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTNTP) which will set out priorities for investment in our road, rail and inter-urban limited stop bus network, including park & ride, through to 2035. This will, in association with Translink, include high level assessments on a variety of potential rail improvements. The RSTNTP will be subject to public consultation and I will be pleased to consider representations on the plan including those in relation to the rail network and disused corridors.At this stage, I do not want to pre-empt the outcome of these pieces of work including where new rail lines may be recommended. However, it is worth noting that my Department is also in the process of preparing a new suite of Local Transport Plans alongside the councils’ LDPs that will set out the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. This may include the utilisation of disused transport routes for new transport schemes, including for public transport and active travel.   The development of Local Transport Plans and LDPs facilitate stakeholder engagement to ensure that a wide range of views are taken into account as they are developed. As the Local Transport Plans are being produced alongside the councils LDPs their timescales will reflect that of the council’s plans. In ad

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice has her Department engaged on the closure of the Law Societies' joint Brussels office and the potential impact on the understanding of NI's unique position in relation to the European Union.
answer_text: The closure of the Law Societies’ joint Brussels office is a matter for the three Law Societies. My Department has not had any engagement on the matter. My Department is committed to continuing its engagement with the EU institutions on justice issues to share views and to highlight the mutual benefit and continued importance of cooperation in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14522/17-22, (i) whether specialists have been appointed to undertake a research project on the operation of the Gender Recognition Act 2004; and (ii) for an update on the review of this legislation.
answer_text: In September 2021, a team of researchers based in the School of Psychology, Queen’s University, Belfast, was appointed to consider the operation of the Gender Recognition Act 2004 in this jurisdiction. The project is scheduled to report in the summer. Its findings will inform policy decisions during the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update what steps his Department is taking to progress the removal of caging from Northern Ireland farming.
answer_text: A key objective of my Department is to ensure that the highest standards of animal welfare are adhered to and my officials continue to consider all developments in this area which could assist in advancing this. I am aware that other jurisdictions, including England, will seek to advance consultations, in due course, on proposals to remove cages and crates from farming and my Officials will continue to keep this matter under review should a consultation and subsequent legislation be progressed.I am very conscious, however, that in considering any future changes in this area there is the need to ensure that all relevant factors are examined including how any moves to ban or limit cage use would impact on our local industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much income did Health and Social Care Trust residential and nursing care homes receive in (i) 2020; and (ii) 2021, through third party top up fees.
answer_text: I refer to recent answers to AQW 1915/22-27, AQW 1978/22-27, AQW 2060/22-27 and AQW 2172/22-27. HSC Trusts must contract for placements at the most competitive rate available, which they consider suitable for meeting the individual’s assessed need even where this is not obtainable at the regional rate. Where a Trust has contracted at that rate there is no top-up.Should a service user select accommodation which is more expensive than other appropriate accommodation which the commissioning Trust can secure at a lower rate (for example, because they wish to have a better room in a particular home or a more expensive care home), that Trust will facilitate that choice, but the additional cost must be met by a third party such as a family member of friend.  This is known as a top-up.These additional charges (top-ups) relate to the cost of accommodation provided by independent care providers. Therefore no income is derived by Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to these arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans her Department has to promote community cohesion and consensus in relation to the flying of flags.
answer_text: This matter is within the remit of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what extra services a resident of a Health and Social Care Trust care or residential home will receive where a relative agrees to pay a third party top up fee.
answer_text: The public consultation on the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care is ongoing and due to close on 1 July 2022.  Once the consultation closes, my officials will take time to analyse and consider carefully the responses. A formal response to the consultation will be published in due course, including on the issue of GP Out of Hours provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health under what circumstance a relative of a Health and Social Care Trust care or residential home resident may be asked to pay a third party top up fee.
answer_text: Regional Guidance set out in Circular HSC (ECCU) 1/2010 - Care Management, Provision of Services and Charging Guidance,https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dhssps/hsc-eccu-1-2010.pdf states HSC Trusts must contract for placements at the most competitive rate available, which they consider suitable for meeting the individual’s assessed need even where this is not obtainable at the regional rate. Where a Trust has contracted at that rate there is no top-up.Should a service user select accommodation which is more expensive than other appropriate accommodation which the commissioning Trust can secure at a lower rate (for example, because they wish to have a better room in a particular home or a more expensive care home), that Trust will facilitate that choice, but the additional cost must be met by a third party such as a family member of friend.  This is known as a top-up. Whilst the aim should always be to facilitate choice for service users’, Trusts have a parallel responsibility to secure the best value for money and protect the public funds for which they are accountable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when a fuel poverty strategy will be published.
answer_text: My Department continues to have engagement with the Utility Regulator, Consumer Council and other stakeholders in relation to increased energy costs and wider issues relating to fuel poverty. These include exploring further options and actions to mitigate the problems being faced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has completed action 2 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan and established a cross-departmental working group on fuel poverty.
answer_text: My Department continues to have engagement with the Utility Regulator, Consumer Council and other stakeholders in relation to increased energy costs and wider issues relating to fuel poverty. These include exploring further options and actions to mitigate the problems being faced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what legislation or policy allows his Department to regulate third party top up fees in nursing and residential care homes.
answer_text: The policy on charging top-up fees, or third party charges is detailed in Departmental guidance ECCU 1/2010 ‘Care Management, Provision of Services and Charging Guidance’ (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dhssps/hsc-eccu-1-2010.pdf).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many nursing and residential homes charge a third party top-up fee.
To ask the Minister for Communities what actions have been taken to progress the strategic plan for Shaftesbury Square, which was first published in 2017.
answer_text: My Department formed the Shaftesbury Square Stakeholders Group with the aim of pursuing the objectives set out in the Shaftesbury Square Development Framework. Good progress continues to be made in understanding and co-ordinating the ‘live’ strategic projects in the area including: My Department’s Belfast Streets Ahead 5 scheme;Department for Infrastructure’s Belfast Rapid Transit II;Translink’s new Weaver’s Cross travel hub;The Executive Office’s Urban Villages initiative;Belfast City Council’s A Bolder Vision for Belfast, produced jointly by my Department, Belfast City Council and the Department for Infrastructure;The Linen Quarter Business Improvement District; and Forward South Partnership (FSP) Strategic Vision and plans for Botanic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to ensure GP services return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Primary Care services were already coming under increasing pressure.  Figures in 2019 indicated patient contacts were almost 15 million per year up from an estimated 12.7 million in 2014. In recognition of the increasing demand for primary care services, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.It is important to note however that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Rather this is part of a wider programme of work to help improve patient access to services in primary care.  Other elements include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams as well as the introduction of Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as deemed clinically appropriate by GPs. The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of us who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners.  I am aware that some patients are experiencing difficulties in relation to access to GP services and understand the frustration this can cause.  My Department has established a Working Group to explore issues around access to GP services, including demand for services, limitations of infrastructure, and staffing and resourcing issues.  The Working Group will also consider how technology can be better used to provide improved access in a standardised way across Northern Ireland.  The Working Group will propose actions to be taken in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the role of producing responses to planning applications under the Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016 within the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, to detail (i) how many staff are currently employed; and (ii) how many staff were employed in each of the last five years.
answer_text: DAERA has a role in development management and responds to development proposals where there is potential for impacts on the natural and marine environments and fisheries interests. The Department provides a single, integrated response for each consultation across DAERA’s areas of responsibility; this includes Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and Environment Marine and Fisheries Group (EMFG).The table below provides detail on the permanent staff complement, in relation to the role of producing responses to planning applications that are currently employed within NIEA and EMFG and were employed in each of the last five years.   2017/20182018/20192019/20202020/20212021/20222022/2023NIEA/EMFG687277748080** Current positionIt should be noted that this has been supplemented on occasions with the use of temporary promotions, temporary workers or redeployed staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the role of producing responses to planning applications under The Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016, how many staff (i) are employed this year; and (ii) were employed in each of the last five years, in (a) the Historical Environment Division; and (b) the Housing Executive.
answer_text: My Department employs 20 staff in Historic Environment Division who are engaged in producing responses to planning consultations, as part of their wider development management roles. This figure has not changed materially in the last five years. There are currently 15 staff members employed in the Housing Executive with responsibility for producing responses to planning applications, alongside other duties.  Staff numbers have been consistent with current levels over each of the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions his Department is taking to ensure that people with chronic conditions have adequate access to GP services.
answer_text: It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as deemed clinically appropriate by GPs.  This includes for those patients living with long term (chronic) conditions.The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of people who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners. The Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) measures GP Practice achievement against evidence-based indicators, including a range of clinical indicators to support the management of patients across a number of long term conditions.  At the outset of the pandemic, it was agreed that to support General Practice’s response to COVID, the Quality and Outcomes Framework activity and reporting would be suspended.  While most elements of QOF activity and reporting were stood down during the pandemic, in line with the strategic priority of the re-establishment of normal practice in Primary Care the focus for 2022/23 is on re-commencing the QOF system across all GP practices.Unfortunately, even prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, primary care services were already coming under increasing pressure. It remains my assessment that GP-led services cannot continue to meet the rising demand and expectations without urgent action to address the pressures facing the service. To do this, means that we have to focus on both the GP workforce and on developing new ways of working that were already being implemented but which have accelerated during the pandemic. Work is underway  on a review of GP trainee places with a working group established to explore issues relating to the GP workforce to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst its work is still ongoing, I have accepted the working group’s recommendation of an immediate increase of 10 additional GP training places as an interim measure for the 2022/23 academic year. Any future increases will be informed by completion of the work of the working group and subject to the availability of funding.In seeking to meet increasing demand, primary care is also building on new and innovative ways of working.  This includes making greater use of technology as well as broadening skills mix though the development of new roles, such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners and General Practice Pharmacists and the roll out of our Primary Care

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the role of producing responses to planning applications under the Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016, to detail (i) how many staff are currently employed; and (ii) how many staff were employed, in each of the last five years, in (a) Northern Ireland Water; (b) DFI Roads; and (iii) DFI Rivers.
answer_text: The number of staff within my Department’s Roads and Rivers Directorates which are employed in producing responses to planning consultations is provided in the table below. However, there have been occasions within these five years when the teams responding to planning consultations have operated with staff absences and vacant posts.YearRoadsRivers2018409.520193910.520204013.720214012.220223912.6Staffing within NI Water is an operational matter and detail in relation to their staff numbers should be requested directly from them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Law Commission’s recommendation that there should be a new offence created to target intentional encouragement or assistance of self-harm, particularly in the context of the Department of Health led strategy Protect Life 2.
answer_text: In the Modernising Communications Offences report, published in July 2021 the Law Commission put forward a number of recommendations to address the harm arising from online abuse. One of the recommendations relates to the encouragement of serious self-harm. The UK Government is currently considering that recommendation and the details of such a new offence.The Department of Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) is leading on the Online Safety Bill and an amendment to the Bill to add an offence of encouraging or assisting self-harm by means of communication was debated on 14 June 2022. However, the Government indicated its intention to give full effect to the Law Commission recommendation in separate legislation and the amendment was withdrawn.Departmental officials intend to discuss and seek clarity on the current position with DCMS and also discuss with local professionals who treat self-harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many consultation requests under the Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016 were received (i) this year; (ii) and in the last five years, by (a) NI Water; (b) DFI Roads; and (c) DFI Rivers, broken down by how many of these met the target response time of 21 days for non-EIA development and 28 days for EIA development.
answer_text: My Department publishes performance reports for consultees in relation to the statutory consultation target of 21 days under the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015.  I would refer you page 10 – table 5 in the web-link attached, which provides the data requested for the past 4 years. Data for 2021/22 will be available in the next few months.My Department does not publish information specifically relating to EIA development and the data which we currently hold would not facilitate an analysis of this request.www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/stat-consultations-annual-report-202021.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the role of producing responses to planning applications under the Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016, how many staff (i) are employed this year; and (ii) were employed in each of the last five years, in the Health and Safety Executive.
answer_text: HSENI staff work within operational groups covering a range of work sectors and prepare responses to planning consultations as part of their role. The majority of planning consultations fall to the Major Hazards Group. Currently and over the past five years, approximately three Inspectors and one Administrative Officer are involved in preparing responses for planning consultations. However, it should be noted that this accounts for a small proportion (less than 10%) of their overall job roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how many consultation requests under the Planning (General Development Procedure) (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2016 were received (a) this year; and (b) in the last five years, by the Health and Safety Executive; and (ii) to outline how many of these met the target response time of (a) 21 days for non Environmental Impact Assessment development; or (b) 28 days for Environmental Impact Assessment development.
answer_text: HSENI received a total of 77 planning consultations since 1st January 2022 to 16th June 2022. 58 of these were statutory consultations, of which 55 were responded to within 21 days. Two of the planning consultations received related to Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and both of these were responded to within 28 days.Since 1st April 2017 until 16th June 2022, HSENI has received a total of 681 planning consultations. 451 of these were statutory consultations, of which 357 were responded to within 21 days. 17 of the planning consultations received related to EIA and 12 of these were responded to within 28 days.When it is not possible to provide a response to a planning consultation within the applicable period, HSENI endeavour to contact the relevant Planning Authority at the earliest opportunity to seek an extension and explain the reason for the request (e.g. further information may be required).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with gas and electricity utility companies regarding price rises, since the start of the year.
answer_text: I recently held a roundtable meeting, attended by a number of gas and electricity companies, to discuss a range of issues including energy prices, the impact on businesses including micro and SME businesses and cyber security matters. 			My officials have ongoing engagement with the Utility Regulator and energy suppliers via the Retail Market Forum and regulated tariff regime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list the meetings she has had with the arts sector since January 2022.
answer_text: Since January 2022 I have met with representatives from the arts sector on the following dates:02 February 202214 February 202216 February 202224 March 2022My officials continue to engage regularly with representatives of the arts sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings he has had with the Utility Regulator regarding energy price rises, since the start of the year.
answer_text: I have met with the Chief Executive of the Utility Regulator twice since the start of the year regarding energy prices. In addition, my officials are in regular contact with the Utility Regulator and his colleagues on a range of matters including energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many meetings he has had with his counterpart in the Irish Government since January 2020.
answer_text: I have had three bilateral meetings with my counterparts in the Irish Government since January 2020, one with the Minister of Finance and two with the Minister for Public Expenditure and Reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his officials have prepared draft legislation to put the Fiscal Council on a statutory footing.
answer_text: I had hoped to bring a draft policy document to the Executive for approval to instruct Office of Legislative Counsel (OLC) to formally start the drafting process for the primary legislation required, prior to the end of the last mandate.  However this was not possible due to the collapse of the Executive.Nevertheless, my officials are undertaking the preparatory work that is possible in the current circumstances to progress the development of the draft legislation for progression once an Executive is restored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what action he will take as a result of the final report of the Fiscal Commission.
answer_text: Although it will be for a restored Executive to take a formal position on the Commission’s recommendations, preparatory work can begin now. My officials are currently considering how the availability of robust local tax data can be improved which will be instrumental in informing future decisions on whether and how taxes might be devolved and managed here. Treasury of course have a key role to play in this, as have HMRC and Office of National Statistics (ONS) who collect and publish much of the information on tax receipts. I raised the need for positive engagement on this with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, who commented on the high quality of the report, when I met him at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee on 15 June 2022. Separately, Paul Johnson, Chair of the Fiscal Commission is currently developing a plan for further disseminating the report and I have written to him offering my Department’s full support in that regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the last two years, (i) how many legal cases have been brought against her Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; and (ii) how many of these cases have ended with the Department agreeing a financial settlement with the claimant; and (iii) how much has the Department paid to settle with the claimant.
answer_text: In the last two years there have been: no legal cases brought against the Department for Communities relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; andno cases which have ended with the Department for Communities agreeing a financial settlement with a claimant in relation to the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; andthe Department for Communities has not made any payments to settle with a claimant in relation to unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the scoping exercise being undertaken by departmental and Housing Executive officials on the delivery of a non-ACM scheme.
answer_text: My Department is scoping a non-ACM scheme with support from the Department of Finance.  		DfC are engaging with officials in the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities, details of all of the properties that were the subject of submissions to the DoF call for evidence have been shared by DoF with the Fire and Rescue Service, who have asked for additional information on buildings’ names and confirmed heights. They will consider buildings within scope and work to adapt response plans for the intention of mitigating associated risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health in the last two years, (i) how many legal cases have been brought against his Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; and (ii) how many of these cases have ended with the Department agreeing a financial settlement with the claimant; and (iii) how much has the Department paid to settle with the claimant.
answer_text: No legal cases have been brought against the Department in the last two years relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the decision making process used by the Western Health and Social Care Trust when selecting Musgrave House Hospital to perform orthopaedic surgeries on the Trust’s behalf.
answer_text: In 2020/21 and 2021/22, the then Health and Social Care Board requested that all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts which provide elective orthopaedic services contact Independent Sector healthcare providers to assess available capacity to provide elective orthopaedic surgery for HSC patients. Once identified, this capacity was commissioned and shared amongst Trusts, with prioritisation based on addressing urgent cases and the longest waiting patients, within available resources. One of these providers, Musgrave House Healthcare, submitted a proposal to Western health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) to lease uncommissioned ward and theatre capacity in the South West Acute Hospital, Enniskillen to deliver elective orthopaedics services. The WHSCT worked directly with this provider to progress this arrangement. This was done via a Direct Award Contact which was approved by the WHSCT Accounting Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice in the last two years, (i) how many legal cases have been brought against her Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; and (ii) how many of these cases have ended with the Department agreeing a financial settlement with the claimant; and (iii) how much has the Department paid to settle with the claimant.
answer_text: There have been no legal cases brought against my Department in the last two financial years in relation to allegations of unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education in the last two years, (i) how many legal cases have been brought against her Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; and (ii) how many of these cases have ended with the Department agreeing a financial settlement with the claimant; and (iii) how much has the Department paid to settle with the claimant.
answer_text: There have been no legal cases brought against my Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower in the past two financial years and therefore there are no financial settlements to report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy in the last two years, (i) how many legal cases have been brought against his Department relating to allegations of the unprofessional treatment of a whistleblower; and (ii) how many of these cases have ended with the Department agreeing a financial settlement with the claimant; and (iii) how much has the Department paid to settle with the claimant.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he plans to make an announcement on the preferred route for the Glider in North Belfast.
answer_text: Development of an outline business case, which will help inform selection of the preferred route, is well advanced. At this stage, it is too early to provide a timeline for the preferred route announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how much was spent on the greening of alleyways under the Blue & Green Infrastructure Fund in (i) 2021; and (ii) 2022; broken down by (a) local council area; and (b) constituency.
answer_text: My officials have engaged, and continue to engage, with Councils on proposals for the greening of alleyways however no funding has been allocated to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planned cycle lane on Limestone Road and Cavehill Road, Belfast.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives.Following initial objections to the construction of Phase I of the pop-up Cycle Lane on the Limestone Road, Belfast, an information event on 27th April 2022 was held, giving all stakeholders the change to view and discuss the plans. This event also allowed discussion on Phase II along the Cavehill Road.Officials are currently collating this information for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social housing units in 2021 and 2022 that have (i) been approved by her Department and not commenced construction; (ii) commenced, but not completed, construction; (iii) completed construction and are awaiting handover to a housing association; (iv) had Completion Certificates issued by the contractor to the housing association; and (v) been allocated to tenants by housing associations in (a) Belfast North; (b) Belfast South; (c) Belfast East; and (d) Belfast West.
answer_text: BELFAST NORTHWithin this constituency 31 social housing units have been approved since beginning of 2021, but construction has not yet commenced. There are currently 350 social housing units under construction. There have been 97 social housing completions, since the beginning of 2021 to date. In this context, “completion” means that work on the dwelling is complete and that these 97 units have been handed over to a housing association (following receipt of a Completion Certificate from a contractor). Information on allocations is managed by the housing associations.BELFAST SOUTHWithin this constituency there have been 36 social housing units approved since beginning of 2021, but construction has not yet commenced. There are currently 187 social housing units under construction. There have been 91 social housing completions, since the beginning of 2021 to date. In this context, “completion” has the same meaning as given above. Information on allocations is managed by the housing associations.BELFAST EASTWithin this constituency there have been 6 social housing units approved since beginning of 2021, but construction has not yet commenced. There are currently 261 social housing units under construction. There have been 118 social housing completions, since the beginning of 2021 to date. In this context, “completion” has the same meaning as given above. Information on allocations is managed by the housing associations.BELFAST WESTWithin this constituency there have been 281 social housing units approved since beginning of 2021, but construction has not yet commenced. There are currently 1,171 social housing units under construction. There have been 32 social housing completions, since the beginning of 2021 to date.  In this context, “completion” has the same meaning as given above. Information on allocations is managed by the housing associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps are being taken to introduce a licensing system which will allow home dog boarding services to operate.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that complying with existing licensing requirements can be difficult for dog home boarding businesses. My Department has not, therefore, required businesses undertaking this activity to cease trading but works closely with operators to encourage them to meet licencing conditions where they are able to do so.My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate. To this end, my officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate. This will include action on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2021 being the most recent available. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s plan for the pedestrianisation of Union Street, Belfast.
answer_text: Union Street is currently closed to vehicular access under an Experimental Traffic Control Scheme with the exception of access for permit holders, loading and unloading between certain hours, and cycles.  It is intended that this scheme will be extended for a further 6 months from the 11th June 2022.  At the end of this further 6 months a decision will be made as to whether the scheme will be made permanent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s plan for the pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast.
answer_text: My officials have recently developed a new proposal for the pedestrianisation of Hill Street and are currently engaging with stakeholders and local businesses on this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many jobs have been created in 2022 with support from Invest NI, broken down by parliamentary constituency.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2021 being the most recent available.  During this period, Invest NI provided support to businesses, which will lead to the creation of 4,217 new jobs.  Jobs arising from these projects will be created over the next 3 to 5 years, and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £128.82m.Table 1 below details the number of new jobs by Parliamentary Constituency area supported by Invest NI over the period 2020-21.Information on the 2021-22 financial year will be available in due course.
